Correlation coefficients between age and peripapillary RNFL thickness, ONH rim area, and anterior chamber parameters Age-related changes in ocular parameters are shown in Table 4 . The findings revealed a statistically significant inverse association between age and both central corneal thickness (r = -0.209) and corneal thickness at the thinnest point (r = -0.190*) (P < 0.05). Regarding anterior chamber parameters, a statistically significant negative correlation was observed between anterior chamber volume and age (r = -0.263, P = 0.001). Similarly, anterior chamber depth showed a significant negative correlation with age (r = -0.278**, P = 0.001). Interestingly, the anterior chamber angle also demonstrated a significant inverse association with age (r = -0.197*, P = 0.015). There was a weak, non-significant positive correlation between peripapillary RNFL thickness and age (r = 0.124, P = 0.128). Conversely, a statistically significant inverse correlation was observed between ONH rim area and age (r = -0.198, P = 0.014). Table 4 Correlation coefficients between peripapillary retinal nerve fibre layer (RNFL) thickness, optic nerve head rim area, anterior chamber parameters, axial length and age (n=152) *: p<0.05 Age (Years) (26.72±9.35) Characteristics Mean ± S.D Correlation P-value Central corneal thickness (μm) 557.78 ± 34.27 -0.209 ** 0.01 Corneal thickness at thinnest area (μm) 552.76 ± 33.89 -0.190 * 0.019 Anterior chamber angle (degree) 39.77±5.52 -0.197* 0.015 Anterior chamber volume (mm 3 ) 169.55±1.34 -0.263 ** 0.001 Anterior chamber depth (mm) 3.10±0.32 -0.278 ** 0.001 Axial length (mm) 23.95±0.67 0.158 0.052 Peripapillary RNFL thickness (μm) 104.76±9.51 0.124 0.128 Optic nerve head rim area (mm²) 1.28±0.32 -0.198 * 0.014

[discussion] Discussion In myopic eyes, distinct anatomical changes affect both the posterior and anterior segments. These alterations include axial elongation, scleral widening, and enlargement of the lamina cribrosa, all of which may influence optic disc parameters. These structural changes can also extend to the anterior eye segment, potentially modifying corneal curvature and thickness, as well as anterior chamber characteristics such as depth, volume, and angle. These modifications reflect the broader impact of axial elongation on the overall structure and function of the eye. Thus, the current study aimed to investigate the correlation between peripapillary RNFL thickness, ONH rim area, anterior chamber depth, anterior chamber angle, and anterior chamber volume in individuals with myopia. This study highlights significant structural changes in myopic eyes, particularly in relation to corneal thickness, anterior chamber parameters, and optic nerve morphology. The observed inverse correlation between myopia severity and both the central and thinnest points of corneal thickness aligns with previous studies suggesting that corneal thinning may be a biomechanical consequence of axial elongation in myopic eyes [ 11 , 18 ]. A recent study has shown a negative correlation between central corneal thickness and both the rate of myopia progression and axial length elongation. This suggests that a thinner cornea may be associated with a fast progression of myopia, highlighting the potential role of corneal biomechanics in the development and progression of refractive errors [ 19 ]. The positive associations between anterior chamber volume and anterior chamber depth with the severity of myopia indicate a pattern of anterior eye segment alteration that is consistently observed in myopic eyes. This finding is consistent with a previous study, which showed that anterior chamber depth increases with the severity of myopia [ 20 ]. Furthermore, the positive correlation observed between anterior chamber depth and axial length in the present study suggests that deeper anterior chamber depth may be associated with greater axial elongation, potentially reflecting structural adaptations in the myopic eye. These results support the hypothesis which stated that biometric parameters, particularly axial length and anterior chamber depth, play a crucial role in the development and monitoring of myopia. Although our study reinforces existing evidence, further longitudinal research is needed to clarify the causal associations and explore the clinical implications of these associations in different age groups and degrees of myopia [ 21 ]. Moreover, an earlier study [ 11 ] showed that myopic eyes typically have a deeper anterior chamber depth compared to hyperopic eyes, reflecting structural differences associated with refractive status. The lack of significant association between anterior chamber angle and myopia suggests that not all anterior segment parameters are equally affected by refractive status. Axial length demonstrated a strong inverse correlation with both peripapillary RNFL thickness and ONH rim area. These findings are consistent with a recent study indicating that axial elongation significantly influences OCT measurements, often leading to reduced RNFL thickness and ONH parameters in myopic eyes [ 22 ]. Such structural changes can make glaucoma diagnosis more challenging, as the anatomical alterations seen in high myopia may resemble or conceal true glaucomatous damage [ 23 ]. Cakir et al. reported that while RNFL thickness was helpful in diagnosing glaucoma in myopic patients, ONH rim thickness showed superior diagnostic accuracy. This highlights the potential of ORH rim measurements as more reliable indicators of glaucomatous changes in eyes affected by axial elongation [ 24 ]. Myopic eyes have been shown to exhibit distinct structural characteristics, including larger optic discs and ONH areas, tilted optic discs, and increased peripapillary atrophy [ 25 , 26 ]. Given the diagnostic challenges posed by high myopia, particularly in differentiating glaucomatous damage from myopia-related anatomical changes, careful interpretation of OCT parameters is essential. Recent evidence highlights the greater diagnostic value of RNFL and ganglion cell-inner plexiform layer thickness compared to ONH metrics in detecting glaucoma among myopic patients [ 22 ]. These insights underscore the importance of integrating axial length and macular parameters into routine clinical assessments to enhance diagnostic accuracy and facilitate early detection of glaucoma in myopic populations. It should also be noted that OCT-derived measurements in highly myopic eyes can be influenced by ocular magnification related to increased axial length. This optical effect may lead to an apparent reduction in peripapillary RNFL thickness, even without true neural loss, and therefore represents an important confounder when interpreting structural OCT findings in myopic patients. Previous research has shown that applying magnification-correction methods, such as those described by Littmann and Bennett, can help minimise this effect and enhance the accuracy of RNFL assessment in eyes with longer axial lengths [ 12 , 13 , 23 ] In the present study, the analysis revealed age-related changes in various ocular parameters. A statistically significant negative correlation was found between age and anterior chamber volume (r = -0.263, P = 0.001), as well as anterior chamber depth (r = -0.278, P = 0.001), indicating a reduction in these dimensions with increasing age. Additionally, the anterior chamber angle showed a significant inverse association with age (r = -0.197, P = 0.015), suggesting narrowing of the angle with increasing age. However, a significant inverse association was observed between optic nerve head rim area and age (r = -0.198, P = 0.014), indicating a potential reduction in neuroretinal rim tissue with aging. These findings highlight the importance of considering age-related anatomical changes when evaluating ocular health and disease risk. Recent studies suggest that normal ageing plays a significant role in RNFL thinning, highlighting the importance of accounting for age-related changes when interpreting OCT results. The study revealed that both baseline RNFL thickness and age significantly influence the rate of RNFL decline. This distinction is crucial for differentiating pathological thinning due to glaucoma from physiological thinning associated with ageing or myopic changes [ 22 ].

[conclusion] Conclusions The study demonstrates that increased myopia is significantly associated with reduced corneal thickness as well as increased anterior chamber volume and depth, while the anterior chamber angle remains unaffected. Additionally, axial elongation shows a strong inverse correlation with peripapillary RNFL thickness and ONH rim area, emphasising the importance of considering axial length and structural changes when evaluating myopic eyes. These findings underscore the importance of assessing RNFL thickness, neuroretinal rim thickness, anterior chamber, and axial elongation in myopic eyes, particularly in the context of ocular diseases such as glaucoma, myopic macular degeneration, and retinal detachment.

retrieved_papers.title: Acute effects of high-intensity interval training on retinal microvascular circulation in cancer patients and healthy controls: insight from the optical coherence tomography angiography.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] This study assessed retinal microvascular adaptations to a single high-intensity interval (HIIT) session using optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) in young cancer patients receiving vasculotoxic chemotherapy, compared to healthy controls of similar age.
Cancer patients (age 33 ± 4 years, body-mass index = 22.6 ± 3.11 kg/m2, n = 12, 66% women) undergoing acute anti-cancer treatment and controls (30 ± 4 years, body-mass index = 23.6 ± 1.76 kg/m2, n = 12, 50% women) completed two laboratory visits. In the first, they performed a cardiopulmonary exercise test (CPET) until voluntary exhaustion. Within 48-72 h, participants completed a HIIT protocol (7 × 1 min at 90% peak power output, PPO) on a stationary cycle-ergometer. OCTA assessed retinal blood flow and acute vascular responses at three time points: baseline, immediately post-exercise, and 30 min post-exercise.
Cancer patients attained 31% lower PPO and had four minutes shorter CPET than controls, resulting in lower peak gas exchange values. During HIIT, groups exercised at different training loads (222 vs. 152 W). OCTAVA software analyzed acute responses in the deep retinal layer, superficial layer, and optic disc. A significant interaction effect was observed mainly in deep and superficial retina (η² ranged from 0.165 to 0.473, p < 0.01), not in the optic disc. The only significant correlation was between optic disc-derived m. tortuosity and V̇O₂ peak in cancer patients (r = 0.643, p = 0.024), not controls (p = 0.642).
Retinal microvascular reactivity was significantly altered following acute HIIT in patients undergoing chemotherapy, compared to healthy controls. Future research should evaluate chronic HIIT effects and explore the prognostic relevance of ocular microvascular adaptations in cancer care.

retrieved_papers.title: An adaptive threshold based image processing technique for improved glaucoma detection and classification.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2015]
[Abstract] Glaucoma is an optic neuropathy which is one of the main causes of permanent blindness worldwide. This paper presents an automatic image processing based method for detection of glaucoma from the digital fundus images. In this proposed work, the discriminatory parameters of glaucoma infection, such as cup to disc ratio (CDR), neuro retinal rim (NRR) area and blood vessels in different regions of the optic disc has been used as features and fed as inputs to learning algorithms for glaucoma diagnosis. These features which have discriminatory changes with the occurrence of glaucoma are strategically used for training the classifiers to improve the accuracy of identification. The segmentation of optic disc and cup based on adaptive threshold of the pixel intensities lying in the optic nerve head region. Unlike existing methods the proposed algorithm is based on an adaptive threshold that uses local features from the fundus image for segmentation of optic cup and optic disc making it invariant to the quality of the image and noise content which may find wider acceptability. The experimental results indicate that such features are more significant in comparison to the statistical or textural features as considered in existing works. The proposed work achieves an accuracy of 94.11% with a sensitivity of 100%. A comparison of the proposed work with the existing methods indicates that the proposed approach has improved accuracy of classification glaucoma from a digital fundus which may be considered clinically significant.

[Introduction] 1. INTRODUCTION Glaucoma is a leading cause of irreversible blindness. Early detection of this condition is challenging, and accurate diagnosis typically requires multiple evaluations by experienced specialists using various medical imaging tools. Color fundus imaging is widely used to identify suspicious optic discs because it is easily accessible, affordable, and noninvasive. Numerous studies have demonstrated the diagnostic potential of glaucoma detection algorithms based on color fundus imaging. 1 , 2 The following text outlines the progression of medical imaging techniques for glaucoma detection, spanning the initial development of imaging tools and processing algorithms to the application of machine and deep learning techniques for detection. These techniques have considerably improved the efficiency of glaucoma detection on color fundus images. Red-free light fundus photography was first introduced in 1972. It substantially improved glaucoma detection by increasing the visibility of superficial retinal reflections, particularly the stripe reflections of nerve fiber bundles. 3 – 6 In 2008, an image processing algorithm was developed to measure the optic disc and optic cup areas, enabling providers to calculate the cup-to-disc ratio (CDR) and facilitating large-scale automatic screening for glaucoma. 7 In 2016, the application of machine learning and image processing to glaucoma detection marked a further breakthrough, substantially improving the efficiency of automated CDR calculations. 8 In 2017, deep learning technology was introduced, enabling the application of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) as feature engineering modules. After image features are extracted, these modules match classifiers with machine learning algorithms or fully connected layers to train glaucoma detection models. 9 , 10 Deep learning has also been used to automatically segment and calculate the CDR, demonstrating its potential for cross-dataset applications. 11 , 12 In 2022, the use of diverse datasets to train models enhanced their versatility, 13 and in 2023, the integration of multiple deep learning models designed for image quality detection and glaucoma disease segmentation and detection further enhanced glaucoma detection. 14 Scholars in this area of research have primarily focused on developing algorithms for single datasets. However, models must be generalizable and cost-effective for artificial intelligence (AI)–assisted glaucoma detection to be widely applicable. Accordingly, in this study, we created a cost-effective generalized glaucoma detection model using color fundus images. Fundus images are of high resolution and contain vivid colors, and therefore, strategies are required to address variations in image characteristics arising from differences in camera equipment and patient characteristics, such as race and comorbidities. In addition, maintaining consistent performance across diverse datasets and images reflecting various comorbidities can be challenging. We therefore integrated several image processing methods and semantic segmentation models to develop a robust feature extraction process. Weighted images were used to fine-tune the pretrained classification model. Diagnostic algorithms based on fundus images, which are readily available in hospitals and clinics, have considerable potential for real-world application. We created such an algorithm through a multistep process: Like red-free images, color fundus images retain only the green light wavelength, enhancing image features. We thus applied image processing algorithms to the green channel to improve feature contrast and sharpness. Key diagnostic areas were identified through semantic segmentation, which avoided the influence of noise and the confounding effects of comorbidities. Domain knowledge was used to assign increased weight to areas critical for glaucoma detection, which further reduced the effects of noise and comorbidities. We validated our method in multiple external datasets, demonstrating a balanced accuracy of 80%, which is comparable to that of commercial optical coherence tomography (OCT) images. 15 Furthermore, our use of color fundus images offers considerable cost advantages, underscoring our method’s promise for use in large-scale glaucoma screening.

[introduction] 1. INTRODUCTION Glaucoma is a leading cause of irreversible blindness. Early detection of this condition is challenging, and accurate diagnosis typically requires multiple evaluations by experienced specialists using various medical imaging tools. Color fundus imaging is widely used to identify suspicious optic discs because it is easily accessible, affordable, and noninvasive. Numerous studies have demonstrated the diagnostic potential of glaucoma detection algorithms based on color fundus imaging. 1 , 2 The following text outlines the progression of medical imaging techniques for glaucoma detection, spanning the initial development of imaging tools and processing algorithms to the application of machine and deep learning techniques for detection. These techniques have considerably improved the efficiency of glaucoma detection on color fundus images. Red-free light fundus photography was first introduced in 1972. It substantially improved glaucoma detection by increasing the visibility of superficial retinal reflections, particularly the stripe reflections of nerve fiber bundles. 3 – 6 In 2008, an image processing algorithm was developed to measure the optic disc and optic cup areas, enabling providers to calculate the cup-to-disc ratio (CDR) and facilitating large-scale automatic screening for glaucoma. 7 In 2016, the application of machine learning and image processing to glaucoma detection marked a further breakthrough, substantially improving the efficiency of automated CDR calculations. 8 In 2017, deep learning technology was introduced, enabling the application of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) as feature engineering modules. After image features are extracted, these modules match classifiers with machine learning algorithms or fully connected layers to train glaucoma detection models. 9 , 10 Deep learning has also been used to automatically segment and calculate the CDR, demonstrating its potential for cross-dataset applications. 11 , 12 In 2022, the use of diverse datasets to train models enhanced their versatility, 13 and in 2023, the integration of multiple deep learning models designed for image quality detection and glaucoma disease segmentation and detection further enhanced glaucoma detection. 14 Scholars in this area of research have primarily focused on developing algorithms for single datasets. However, models must be generalizable and cost-effective for artificial intelligence (AI)–assisted glaucoma detection to be widely applicable. Accordingly, in this study, we created a cost-effective generalized glaucoma detection model using color fundus images. Fundus images are of high resolution and contain vivid colors, and therefore, strategies are required to address variations in image characteristics arising from differences in camera equipment and patient characteristics, such as race and comorbidities. In addition, maintaining consistent performance across diverse datasets and images reflecting various comorbidities can be challenging. We therefore integrated several image processing methods and semantic segmentation models to develop a robust feature extraction process. Weighted images were used to fine-tune the pretrained classification model. Diagnostic algorithms based on fundus images, which are readily available in hospitals and clinics, have considerable potential for real-world application. We created such an algorithm through a multistep process: Like red-free images, color fundus images retain only the green light wavelength, enhancing image features. We thus applied image processing algorithms to the green channel to improve feature contrast and sharpness. Key diagnostic areas were identified through semantic segmentation, which avoided the influence of noise and the confounding effects of comorbidities. Domain knowledge was used to assign increased weight to areas critical for glaucoma detection, which further reduced the effects of noise and comorbidities. We validated our method in multiple external datasets, demonstrating a balanced accuracy of 80%, which is comparable to that of commercial optical coherence tomography (OCT) images. 15 Furthermore, our use of color fundus images offers considerable cost advantages, underscoring our method’s promise for use in large-scale glaucoma screening.

[methods] 2. METHODS Fig. 1 illustrates the study workflow. We leveraged image preprocessing techniques to extract the green channels of color fundus images and enhance the contrast and sharpness of image features. After preprocessing, we used the DeepLabV3+ architecture 16 to construct a semantic segmentation model capable of selecting critical areas for glaucoma detection. The DeepLabV3+ model, which uses transfer learning, was trained on images from Taipei Veterans General Hospital (TVGH). We then applied the segmentation results from DeepLabV3+ to weight the original image regions. These weighted images were used to train the EfficientNetV2-M 17 transfer learning model, creating our glaucoma detection classifier, referred to hereafter as the TVGH model. Fig. 1 Overview of proposed glaucoma detection workflow, encompassing preprocessing, segmentation, and classification stages. The diagnostic accuracy of the TVGH model was evaluated according to test images from TVGH and five external datasets, including four open datasets (DRISHTI-GS1, 18 VEIRC, 19 , 20 Refuge, 21 and LES-AV 22 ) and one private dataset (Cheng Hsin General Hospital [CHGH]). Our comprehensive glaucoma detection model integrates image feature enhancement and automatic segmentation of critical areas for disease detection, which enhances its predictive performance on external datasets. 2.1. Data collection The TVGH dataset included 1075 nonglaucomatous eyes from the TVGH Ophthalmic Imaging Database and 621 fundus photographs of eyes with glaucoma in the JPEG format. Glaucoma was diagnosed on the basis of characteristic changes in the optic nerve head (ONH) and associated reproducible visual field defects. Glaucomatous ONH changes were defined as an elevated CDR and generalized neuroretinal rim thinning or localized notches in the rim. All patients with glaucoma underwent visual field analysis with the 24-2 Swedish Interactive Threshold Algorithm standard on a Humphrey Field Analyser 750i (version 4.2, Zeiss-Humphrey Instruments, Dublin, CA) and retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) scanning with a Cirrus high-definition OCT device (model 4000; Carl Zeiss Meditec, Dublin, CA). The nonglaucomatous control eyes had intraocular pressure of <21 mmHg and an ONH of normal appearance without RNFL defects. Images with severe artifacts, excessive blurring, or loss of visibility in the ONH or RNFL were excluded from the training set. Additionally, overexposed optic discs were excluded through careful visual assessment by glaucoma specialists to ensure adequate image quality for model training. Only images in which the main diagnostic features remained visible after preprocessing were included in the training set. Table 1 lists the imaging characteristics of the TVGH and external datasets. Images were classified on the basis of the majority opinion of experts for the DRISHTI-GS1, Refuge, VEIRC, and LES-AV datasets and through expert consensus after a review of detailed clinical information for the CHGH and TVGH datasets. The TVGH dataset images were divided into training, validation, and test sets in a 64%:16%:20% sampling ratio. Table 1 Characteristics of the datasets used in this study Dataset Numbers (glaucoma) Image size (pixels) Field of view (center) Camera Origin TVGH 1696 (621) 3888 × 2552 30° (macula) Canon CX-1, CR-2, DGi Taiwan CHGH 158 (78) 3216 × 2136 30°(macula) Topcon TRC-NW8F Taiwan DRISHTI-GS1 101 (70) 2047 × 1760 30°(disc) Zeiss Visucam NM/FA India VEIRC 257 (32) 2544 × 1696 25°(macula) Welch Allyn Pan India Refuge 400 (40) 2124 × 2056 (macula) Zeiss Visucam 500 China LES-AV 22 (11) 1620 × 1444 30°(disc) Visucam ProNm Austria 2.2. Image preprocessing The five-step image preprocessing procedure is illustrated in Fig. 2 . First, color fundus images were converted into binary images, and the area outside the retina was replaced with zero padding. This step ensured that the background signal did not interfere with subsequent CNN modeling. Second, the image was cropped to square dimensions along the retinal boundaries. Nonsquare cropped images were padded with zeros to create square dimensions. This step ensured that the input images had the same layout and that the image features were not distorted by resizing when added to the CNN model. Third, the green channel was retained in the cropped images, which enabled observation of disease characteristics such as RNFL striation and changes in the vessel contour within the optic disc. Fourth, the contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE) algorithm was applied for image contrast enhancement. 23 , 24 Finally, image sharpness was increased through an unsharp masking process based on Gaussian blur. Fig. 2 Five-step image preprocessing pipeline: (1) zero padding outside retinal boundary, (2) cropping and squaring of retinal region, (3) green channel extraction, (4) contrast enhancement with CLAHE, and (5) sharpening through unsharp masking based on Gaussian blur. CLAHE = contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization. 2.2.1. Image contrast enhancement with CLAHE The CLAHE technique is widely used to enhance image features by increasing contrast. This method improves upon histogram equalization by both increasing image contrast and suppressing noise amplification through the application of a brightness threshold. Fig. 3 illustrates the differences between the green channel, the green channel with histogram equalization, and the green channel with CLAHE. Fig. 4 displays the corresponding histogram differences for Fig. 3 . Fig. 3 Comparison of green channel images: (A) original, (B) histogram equalized, and (C) CLAHE-enhanced. CLAHE = contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization. Fig. 4 Corresponding intensity histograms for images in the figure. CLAHE = contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization. 2.2.2. Unsharp masking for enhanced image sharpness Unsharp masking is a technique that increases image sharpness by emphasizing the image’s edges. This method is used to generate a sharpened image by subtracting a blurred version from the original. Fig. 5 demonstrates differences in the characteristic channel image after the application of both CLAHE and unsharp masking for sharpening. Fig. 5 Image sharpness comparison after application of CLAHE and unsharp masking. CLAHE = contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization. 2.3. Image segmentation and weighting with DeepLabV3+ architecture Glaucoma specialists determine the presence of glaucoma by examining the ONH and RNFL in color fundus images. Because CNN models are sensitive to subtle changes in image features, numerous characteristics of fundus photographs from different datasets can influence their classification. These characteristics include ethnic variations, differences in fundus cameras and operators, and interference from other retinal conditions, such as retinal hemorrhages, exudates, or other optic neuropathies. We manually annotated the regions of interest (ROI) for segmentation, focusing on the ONH and surrounding RNFL. The segmented area was extended radially according to the disc radius to encompass major vessel trunks representing the RNFL double-hump region. 25 Macular regions were excluded to avoid the confounding effects of unrelated retinal pathologies, as illustrated in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6 Manually annotated ROI, including ONH and surrounding RNFL, with macular area excluded. ONH = optic nerve head; RNFL = retinal nerve fiber layer; ROI = regions of interest. The segmentation model (DeepLabV3+ with MobileNet-V2 backbone) was trained on the ROI masks we defined. The resulting segmentation masks were used to generate weighted images, in which regions corresponding to the ROI retained their original pixel intensity and all other areas were down-weighted. This weighting strategy was designed to guide the classification model to focus on clinically relevant features and to reduce the effects of comorbidities and background noise. We used a pretrained semantic segmentation model incorporating the DeepLabV3+ architecture to automatically segment the ROI. DeepLabV3+ was introduced by Google at the European Conference on Computer Vision in 2018. Its encoder-decoder structure and enhanced Xception model serve as the backbone network of encoders for image feature extraction. The encoder-decoder structure of the DeepLabV3+ architecture, illustrated in Fig. 7 , contains several components designed for improved semantic segmentation, including the Atrous spatial pyramid pooling (ASPP) module. This module enables the model to capture features at multiple scales by applying dilated convolutions. As illustrated in Fig. 8 , ASPP expands the receptive field coverage, improves feature extraction across resolutions, and reduces computational complexity. Depthwise separable convolution, illustrated in Fig. 9 , is another crucial element of the DeepLabV3+ architecture. This feature combines depthwise and pointwise convolutions to reduce computational complexity while maintaining performance, a notable advantage in our study. Fig. 7 Network architecture of DeepLabV3+ with MobileNet-V2 backbone for semantic segmentation. Fig. 8 Multiscale feature capture by ASPP module. ASPP = atrous spatial pyramid pooling. Fig. 9 Depthwise separable convolution structure combining depthwise and pointwise operations. We used MobileNet-V2 instead of the Xception model as the backbone of the encoder network. Additionally, we applied Dice Loss 26 as the loss function and mean intersection-over-union (MIoU) as the training evaluation metric. MobileNet-V2 provides a lighter-weight network with fewer parameters and faster computation speed than Xception does. The Dice Loss is defined in Equation (1), and the MIoU diagram is provided in Fig. 10 . Fig. 10 MIoU calculation. MIoU = mean intersection-over-union. D i c e L o s s = 2 ∑ i N p i g i ∑ i N ⁡ p i 2 + ∑ i N ⁡ g i 2 (1) where N is the number of the predicted binary segmentation pixels p i and ground truth binary pixels g i . We applied a weighted image strategy to guide the model’s focus on the ROI and reduce the effects of comorbidities. After ROI segmentation with the DeepLabV3+ model, the original pixel intensities within the ROI were retained, whereas those outside the ROI were down-weighted by 50%. This weighting approach preserved diagnostic information in relevant areas while suppressing noise from less informative regions. Fig. 11 illustrates this process, displaying the original image, segmentation output, and final weighted image. Fig. 11 Weighted image generation process: (A) original, (B) segmentation mask, (C) weighted image. 2.4. Image classification with EfficientNetV2-M architecture and fine-tuning of process workflow 2.4.1. Application of EfficientNetV2-M architecture to fine-tune pretrained model EfficientNetV2 was selected as the pretrained backbone for glaucoma prediction because of its favorable balance of accuracy and computational efficiency. EfficientNetV2 was introduced by Google at the 2021 International Conference on Machine Learning, and it achieved state-of-the-art performance on the ImageNet ILSVRC2012 and ImageNet21k benchmarks. Its key architectural components include the Fused-MBConv module; compound scaling strategies to expand depth, width, and resolution; and progressive learning techniques to accelerate training. These design features contribute to both improved classification accuracy and reduced computational cost. We used a pretrained EfficientNetV2-M model as the glaucoma classification backbone to leverage its computational efficiency and suitability for deployment on resource-limited platforms. We retained the original architecture and replaced the final fully connected layer with a two-neuron output layer using a Softmax activation function for binary classification. The OpenCV Python library was used to resize images to the default resolution of 480 × 480 pixels, ensuring compatibility with the network and maintaining preprocessing efficiency, which improves the model’s real-world applicability. Focal Loss 27 was adopted as the loss function to address class imbalance during fine-tuning. Focal Loss extends standard cross-entropy (CE) loss by down-weighting easy examples to focus training on more difficult, misclassified cases, which is particularly beneficial in imbalanced medical datasets. For binary classification, Focal Loss is determined on the basis of the CE, expressed as follows: C E ( p , y ) = C E ( p t ) = − log p t where y ∈ { ± 1 } specifies the ground truth class and p ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] is the probability predicted by the model for the class labeled y = 1. p t is defined as follows: p t = { − log ⁡ p , y = 1 − log ( 1 − p ) , o t h e r w i s e , Additionally, the α-balanced Focal Loss is defined as follows: α − B a l a n c e d F o c a l L o s s = − α t ( 1 − p t ) γ log ( p t ) (2) where α ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] for class 1 and γ ≥ 0 . The learning rate decreases as the number of epochs increases, with a dynamic mechanism set to monitor the validation loss. The initial learning rate is 10 − 4 and remains fixed as long as the validation loss decreases. If the validation loss does not decrease for more than three epochs, the learning rate is reduced by 30%, with a minimum limit of 10 − 8. We evaluated the model’s predictive performance according to the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC ROC). The area under the curve (AUC) ranges from 0 to 1, with an AUC of 1 indicating a perfect classification model and an AUC of 0.5 indicating a model that randomly assigns categories to test data. The final model was selected on the basis of the AUC value of the validation set. Data augmentation was performed to improve model performance and prevent overfitting; we randomly rotated images by 20°, flipped them horizontally or vertically, and shifted them by 10% of the total width or height. For validation, we applied TensorFlow, an open-source deep learning framework (Version 2.10.0, https://www.tensorflow.org/versions/r2.10/api_docs ) with an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 Ti graphics processing unit (NVIDIA Corporation, Santa Clara, CA). The accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, balanced accuracy, and AUC were calculated as follows: A c c u r a c y = T r u e P o s i t i v e + T r u e N e g a t i v e T r u e P o s i t i v e + T r u e N e g a t i v e + F a l s e P o s i t i v e + F a l s e N e g a t i v e S e n s i t i v i t y = T r u e P o s i t i v e T r u e P o s i t i v e + F a l s e N e g a t i v e S p e c i f i c i t y = T r u e N e g a t i v e T r u e N e g a t i v e + F a l s e P o s i t i v e B a l a n c e d A c c u r a c y = S e n s i t i v i t y + S p e c i f i c i t y 2 2.4.2. Youden index for optimal prediction thresholds and Grad-CAM for visualization of prediction results During inference, the model outputs a probability score and applies a default classification threshold of 0.5 to distinguish between glaucomatous and normal cases. However, this default threshold may not yield optimal predictive performance, particularly in imbalanced or heterogeneous datasets. Given that the training, validation, and test sets in this study were derived through random sampling, we determined the optimal threshold on the basis of the validation set by using the Youden index, which balances sensitivity and specificity. The selected threshold was then applied to the test set to increase performance consistency. Given the clinical imperative of early glaucoma detection, a sensitivity of at least 80% was set as a requirement. Therefore, we only calculated the Youden index for thresholds that achieved a sensitivity ≥80%, ensuring the selected threshold met the minimum clinical criterion. T h r e s h o l d ( T ) = { i 1000 : i = 0 , 1 , ⋯ , 999 , 1000 } Y o u d e n I n d e x = max τ ∈ T ( S e n s i t i v i t y τ + S p e c i f i c i t y τ − 1 ) When this definition of the Youden index was applied, the recorded sensitivity exceeded the 80% threshold. The recorded threshold values were then used in sequence to calculate the optimal threshold for the best classification performance according to the Youden index. Gradient-weighted class activation mapping (Grad-CAM) 28 was used to visualize the discriminative regions identified by the CNN classifier. Grad-CAM uses the gradients of the target class flowing into the final convolutional layer to compute the relative importance of each feature map. These importance weights are then combined with the feature maps to generate a coarse localization map, which is projected onto the input fundus image. The resulting heatmap highlights the primary regions contributing to the model’s prediction, enabling users to interpret the classification decision. 2.5. Statistical analysis CNN model performance was evaluated according to the MIoU, AUC, balanced accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy indices. The matplotlib package in Python was used to plot the ROC curve.

[results] 3.3. Grad-CAM for visualization of model prediction results As demonstrated in Fig. 14 , Grad-CAM was applied to highlight model features critical for classification, with these depicted using heat maps. These features were primarily concentrated in the ONH and peripapillary nerve fibers. Fig. 14 Grad-CAM heatmaps illustrating regions critical for classification. Heatmaps were generated from test images processed through different stages (from right to left): original, green channel, preprocessed, segmented, and weighted images. Grad-CAM highlights the predominant diagnostic regions contributing to the model’s decision, particularly around the ONH. Grad-CAM = gradient-weighted class activation mapping; ONH = optic nerve head.

[discussion] 4. DISCUSSION We developed a glaucoma detection model to address the challenges posed by cross-institutional image variability and interference from retinal comorbidities. Our weighted segmentation approach demonstrated high generalizability across multiple external datasets and was validated using a limited number of training images from a single institute. Our approach achieves generalizability through the use of image preprocessing and segmentation without the need for extensive fine-tuning involving large quantities of images from various external sources. These image processing methods are rooted in our domain knowledge of the diagnostic cues and pathogenesis of glaucoma. Our strategy has notable advantages and may provide medical institutions—including institutions with limited resources or image data for fine-tuning their models—with a reliable AI-powered diagnostic tool for glaucoma. Our robust glaucoma feature extraction process was developed using color fundus images, and our method achieved comparable diagnostic performance to strategies involving OCT images. 15 Fundus images, which can be obtained with low-cost, portable equipment, have considerable potential for glaucoma screening. Additionally, our findings suggest that in scenarios involving data imbalance and limited external validation samples, accuracy and AUC metrics may insufficiently reflect model performance. For example, as indicated in Table 2 , the sensitivity of the TVGH-Green Channel and TVGH-Preprocessing models on the VEIRC dataset was 59.38% and 56.25%, respectively. However, their accuracies reached 92.22% and 91.83%, with corresponding AUC values of 0.927 and 0.907. Therefore, we suggest that balanced accuracy may be a more robust evaluation metric for model performance. As expected, the performance of the TVGH-Original model markedly declined when applied to external datasets. We therefore developed the TVGH-Green Channel model, which leverages the image’s green channel for enhanced visualization of the RNFL, a crucial characteristic in glaucoma evaluation. This model demonstrated improved balanced accuracy in the CHGH, DRISHTI-GS1, and Refuge datasets. Within the DRISHTI-GS1 dataset, both the model’s sensitivity and balanced accuracy considerably increased. These experimental results indicate that the features the model learns from the green channel images may not improve overall prediction performance but can reduce cross-institutional variabilities in color fundus images. The TVGH-preprocessing model combines green channel images with image processing methods, offering enhanced contrast and sharpness to improve visualization of glaucoma lesions. This model demonstrated a balanced accuracy above 80% in most test data, that is, all but the VEIRC dataset. Matching of green channel contrast and sharpness thus improved the model’s generalization during cross-institutional verification. As illustrated in Fig. 15 , the segmentation model effectively excluded comorbidity lesions and noise during testing. This model accurately segmented the optic disc and optic nerve fibers in images centered on either the disc or macula, as displayed in Fig. 16 . For the TVGH-Segmentation model, the regions predicted by the segmentation model were used as the training set. The balanced accuracy of its classification within the TVGH test set and CHGH and Refuge datasets reached 87.43%, 89.88%, and 82.92%, respectively. In the DRISHTI-GS1, LES-AV, and VEIRC datasets, it achieved balanced accuracy rates of 76.77%, 77.27%, and 74.58%, respectively. These results indicate that the TVGH-Segmentation model needs to only focus on the areas marked by the segmentation model to achieve a balanced accuracy of at least 74.58% in cross-institutional verification. Fig. 15 Segmentation results demonstrating the model’s ability to exclude comorbid lesions and noise. Fig. 16 Segmentation robustness across image centers. Although the TVGH-Segmentation model effectively reduces the confounding effects of comorbid lesions, it may inadvertently diminish the perception of glaucoma lesions beyond the optic disc and nerve fibers. Thus, in the TVGH-Weighted model, the brightness of the automatically segmented area is maintained, whereas the brightness of all other regions is reduced. This increases the weight of glaucoma lesions while minimizing the effects of comorbid lesions and noise. We trained the TVGH-Weighted model with a higher weight assigned to the area surrounding glaucoma lesions. The experimental results presented in Tables 2 and 3 indicate that the TVGH-Weighted model achieved a balanced accuracy of over 80% for color fundus images in cross-institutional validation datasets and datasets with comorbidities. Our findings regarding the TVGH-Weighted model demonstrate the potential of AI-assisted diagnosis in clinical applications, indicating it exhibits promise for improved glaucoma detection in real-world scenarios. Notably, Al-Bander et al 11 and Pachade et al 12 have used deep learning approaches to segment optic cups and discs from fundus images. Although the CDR is crucial for glaucoma evaluation, it is influenced by disc size and cannot serve as the sole parameter for diagnosis. RNFL patterns and changes in color fundus images also provide valuable diagnostic information. Our image preprocessing and segmentation model is designed to efficiently extract information from the optic disc and RNFL. Similarly, Ko et al 13 used a pretraining model for glaucoma detection and fine-tuned the model weights by integrating external data with the existing training dataset. This approach produced a glaucoma detection model applicable across institutions. However, using this type of mixed-data approach raises concerns regarding information security and does not address the confounding effects of comorbidities. Our study presents a robust feature extraction method that does not require external data to fine-tune weights, featuring a segmentation approach suitable for images with comorbidities. Qian et al 14 adopted a different segmentation approach in which the radius of the optic disc is computed on the basis of the U-Net architecture, with a rectangular area three times that of the radius serving as the ROI. This method was validated on two external datasets from Chinese institutions, yielding AUCs of 0.952 and 0.966, respectively. The higher ROC observed in Qian et al 14 study may be related to the requirement they imposed of images passing quality checks before classification. However, whether their approach is affected by comorbidities involving this area remains unclear. Nevertheless, the aforementioned studies validate the effectiveness of the segmentation approach. The TVGH-Weighted model was validated on an external dataset from Taiwan (CHGH), achieving an AUC of 0.961. It was also tested on multinational external datasets, including DRISHTI-GS1, Refuge, LES-AV, and VEIRC, yielding AUCs of 0.889, 0.913, 0.876, and 0.887, respectively. Verification of the TVGH-Weighted model on a dataset with comorbidities produced an AUC of 0.93. These findings indicate that our proposed TVGH-Weighted model is generalizable to cross-institutional retinal images from individuals of various ethnicities and those with comorbidities, which underscores the feasibility of applying AI-assisted technology in clinical diagnosis. Our method addresses cross-institutional efficiency challenges in AI-assisted diagnosis and enhances green channel image features. To mitigate the effects of comorbidities and noise on model performance, the segmentation model identifies critical areas for glaucoma diagnosis, including the optic disc and the region around major vascular trunks representing the RNFL bimodal peaks. This data-driven approach mitigates the confounding effects of comorbid lesions and noise during ROI selection, enabling the model to devote comprehensive focus to vital information regarding the optic disc and its surroundings. Image quality represents a potential limitation that may hinder the clinical application of our approach. ROI segmentation must be implemented to reduce interference from comorbidities and noise, but low-quality images may hinder the segmentation algorithm’s ability to accurately extract the ROI for classification, which can adversely affect diagnostic performance. Although we verified our model using several open-access external datasets to demonstrate its generalizability, large-scale verification must be implemented to confirm its real-world performance. Our segmentation model is designed to suppress the influence of comorbid lesions by focusing on the ONH and peripapillary RNFL. However, the external datasets we used for validation do not contain structured labels for comorbid retinal conditions. Consequently, our direct evaluation of classification performance in the presence of comorbidities was limited to 151 internally annotated images, constraining the full validation of our approach in heterogeneous real-world populations. Therefore, future work should include prospectively labeled multi-institutional cohorts or simulation-based evaluations. This would enable systematic confirmation of model performance in the presence of diverse retinal comorbidities. In this study, our exclusion of overexposed discs required manual visual assessment; objective brightness metrics can be incorporated in future studies to improve reproducibility. Our segmentation masks, which included major vessel trunks to encompass RNFL bimodal peaks, improved the balanced accuracy in comorbidity-laden images compared with the original model (80.91% vs 71.50%, Table 3 ); however, we did not perform an explicit ablation study to isolate the effects of vessel inclusion on model performance in comorbidity-laden images. In future studies, this design choice should be systematically evaluated to further optimize diagnostic accuracy. In conclusion, this study presents a robust and cost-effective AI-assisted framework for glaucoma detection in color fundus images. The proposed method combines domain-driven image preprocessing with semantic segmentation, effectively mitigating the effects of retinal comorbidities and cross-institutional image variability. Our model achieved at least 80% balanced accuracy in experimental testing across five external datasets, which included patients from diverse ethnic backgrounds and imaging conditions. Notably, segmentation-based weighting markedly increased diagnostic performance in challenging cases with comorbidities. These results highlight the potential of lightweight, generalizable AI models to enable accurate glaucoma screening in fundus images alone, which are widely accessible. This approach has strong potential for deployment in real-world clinical settings, particularly in resource-limited environments. Declaration of generative AI in scientific writing The authors used ChatGPT to prepare the manuscript and subsequently reviewed and edited the content as necessary. The authors take full responsibility for the content of the publication.

[conclusion] Conclusion: The proposed AI system can detect glaucoma on standard color fundus photographs with high accuracy across clinical environments and in the presence of comorbid eye diseases. The system may be a practical and affordable tool for large-scale glaucoma screening, particularly in institutions with limited resources.

retrieved_papers.title: Pathological myopia classification with simultaneous lesion segmentation using deep learning.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2021]
[Abstract] Pathological myopia (PM) is the seventh leading cause of blindness, with a reported global prevalence up to 3%. Early and automated PM detection from fundus images could aid to prevent blindness in a world population that is characterized by a rising myopia prevalence. We aim to assess the use of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for the detection of PM and semantic segmentation of myopia-induced lesions from fundus images on a recently introduced reference data set.
This investigation reports on the results of CNNs developed for the recently introduced Pathological Myopia (PALM) dataset, which consists of 1200 images. Our CNN bundles lesion segmentation and PM classification, as the two tasks are heavily intertwined. Domain knowledge is also inserted through the introduction of a new Optic Nerve Head (ONH)-based prediction enhancement for the segmentation of atrophy and fovea localization. Finally, we are the first to approach fovea localization using segmentation instead of detection or regression models. Evaluation metrics include area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) for PM detection, Euclidean distance for fovea localization, and Dice and F1 metrics for the semantic segmentation tasks (optic disc, retinal atrophy and retinal detachment).
Models trained with 400 available training images achieved an AUC of 0.9867 for PM detection, and a Euclidean distance of 58.27 pixels on the fovea localization task, evaluated on a test set of 400 images. Dice and F1 metrics for semantic segmentation of lesions scored 0.9303 and 0.9869 on optic disc, 0.8001 and 0.9135 on retinal atrophy, and 0.8073 and 0.7059 on retinal detachment, respectively.
We report a successful approach for a simultaneous classification of pathological myopia and segmentation of associated lesions. Our work was acknowledged with an award in the context of the "Pathological Myopia detection from retinal images" challenge held during the IEEE International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (April 2019). Considering that (pathological) myopia cases are often identified as false positives and negatives in glaucoma deep learning models, we envisage that the current work could aid in future research to discriminate between glaucomatous and highly-myopic eyes, complemented by the localization and segmentation of landmarks such as fovea, optic disc and atrophy.

[Introduction] 1 Introduction Myopia, or nearsightedness, is emerging as one of the most prevalent refractive errors and eye problems worldwide. Between 1990 and 2023, the pooled prevalence among children and adolescents increased from approximately 24–36%, with projections reaching nearly 40% by 2050, affecting over 740 million young individuals worldwide ( 1 ). As the prevalence of myopia continues to rise, it poses a major public health challenge ( 1–4 ). In urbanized areas of East and Southeast Asia, which are considered developed regions, the prevalence of myopia is approximately 80–90% among young adults ( 5 , 6 ). Moreover, many developed Western countries (mainly European countries) show substantially lower rates (20–40%) compared to East and South Asian countries, while less-developed regions and developing countries (with less intensive education systems) often have prevalence rates below 10% ( 2 , 7 , 8 ). This issue is not only about the high prevalence but also the alarming rise in incidence. Recent epidemiological studies have demonstrated a dramatic increase in myopia incidence, mainly in urbanized populations of East Asia, where intense educational pressure and prolonged digital screen exposure have raised significant concerns ( 3 , 4 , 9 ). Myopia is not only a major cause of visual impairment but also a significant risk factor: approximately 49% of individuals with high myopia develop myopic macular degeneration, 3–8% experience retinal detachment, and the risk of glaucoma nearly doubles ( 10–12 ). Multiple studies have shown that myopia prevalence follows a distinct pattern, with the highest rates observed in urban female individuals (~20%), followed by urban male (~12%) and rural female individuals (~7%), and the lowest in rural male individuals (~5%). Multivariate analyses across these studies indicate that being a student or a professional significantly increases the risk of myopia, whereas rural residence is associated with a reduced risk. In addition, female individuals exhibit a modestly higher prevalence of myopia compared to male individuals ( 13–16 ). Therefore, understanding the risk factors and etiology of myopia is essential for developing effective prevention and intervention strategies. The etiology and patho-mechanism of myopia are complex and multifactorial, involving an interplay between genetic predisposition and environmental exposures. Recent studies have highlighted two key biological theories explaining how myopia develops. The compensatory mechanism theory suggests that the eye grows in response to blurry images (defocus) to help improve vision, a process known as emmetropization . This has been shown in animal models using special lenses or visual deprivation to trigger eye growth ( 17 , 18 ). The second major theory is the dopamine hypothesis , which explains that dopamine, a chemical released in the retina when exposed to bright light, helps slow down eye elongation. When dopamine levels are low, such as during prolonged time indoors or screen use, eye growth may continue unchecked, leading to myopia ( 19 , 20 ). These core mechanisms are not only important for understanding the biology of myopia but also provide a strong foundation for using advanced statistics to understand each risk factor’s impact and predict the risk of myopia. As previously mentioned, the development of myopia is influenced by various risk factors. Family history (parental myopia) has consistently been shown to be a strong predictor of myopia risk, with numerous studies stressing the heritability of axial elongation and refractive error ( 21 , 22 ). Additionally, both modern and old environmental and behavioral factors—such as increased near work, reduced time outdoors, and higher screen time—have emerged as significant contributors to the development of myopia ( 23 , 24 ). Recent studies with a focus on modern life have also emphasized the role of urbanization and socioeconomic standing, where variations in lifestyle behaviors correlate strongly with myopia prevalence ( 25 , 26 ). These insights have significantly advanced the development of hybrid models that bring together clinical, genetic, and behavioral data to predict myopia risk with much greater accuracy. Furthermore, recent improvements in imaging and biometric technologies have enabled the detailed quantification of ocular parameters, including anterior chamber depth (ACD), axial length (AL), and vitreous chamber depth (VCD). These precise and non-invasive measures provide objective biomarkers, and when combined with lifestyle and genetic data, these measures can clarify the mechanisms underlying myopia and its potential risk factors. Studies have demonstrated that ocular biometric parameters, in combination with genetic markers, explain a considerable portion of the variance in refractive outcomes ( 27 , 28 ). This integrative approach has the potential to notify targeted interventions, particularly for high-risk pediatric populations, to mitigate the long-term burden of myopia-related complications. In light of these developments, we bring together two available myopia studies conducted nearly 28 years apart: Zadnik et al.’s 1995–2000 U. S. longitudinal school-based study (≈500 children) ( 29 ), which offers high-precision ocular biometry (AL, spherical equivalent refraction (SPHEQ), ACD and VCD, lens thickness (LT)), detailed near-work and outdoor activity logs, and parental myopia status; and a 2022–2023 Chinese cross-sectional survey (≈100,000 young people) ( 30 ) emphasizing modern digital behaviors (daily TV/computer screen time, lying-down use, screen distance), homework and outdoor exercise frequency, residence type (urban or rural), socioeconomic factors, and parental myopia. Although these datasets differ in era, geography, design (longitudinal vs. cross-sectional), sample size, and variable types (continuous biometric measures vs. ordinal behavioral categories), we applied ensemble learning and transfer learning techniques to align and fuse their complementary strengths. While this integration is far from a turnkey clinical tool, it represents the first step toward harnessing heterogeneous, temporally separated studies and the power of machine learning to capture both biological and lifestyle drivers of myopia risk and to inspire future, more practical multimodal risk-prediction frameworks.

In light of these developments, we bring together two available myopia studies conducted nearly 28 years apart: Zadnik et al.’s 1995–2000 U. S. longitudinal school-based study (≈500 children) ( 29 ), which offers high-precision ocular biometry (AL, spherical equivalent refraction (SPHEQ), ACD and VCD, lens thickness (LT)), detailed near-work and outdoor activity logs, and parental myopia status; and a 2022–2023 Chinese cross-sectional survey (≈100,000 young people) ( 30 ) emphasizing modern digital behaviors (daily TV/computer screen time, lying-down use, screen distance), homework and outdoor exercise frequency, residence type (urban or rural), socioeconomic factors, and parental myopia.
