[methods] 7.2 Application of materials science in NAION Although the application of materials science in the treatment of non-arteritic anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (NAION) remains in the exploratory phase, early advances show promising potential. Notably, curcumin-polydopamine nanocomposite hydrogels (Cur@PDA@GelCA) have demonstrated significant neuroprotective effects in optic nerve injury models. These materials act by inhibiting reactive oxygen species (ROS)-mediated oxidative stress, indicating potential utility for acute-phase intervention in NAION. In addition to their therapeutic efficacy, the hydrogel’s strong tissue adhesion and excellent biocompatibility highlight its suitability for localized drug delivery and targeted treatment ( Liu et al., 2024 ). These findings underscore the broader feasibility of integrating materials science into NAION therapy. Future research should aim to further elucidate the molecular mechanisms underlying NAION and leverage multi-omics technologies alongside biomaterials innovation to develop personalized treatment strategies that can improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_papers.title: OCT-Based Differentiation of First Acute Optic Neuritis: An International Study of 111 Patients With NMOSD and MOGAD.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2026]
[Abstract] Severe optic neuritis (ON) is a common clinical manifestation in myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody-associated disease (MOGAD) and neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD). Given distinct prognoses and often the necessity of early plasma exchange in NMOSD, prompt differentiation is crucial. In this study, we investigated the utility of optical coherence tomography (OCT) in differentiating between first acute NMOSD-ON and MOGAD-associated optic neuritis (MOGAD-ON), as well as specific factors associated with disc edema.
In this retrospective multicenter study, 111 adult patients with MOGAD or aquaporin-4 antibody-positive NMOSD who experienced a first ON and underwent OCT within 2 weeks of symptom onset were included from 14 centers across 8 countries. Peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer (pRNFL) thickness in µm was analyzed, including the average of both eyes in cases of bilateral manifestation.
Eighty-three patients with MOGAD (51 women; 124 ON eyes; bilateral ON 48.2%) and 28 with NMOSD (24 women; 36 ON eyes; bilateral ON 21.4%) were enrolled. A significant increase in pRNFL thickness (>2SD), suggestive of disc edema, was observed in 73.4% of MOGAD-ON eyes and 11.1% of NMOSD-ON eyes (p < 0.001). The pRNFL thickness cutoff of 117.5 µm provided 92.9% specificity and 71.1% sensitivity in distinguishing between MOGAD-ON and NMOSD-ON (area under the curve = 0.838). There was no association between pRNFL thickening and MOG-IgG titer (high vs low), body mass index, or the delay between ON onset and OCT. Simultaneous bilateral MOGAD-ON was associated with significantly more pronounced pRNFL thickening.
Acute-stage OCT contributes to the rapid and accurate differentiation between MOGAD-ON and NMOSD-ON prior to antibody confirmation, which can be critical for timely therapeutic decisions.

[Introduction] Introduction Myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (MOG) antibody–associated disease (MOGAD) is an important differential diagnosis from aquaporin-4 (AQP4) antibody–positive neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD), particularly in patients with severe optic neuritis (ON), one of the most common clinical manifestations in both diseases. 1,2 Previously, it was shown that optic disc edema occurs frequently in MOGAD, and that peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer (pRNFL) thickness, measured by optical coherence tomography (OCT), enables differentiation of acute MOGAD from multiple sclerosis–associated ON (MS-ON). 2 However, the factors influencing the extent of pRNFL thickening were not previously addressed. Moreover, OCT findings have not been compared between acute MOGAD-ON and NMOSD-ON. Although MOGAD and NMOSD are both antibody mediated, their response to therapy and outcomes are substantially different. 3-5 Previous studies have shown that for NMOSD, early initiation of plasmapheresis during the first days after onset is associated with improved outcomes. 6 By contrast, patients with MOGAD often have a good response to high-dose corticosteroids alone. 7 Decisions regarding escalation to plasmapheresis in aquaporin-4 (AQP4) IgG–positive severe ON should be made as early as possible, often before antibody testing results have returned, making this a challenging clinical dilemma. In this study, we investigated the applicability of OCT in the rapid differentiation between MOGAD and NMOSD at the acute stage of first ON and performed a detailed analysis of factors associated with pRNFL thickening in both entities.

[introduction] Introduction Myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (MOG) antibody–associated disease (MOGAD) is an important differential diagnosis from aquaporin-4 (AQP4) antibody–positive neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD), particularly in patients with severe optic neuritis (ON), one of the most common clinical manifestations in both diseases. 1,2 Previously, it was shown that optic disc edema occurs frequently in MOGAD, and that peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer (pRNFL) thickness, measured by optical coherence tomography (OCT), enables differentiation of acute MOGAD from multiple sclerosis–associated ON (MS-ON). 2 However, the factors influencing the extent of pRNFL thickening were not previously addressed. Moreover, OCT findings have not been compared between acute MOGAD-ON and NMOSD-ON. Although MOGAD and NMOSD are both antibody mediated, their response to therapy and outcomes are substantially different. 3-5 Previous studies have shown that for NMOSD, early initiation of plasmapheresis during the first days after onset is associated with improved outcomes. 6 By contrast, patients with MOGAD often have a good response to high-dose corticosteroids alone. 7 Decisions regarding escalation to plasmapheresis in aquaporin-4 (AQP4) IgG–positive severe ON should be made as early as possible, often before antibody testing results have returned, making this a challenging clinical dilemma. In this study, we investigated the applicability of OCT in the rapid differentiation between MOGAD and NMOSD at the acute stage of first ON and performed a detailed analysis of factors associated with pRNFL thickening in both entities.

[methods] Methods This multicenter, retrospective cross-sectional study included patients from 14 university centers across 8 countries. OCT examinations were performed within 14 days of ON onset in patients with MOGAD and NMOSD who fulfilled the following inclusion criteria: (1) MOGAD diagnosed according to Banwell criteria or AQP4–positive NMOSD diagnosed based on International Panel for NMO Diagnosis criteria 8,9 ; (2) first ON episode; and (3) age at ON of 18 years or older. In cases of bilateral ON, the mean value of both eyes was included in the analysis. ON was confirmed by experienced neuro-ophthalmologists based on the recent ON diagnostic criteria. 10 The exclusion criteria were concomitant ophthalmologic diseases affecting OCT measurements and other causes of ON, such as MS. OCT scans adhered to the APOSTEL recommendations and OSCAR-IB consensus criteria for quality assessment of OCT images. 11,12 Data on patients with MOGAD were partially published previously in a study differentiating MOGAD from MS using OCT. 2 Global pRNFL thickness was measured using OCT with either SPECTRALIS (Heidelberg Engineering, Germany) or Cirrus HD-OCT (Carl Zeiss Meditec, CA) and converted to the thickness value in SPECTRALIS, as previously published. 13 pRNFL thickening was defined as 2 SDs above the mean pRNFL thickness, corresponding to the previously published cutoff of 115 µm (converted from Cirrus to SPECTRALIS). 14 In patients with unilateral ON, the prevalence of pRNFL thickening ≥10 µm, compared with the unaffected contralateral eye (the amount of pRNFL thickening that is visible on ophthalmoscopic examination), was also evaluated. MOG-IgG testing was performed in the experienced laboratories of participating centers using one of the established tests: fixed cell-based assay (CBA) in 22 of 83 patients (26.5%), live CBA in 37 of 83 (44.6%), and live FACS-based assay in 24 of 83 (28.9%). Low and high MOG-IgG titers were distinguished based on the cutoffs listed in the Banwell criteria, depending on the laboratory methods used. Mean and SD were provided for continuous variables and frequency and proportion for categorical variables. Group comparisons were performed using the nonparametric Mann-Whitney U test and the χ 2 test while correlations between continuous variables were assessed using Spearman rank correlation. Statistical significance was defined as p < 0.05. Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis was conducted to evaluate differences between MOGAD and NMOSD. Youden index was used to determine optimal cutoff value. Furthermore, the positive and negative predictive values were determined. A 1-way independent-samples analysis of variance was performed to investigate time-dependent changes in pRNFL. Data were analyzed with SPSS version 29 (IBM SPSS Statistics). Standard Protocol Approvals, Registrations, and Patient Consents Written informed consent was obtained from all patients and their legal representatives participating in the study. The local ethics committees approved the study protocol in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki (1964) in its currently applicable version. Data Availability The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

[results] Results In our study, 83 patients with MOGAD (61.4% female, age at ON [mean ± SD 42.2 ± 15.6 years], 124 ON eyes) and 28 with NMOSD (85.7% female, age at ON [mean ± SD 50.2 ± 12.6 years], 36 ON eyes) with a first unilateral or bilateral ON episode were included ( Table ). Simultaneous bilateral ON occurred more frequently in patients with MOGAD than in those with NMOSD (48.2% vs 21.4%; p = 0.013). The groups were similar regarding body mass index (BMI) and the time interval between ON onset and OCT examination. Patients with MOGAD had a significantly higher mean pRNFL thickness compared with the NMOSD group (157.4 ± 63.7 µm; 101.5 ± 26.8 µm; p < 0.001). A significant increase in pRNFL thickness (>115 µm, corresponding >2 SDs), suggestive of disc edema, was observed in 73.4% of MOGAD-ON eyes and 11.1% of NMOSD-ON eyes ( p < 0.001). Among patients with unilateral ON manifestation, 76.9% of MOGAD eyes and 43.8% of NMOSD eyes showed ≥10 µm pRNFL thickening compared with the contralateral unaffected eye. Table Demographic and Main Clinical Characteristics of Patients With MOGAD and NMOSD MOGAD (n = 83) NMOSD (n = 28) p Value Age at ON in y, mean ± SD 42.2 ± 15.6 50.2 ± 12.6 0.014 Female, n (%) 51 (61.4) 24 (85.7) 0.018 Time interval ON onset and OCT examination in d, mean ± SD 9.6 ± 4.8 11.0 ± 4.1 0.522 Positive OCBs, n (%), data available for 27/54 patients 5 (6.0) 6 (21.4) 0.108 MOG-IgG high titer according to Banwell criteria, n (%) 49 (59.0) — — Simultaneous bilateral ON, n (%) 40 (48.2) 6 (21.4) 0.013 Total ON eyes, n (%) 124 (74.7) 36 (64.3) 0.133 BMI, mean ± SD, data available for 24/63 patients 29.2 ± 7.9 27.3 ± 5.3 0.491 Average pRNFL, mean ± SD (average of both eyes in bilateral ON) 157.4 ± 63.7 µm; IQR 110.0–193.5 µm 101.5 ± 26.8 µm, IQR 93.6–106.3 µm <0.001 pRNFL thickening >115 µm in ON eyes, n (%) 91 (73.4) 4 (11.1) p < 0.001 Abbreviations: BMI = body mass index; IQR = intraquartile range; MOGAD = myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody–associated disease; NMOSD = neuromyelitis optic spectrum disorder; OCBs = oligoclonal bands; ON = optic neuritis; pRNFL = peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer. What Factors Influence Disc Edema in MOGAD-ON and NMOSD-ON? In a further analysis, we focused on the factors associated with pRNFL thickening in each group ( Figure 1 ). pRNFL thickness remained comparable within 2 weeks after onset of ON in each group ( p = 0.088 in MOGAD and p = 0.185 in NMOSD). There was no association between pRNFL thickness, age, and sex in either of the groups (in MOGAD: sex p = 0.288, age p = 0.200; in NMOSD: sex p = 0.776, age p = 0.876). Patients with normal and high BMI (<25 or ≥25 kg/m 2 ) also revealed no differences in either entity (pRNFL 160.1 ± 75.4 µm vs 159.1 ± 55.9 µm; p = 0.440 in MOGAD and 99.3 ± 11.9 µm vs 112.0 ± 41.6 µm; p = 0.910 in NMOSD). In patients with MOGAD, the degree of pRNFL thickening was also similar between those with low and high MOG-IgG titers (159.5 ± 55.8 µm vs 156.0 ± 69.2 µm; p = 0.322). The laterality of ON appeared to be the only relevant factor. In the MOGAD group, simultaneous bilateral ON was associated with more pronounced pRNFL thickening compared with unilateral manifestation (194.4 ± 81.8 µm vs 155.4 ± 76.6 µm; p = 0.006). Conversely, in the NMOSD-ON group, pRNFL thickness was greater in eyes with unilateral ON than in those with bilateral ON (109.5 ± 32.3 µm vs 86.3 ± 16.7 µm; p = 0.005). Figure 1 MOGAD Subgroup Analysis pRNFL was similar between patients with high and low MOG-IgG titers (159.5 ± 55.8 µm; 156.0 ± 69.2 µm; p = 0.322 [A]), as well as those with and without obesity (BMI >25 kg/m 2 vs < 25 kg/m 2 , 160.1 ± 75.4 µm; 159.1 ± 55.9 µm; p = 0.440 [B]). Bilateral ON was associated with a more pronounced disc edema compared with unilateral ON (194.4 ± 81.8 µm; 155.4 ± 76.6 µm; p = 0.006 [C]). pRNFL thickness remained stable within 2 weeks after onset (D). BMI = body mass index; MOGAD = myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody–associated disease; pRNFL = peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer. Can OCT Differentiate Between Acute MOGAD-ON and NMOSD-ON? A ROC analysis comparing pRNFL thickness (mean pRNFL thickness for bilateral ON manifestation) in acute MOGAD-ON vs NMOSD-ON provided a cutoff of 117.5 µm, where thicker pRNFL significantly favored MOGAD-ON over NMOSD-ON (area under the curve [AUC] = 0.838, 92.9% specificity, 71.1% sensitivity) ( Figure 2 ). The positive predictive value was 96.7%, and the negative predictive value was 52.0%. Figure 2 Comparison of Patients With MOGAD and NMOSD pRNFL thickening was more severe in the MOGAD-ON group compared with the NMOSD-ON group, visualized in 2 boxplots. A cutoff of 117.5 µm enabled the distinction between both groups (ROC curve). MOGAD = myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody–associated disease; NMOSD = neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder; pRNFL = peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer; ROC = receiver operating characteristic. A subgroup analysis for unilateral ON manifestation also provided 117.5 µm as the optimal cutoff with 90.9% specificity, 65.1% sensitivity, a positive predictive value of 93.3%, and a negative predictive value of 57.1% (AUC = 0.739). Application of the previously published cutoff of 123 µm (suggested for differentiation between MOGAD-ON and MS-ON in acute stage, 2 converted from Cirrus to SPECTRALIS) showed 96.4% specificity, 63.9% sensitivity, a positive predictive value of 98.1%, and a negative predictive value of 47.4%.

[discussion] Discussion Clinical differentiation between MOGAD and NMOSD during the first ON episode is often challenging and treatment relevant. Optic disc edema is primarily characteristic of MOGAD-ON; however, its prevalence, severity, and clinical applicability, as measured by OCT, in the differentiation from NMOSD-ON have not been systematically investigated previously. The main finding of this study is that acute-stage OCT can effectively contribute to differentiation between acute MOGAD-ON and NMOSD-ON, with thicknesses greater than 117.5 µm strongly favoring MOGAD-ON. The applicability of this cutoff was also confirmed in cases of unilateral ON manifestation. An intereye pRNFL thickness difference of 10 µm or more, detectable on fundoscopy, was less informative, being observed in 77% of unilateral MOGAD-ON cases and also in 44% of NMOSD-ON cases. Of interest, a prior study by Chen et al. 2 found a comparable cutoff (118 µm for OCT by Zeiss, converted to 123 µm for OCT by Heidelberg Engineering) when comparing acute MOGAD-ON and MS-ON, with similar accuracy. Using this cutoff in our study yielded a specificity of 96% for MOGAD-ON. In daily clinical practice, this universal cutoff can reliably confirm acute MOGAD-ON and distinguish it from both other main causes of ON, NMOSD and MS. However, owing to the lower negative predictive value, the use of OCT in differentiating MOGAD-ON and NMOSD-ON primarily applies in the presence of at least mild pRNFL thickening, suggestive of optic disc edema. Further research is needed to elucidate OCT parameters capable of distinguishing acute NMOSD-ON from MOGAD-ON without optic disc edema. Previous studies have shown that early plasmapheresis is associated with improved outcomes in severe ON, especially in NMOSD. 3,6,15-18 Therefore, there is a relatively strong consensus that severe NMOSD-ON attacks should be treated concurrently with early corticosteroids and plasmapheresis. By contrast, high-dose corticosteroids often lead to a complete or almost-complete improvement of the high-contrast visual acuity in MOGAD despite similar severe nadir of vision loss during the acute stage. 4,7,19-21 A recent study demonstrated a more favorable outcome with a shorter delay to escalation on plasmapheresis in a selected cohort of patients with MOGAD-ON; however, no systematic comparative data on IV pulse methylprednisolone and early plasmapheresis are available. 22 Most of the MOGAD attacks are treated initially with corticosteroids alone, and plasmapheresis is reserved for attacks that do not respond. Therefore, OCT during an acute ON attack should be considered as a valuable additional diagnostic tool to help differentiate MOGAD from NMOSD and guide the decision for early plasmapheresis in severe and/or bilateral ON while awaiting antibody results. Because the return of antibody test results may require up to 7–10 days, escalation to plasmapheresis in suspected NMOSD should be considered in advance of result availability. Marked pRNFL thickening would suggest MOGAD with a substantially more favorable visual outcome and favor prolongation of steroids rather than early escalation to plasmapheresis during the first days of onset. 6,23 In a subgroup analysis investigating variables that may influence pRNFL thickness in acute MOGAD-ON, a potential overlap with MS-ON in patients with low MOG-IgG titers was refuted because all patients fulfilled the Banwell 8 criteria and there was no difference in pRNFL thickness between the subgroups with low and high MOG-IgG titers. Increased CSF pressure has been repeatedly reported in MOGAD; however, its association with papilledema in MOGAD-ON remains unclear. 24 Increased BMI, a well-known risk factor of intracranial hypertension, revealed no association with pRNFL thickness in either MOGAD or NMOSD. An acute inflammation, locally affecting the orbital region of the optic nerve, may explain the increased disc edema in MOGAD-ON. 25 Of interest, among the variables analyzed, only bilateral ON manifestation was associated with more severe disc edema compared with unilateral manifestation in MOGAD-ON. By contrast, NMOSD-ON showed more pRNFL thickening in cases of unilateral manifestation, which may be explained by a more proximal optic nerve inflammation in contrast to distal perichiasmal inflammation in bilateral ON manifestation. Further studies are needed to analyze the impact of the extent and localization of optic nerve inflammation on pRNFL thickening in both diseases. Certain limitations should be taken into account when interpreting these results. Owing to its retrospective nature, reporting biases regarding ON and disease history cannot be excluded, as patients were selected by academic centers. Although an increased CSF pressure has been reported in MOGAD, no systematic data were available in our cohort. The accuracy of OCT in differentiating MOGAD-ON from NMOSD-ON in patients with only very mild pRNFL thickening is relatively low. Differences in age and sex between NMOSD and MOGAD groups are attributable to the rarity of both diseases and their characteristics. OCT should be considered as a relevant acute-stage diagnostic tool in patients with first ON, helpful in the rapid differentiation between MOGAD-ON and NMOSD-ON, particularly in patients with pRNFL thickening. This diagnostic approach is especially relevant in severe and/or bilateral ON, characteristic of NMOSD and MOGAD, because immediate escalation to plasmapheresis is critical in NMOSD-ON and may be warranted before antibody results are available.

retrieved_papers.title: Retinal and Optic Nerve Integrity After Endoscopic Transorbital Skull Base Surgery Assessed by Optical Coherence Tomography.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] The endoscopic transorbital approach (ETOA) provides minimally invasive access to the skull base, but its orbital trajectory raises concern for subclinical optic nerve or retinal injury. We assessed the structural safety of ETOA using high-resolution optical coherence tomography as an objective neuro-ophthalmic tool, focused mainly on patients without orbital involvement.
From 41 consecutive ETOA cases (2017-2024), 16 patients had analyzable baseline and approximately 12-month postoperative imaging and were included: a Group A without orbital involvement (n = 10) and a Group B with orbital or optic nerve involvement but interpretable scans (n = 6). Macular ganglion cell layer (GCL) and peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) thickness were measured with spectral-domain optical coherence tomography. Paired analyses were prespecified for the Group A; the Group B was summarized descriptively. The contralateral eye served as an internal control.
Across all 16 patients, no structural or functional deterioration attributable to surgery was observed. In the Group A, GCL thickness in the operated eye decreased slightly from 81.0 µm to 80.5 µm (mean change -0.5 µm), as well as in the contralateral eye used as control (-0.2 µm). RNFL thickness in the operated eye increased from 96.4 µm to 97.3 µm (mean change +0.9 µm), while the contralateral eye decreased (-1.2 µm). These changes were not statistically significant and remained within physiological variability (±2 µm for GCL; ±3 µm for RNFL). Visual acuity, visual fields, color vision, and pupillary responses were preserved, with no signs of functional impairment. In the Group B, the operated eye showed RNFL thinning (≈-7.7 µm), while GCL remained stable. This was attributed to preexisting optic neuropathy or resolution of preoperative disc edema rather than surgical injury. Importantly, functional outcomes (visual acuity, visual fields, color vision, and pupillary responses) improved or remained stable.
ETOA does not induce subclinical retinal or optic nerve injury at late follow-up. The approach demonstrated structural and functional safety, particularly in patients without orbital involvement (Group A), where preservation of visual integrity is imperative. These findings support the neuro-ophthalmic safety of the transorbital corridor and justify larger prospective studies.

retrieved_papers.title: Peripapillary Retinoschisis: The Expanded Spectrum and New Insights From Multimodal Imaging.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] To characterize and classify different forms of peripapillary retinoschisis (PPRS), and to clarify the nomenclature of this condition.
A retrospective, multicenter, multinational case series of PPRS was performed from August 2021 to September 2024. Cases were included if they demonstrated retinoschisis contiguous with and thought to be originating from the optic disc. Demographic and clinical data collected included age, gender, visual acuity, intraocular pressure, axial length, refraction and referring symptoms. Mandatory investigations included optical coherence tomography of the optic disc and macula with radial scans, color fundus photography, and fundus autofluorescence. Select cases underwent fundus fluorescein and/or indocyanine green angiography. Retinoschisis was characterized by the meridian in relation to the optic disc, layer(s) of the retina affected and associated conditions. A literature review was performed to identify all causes of PPRS.
A total of 47 eyes from 41 patients with PPRS were identified, comprising of 22 (54%) females and a mean age of 56 years (range 14-92 years). These were classified into 9 aetiologies: Congenital Disc Abnormalities (CDA, n = 16 eyes), peripapillary chorioretinal coloboma (n = 1), peripapillary atrophy (n = 3), glaucoma (n = 7), Peripapillary Pachychoroid Syndrome (PPS, n = 4), peripapillary choroidal neovascularization (PP-CNV, n = 3), high/ pathological myopia (n = 5), vitreopapillary traction (VPT, n = 4) and idiopathic (n = 4).
The 9 aetiologies of peripapillary retinoschisis can be classified into 5 groups: non-glaucomatous optic disc abnormalities (CDA, PP-coloboma, PP-atrophy), glaucomatous optic disc abnormalities, peripapillary choroidal diseases (PPS and PP-CNV), vitreous optic disc interface abnormalities and idiopathic. A new entity, "Focal Optic Disc Dome" (FODD) was identified. Understanding the full spectrum of PPRS can assist in correct diagnosis and management.

retrieved_papers.title: Evaluation of optic disc and parafoveal parameters in Neuro-Behçet patients without ocular involvement using optical coherence tomography angiography.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] The study aims to examine vascular changes in the optic disc and macular region using Optical Coherence Tomography Angiography (OCTA) in patients with Neuro-Behçet's disease without ocular involvement.
36 eyes of 19 parenchymal Neurobehçet patients without ocular pathology were included in the Neurobehçet group (NBG). As controls, 44 eyes of 22 nonocular Mucocutaneous Behçet's disease patients (MBG) and 51 eyes of 26 healthy individuals (HG) matched by age and gender were included. Full ophthalmological examination and OCTA measurements were performed in all groups.
In the comparison of mean peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer thickness, NBG had the lowest value, while no significant difference was observed between groups. In the analysis of peripapillary vascular density (VD), mean VD in the inferior quadrant was significantly lower in NBG than in the other two groups (p = 0.014). In the foveal region, both superficial capillary plexus and deep capillary plexus VD means were significantly lower in NBG compared to MBG (p = 0.019 and p = 0.02, respectively). Additionally, NBG had significantly wider foveal avascular zone compared to MBG (p = 0.035). In the assessment of choriocapillaris flow rate, mean values for NBG in both the 1 mm and 6 mm diameter areas were significantly lower than those in other groups (p = 0.002, p = 0.000, respectively).
The ability of OCTA to demonstrate changes in retinal and choroidal blood flow in Neuro-Behçet patients without ophthalmological symptoms or findings suggests the presence of subclinical effects and indicates the potential utility of OCTA in monitoring these patients.

retrieved_papers.title: Analysis of colour defects and microvascular abnormalities in dysthyroid optic neuropathy using optical coherence tomography angiography.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] To evaluate the correlation between colour vision deficiency and microvascular abnormalities in patients with dysthyroid optic neuropathy (DON).
Patients with DON and healthy controls were enrolled. HRR colour examination and OCTA device were used for all the subjects. The vessel density (VD), optic disc-associated capillary density (CD), foveal avascular zone (FAZ) area, ganglion cell complex (GCC) and retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) were compared between the DON and normal groups. In addition, the correlation between the OCTA parameters and the Hardy-Rand-Rittler (HRR) score was also assessed.
One hundred and fourteen eyes of 60 patients (30 patients with DON and 30 normal controls) were included in our study. The HRR score, RNFL, FAZ and parafoveal vessel density were lower in DON patients than in healthy controls (p < 0.05). We detected significant correlations between the HRR score and the radial capillary density (RPC), superficial capillary plexus (SCP), deep capillary plexus (DCP) and RNFL. The HRR test plays an important role in the diagnosis of DON. Protan deficiency was the major type of colour deficiency in DON according to the HRR test.
In terms of pathogenesis, colour vision defects may be correlated with changes in retinal blood flow in patients with DON, which could be early signs for the diagnosis of DON.

retrieved_papers.title: Deep Learning Models for the Screening of Cognitive Impairment Using Multimodal Fundus Images.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2024]
[Abstract] We aimed to develop a deep learning system capable of identifying subjects with cognitive impairment quickly and easily based on multimodal ocular images.
Cross sectional study.
Participants of Beijing Eye Study 2011 and patients attending Beijing Tongren Eye Center and Beijing Tongren Hospital Physical Examination Center.
We trained and validated a deep learning algorithm to assess cognitive impairment using retrospectively collected data from the Beijing Eye Study 2011. Cognitive impairment was defined as a Mini-Mental State Examination score < 24. Based on fundus photographs and OCT images, we developed 5 models based on the following sets of images: macula-centered fundus photographs, optic disc-centered fundus photographs, fundus photographs of both fields, OCT images, and fundus photographs of both fields with OCT (multimodal). The performance of the models was evaluated and compared in an external validation data set, which was collected from patients attending Beijing Tongren Eye Center and Beijing Tongren Hospital Physical Examination Center.
Area under the curve (AUC).
A total of 9424 retinal photographs and 4712 OCT images were used to develop the model. The external validation sets from each center included 1180 fundus photographs and 590 OCT images. Model comparison revealed that the multimodal performed best, achieving an AUC of 0.820 in the internal validation set, 0.786 in external validation set 1, and 0.784 in external validation set 2. We evaluated the performance of the multi-model in different sexes and different age groups; there were no significant differences. The heatmap analysis showed that signals around the optic disc in fundus photographs and the retina and choroid around the macular and optic disc regions in OCT images were used by the multimodal to identify participants with cognitive impairment.
Fundus photographs and OCT can provide valuable information on cognitive function. Multimodal models provide richer information compared with single-mode models. Deep learning algorithms based on multimodal retinal images may be capable of screening cognitive impairment. This technique has potential value for broader implementation in community-based screening or clinic settings.
Proprietary or commercial disclosure may be found in the Footnotes and Disclosures at the end of this article.

[methods] Methods Study Population In this retrospective study, we investigated the use of ultra-widefield retinal images (Optos PLC) and B-scan US (ABSolu, Lumibird Medical) for the classification of UM and choroidal nevi. The study was approved by the Institutional Review Board of the University of Illinois Chicago and conducted in accordance with the Declaration of Helsinki. This was a retrospective study, so informed consent was waived. A total of 174 patients were included, comprising 93 cases of UM and 81 cases of choroidal nevi. All patients were evaluated and diagnosed at the University of Illinois Chicago Eye Clinic. Diagnoses were established clinically by a fellowship-trained ocular oncologist based on ophthalmoscopy, ultrasonography, ultra-widefield fundus photography, fundus autofluorescence, OCT, and fluorescein angiography at the clinician’s discretion. When available, histopathology from enucleation specimens and cytology or next-generation sequencing from biopsies were also used. Indeterminate lesions were monitored longitudinally, and those that grew or required treatment were reclassified as melanoma. No minimum follow-up was imposed because the purpose of this study was to evaluate the model as a potential tool for triaging at initial presentation rather than to establish a definitive diagnosis superior to that of an ocular oncology specialist. For each patient, both ultra-widefield retinal and ultrasound images were collected. Only 1 eye per patient was included, ensuring that each case represented an independent patient. The ultrasound images were taken both in the transverse and longitudinal orientations to account for variations in tumor dimensions and to capture a complete structural profile of each lesion. Patients who had undergone prior treatment before imaging were excluded from the study. Deep Learning Architecture and Training Procedure This study utilized EfficientNetV2-S as the base CNN architecture, leveraging pretrained ImageNet weights for transfer learning. 28 This approach was adopted to address the challenges associated with a limited dataset, allowing the model to benefit from prior knowledge learned on large-scale image data. EfficientNetV2-S is a lightweight yet high-performing CNN that improves both accuracy and training efficiency through optimized architecture design. 29 The EfficientNetV2-S model functioned as a feature extractor, with the extracted features subsequently processed through 2 fully connected dense layers for classification. The model architecture used in this study is shown in Figure 1 . Figure 1 Architecture of EfficientNetV2-S. All deep learning implementations were carried out using Keras API with TensorFlow backend. The training was performed on a Windows 11 workstation (Microsoft) equipped with an NVIDIA RTX A6000 graphic processing unit. Data augmentation techniques such as horizontal and vertical flips and rotations were applied to improve model robustness, enhance generalizability, and mitigate the risk of overfitting. These augmentations served to diversify the training data, enabling the model to learn diverse spatial and structural variations. The training procedure employed the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.0001 and a batch size of 32. We employed a checkpoint strategy to mitigate overfitting to save the model’s weights at its peak performance, marked by the lowest validation loss. A fivefold cross validation procedure was conducted to ensure robust and reliable evaluation and performance assessment. For each fold, 80% of the images were used for training, whereas 20% were reserved for validation. The same fold partitions were applied consistently across all single-modality and fusion training. Thus, each case appeared in the validation set exactly once and never in the training set for that iteration, eliminating the risk of data leakage. After training each fold, the model and its trained weights were discarded, and the model was reinitialized with the pretrained ImageNet weights before training on the next fold. This iterative process provided a statistically rigorous assessment by averaging performance metrics across all 5 folds. Multimodal Fusion Strategies Each imaging modality (fundus photographs and longitudinal and transverse ultrasound images) was trained independently to establish baseline classification performance before implementing multimodal fusion strategies ( Fig 2 A–C). To effectively integrate information from the fundus photographs, longitudinal ultrasound images, and transverse ultrasound images, 2 distinct fusion strategies were implemented: prediction averaging and feature-level fusion with convolutional block attention modules (CBAMs), as illustrated in Figure 2 . In the averaging approach ( Fig 2 D), classification probabilities from each independently trained single-modality model were averaged to generate the final classification output. This method helped to reduce modality-specific biases and improve classification stability by leveraging consensus across models. In the feature-level fusion approach ( Fig 2 E), feature maps extracted from each modality were first refined using CBAMs before concatenation. The CBAM consists of 2 sequential attention mechanisms: the channel attention module, which selectively enhances diagnostically relevant feature channels, and the spatial attention module, which focuses on critical spatial regions within each modality. The architecture of the CBAM is shown in Figure 2 F. By applying these attention mechanisms to the individual feature maps before concatenation, the model ensured that only the most relevant multimodal information was retained. The refined feature representations were then concatenated and passed through fully connected dense layers for final classification. Figure 2 Fusion strategies. (A) Fundus photographs only. (B) Longitudinal ultrasound (LUS) only. (C) Transverse ultrasound (TUS) only. (D) Prediction averaging strategy. (E) Attention-based feature fusion strategy. (F) Convolutional block attention mechanism. CAM = channel attention module; SAM = spatial attention module. Model Performance Evaluation A fivefold cross validation strategy was implemented to ensure a rigorous assessment of model performance. This approach allowed for a more reliable evaluation by reducing bias and ensuring that performance metrics were not dependent on a single dataset split. The final results were obtained by averaging the performance across all 5 folds, providing a more comprehensive assessment of classification effectiveness. Model performance was evaluated using 3 key metrics: F1 score, accuracy, and the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC). The F1 score was selected because it accounts for both precision and recall, offering a balanced measure of classification performance, particularly in cases of class imbalance. Accuracy was used to quantify the proportion of correctly classified instances relative to the total number of samples, serving as an overall measure of model correctness. The AUC provided insight into the model’s ability to distinguish between UM and choroidal nevi across different classification thresholds, with higher values indicating improved discrimination between the 2 classes.

[results] Results A total of 174 patients diagnosed with either UM or choroidal nevi were included in this study. The mean age was not significantly different between the 2 groups. White patients represented the majority in both groups (80% in UM and 73% in choroidal nevi), consistent with the known epidemiologic patterns of these conditions. The clinical and demographic characteristics of the patients are summarized in Table 1 . The classification performance of the single-modality and multimodal models is summarized in Table 2 . Among the individual modalities, the model trained on transverse ultrasound images achieved the highest performance, with an average accuracy of 92%, an F1-score of 0.9227, and an AUC of 0.9538. Multimodal fusion strategies were observed to have improved classification performance compared with individual imaging modalities. The attention-based fusion yielded the best results overall, with an accuracy of 94%, an F1-score of 0.9445, and an AUC of 0.9606. The prediction averaging approach achieved slightly lower performance, with an accuracy of 91%, an F1-score of 0.9125, and an AUC of 0.9594. Table 1 Demographic and Clinical Characteristics of Study Participants Characteristic Uveal Melanoma (N = 93) Choroidal Nevus (N = 81) Age, mean (SD), yrs 65.8 (12.6) 65.2 (15.4) Sex, No. (%) Male 52 (56) 25 (31) Female 41 (44) 56 (69) Race, No. (%) White 74 (80) 60 (74) Black or African American 1 (1) 1 (1) Asian 1 (1) 0 (0) Other 17 (18) 20 (25) Clinical risk factors Thickness, mean (SD), mm 5.98 (3.08) 1.75 (0.7) Basal diameter, mean (SD), mm 12.59 (3.20) 6.45 (3.16) Subretinal fluid, No. (%) 72 (80) 12 (15) Orange pigment, No. (%) 28 (31) 7 (9) Drusen, No. (%) 28 (31) 46 (60) Halo, No. (%) 0 (0) 4 (5) Ultrasound hollowness, No. (%) 76 (84) 26 (36) Mushroom shape, No, (%) 18 (20) 0 (0) AJCC T-category, No. (%) T1 13 (14) 76 (94) T2 45 (49) 5 (6) T3 27 (29) 0 (0) T4 7 (8) 0 (0) AJCC = American Joint Committee on Cancer; SD = standard deviation. Table 2 Cross-Validation Evaluation Metrics Strategy Accuracy (%) F1-Score AUC Longitudinal ultrasound only 90 ± 1.63 0.9042 ± 0.0196 0.9323 ± 0.0436 Transverse ultrasound only 92 ± 4.53 0.9227 ± 0.0459 0.9538 ± 0.0308 Fundus photographs only 87 ± 0.81 0.8722 ± 0.0043 0.8937 ± 0.0214 Prediction averaging 91 ± 0.03 0.9125 ± 0.0259 0.9594 ± 0.0297 Attention-based fusion 94 ± 2.93 0.9445 ± 0.0295 0.9606 ± 0.0318 AUC = area under the receiver operating characteristic curve. Figure 3 presents the confusion matrices for each model generated by aggregating predictions across the 5 folds. The model trained on transverse ultrasound images demonstrated the highest classification accuracy among the single-modality models, correctly identifying 85 of 93 UM cases and 75 of 81 choroidal nevi cases. The model trained on longitudinal ultrasound images showed slightly lower overall performance, correctly identifying 81 UM cases and 76 nevi cases. The model trained on fundus photographs only exhibited the weakest performance, misclassifying 18 UM cases and 4 choroidal nevi cases. The prediction averaging strategy showed improved performance compared with the models trained on the fundus photographs and longitudinal ultrasound images, correctly identifying 83 UM and 75 nevi, but its overall accuracy was lower than that of the model trained on transverse ultrasound images and the attention-based model. The attention-based fusion model achieved the highest overall performance, correctly classifying 86 of 93 UM cases and 78 of 81 choroidal nevi cases, with the fewest false positives and false negatives observed across all configurations. Figure 3 Confusion matrices of the various models. (A) Fundus photographs only. (B) Longitudinal ultrasound only. (C) Transverse ultrasound only. (D) Prediction averaging strategy. (E) Attention-based feature fusion strategy. UM = uveal melanoma. To investigate the limitations of the averaging strategy, we reviewed cases in which the final classification was incorrect despite ≥1 individual input models generating correct predictions. As illustrated in Table 3 , there were instances where the averaged probability shifted the final output toward the wrong class. In 4 of 5 examples, the ground truth was choroidal nevus (class 1), but the averaged probabilities fell below the decision threshold of 0.5, resulting in incorrect classification as UM (class 0). In cases 1 and 2, the model trained on fundus photographs correctly predicted the true class with probabilities of 0.65 and 0.72, whereas the model trained on longitudinal ultrasound images produced confidently incorrect predictions (0.14 and 0.21), and the model trained on transverse ultrasound images yielded borderline incorrect predictions (0.43 and 0.33). These lower values from the models trained on ultrasound images disproportionately influenced the final prediction. In case 3, the model trained on transverse ultrasound images correctly predicted nevus with a probability of 0.68, but the models trained on fundus photographs and longitudinal ultrasound images predicted UM with probabilities of 0.21 and 0.49, respectively, lowering the average to 0.46 and resulting in misclassification. In case 4, the models trained on longitudinal ultrasound images and transverse ultrasound images predicted the correct class with values above 0.5, but the model trained on fundus photographs assigned a confidently incorrect prediction (0.24), which suppressed the final average. In case 5, where the true label was UM, all 3 models produced borderline outputs, but the model trained on transverse ultrasound images yielded a relatively confident prediction for nevus (0.72), raising the average to 0.50, resulting in a wrong classification. Table 3 Impact of Averaging on Final Classification Decision Case Fundus Photograph Longitudinal Ultrasound Transverse Ultrasound Average Final Classification Ground Truth 1 0.6550 0.1371 0.4281 0.4067 0 1 2 0.7156 0.2079 0.3263 0.4166 0 1 3 0.2059 0.4887 0.6831 0.4592 0 1 4 0.2435 0.5587 0.6233 0.4752 0 1 5 0.3189 0.4581 0.7242 0.5004 1 0

[discussion] Discussion In this study, we developed a deep learning–based classification model that integrates ultra-widefield fundus photographs and B-scan ultrasound images for objective classification of UM and choroidal nevi. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study to combine these modalities for automated UM classification using a deep learning approach. We first evaluated each modality independently to quantify their individual contributions to classification performance before implementing multimodal fusion. The model trained on transverse ultrasound images achieved the highest performance among the single-input models, followed by the model trained on longitudinal ultrasound images and the model trained on fundus photographs. Both models trained on ultrasound images outperformed the model trained on fundus photographs across all evaluation metrics, highlighting the diagnostic value of depth-resolved features, such as thickness and internal reflectivity, which are not captured in fundus photography. This finding aligns with the study by Iddir et al, who developed machine learning models to classify high-risk versus low-risk choroidal nevi using features derived independently from fundus photographs and ultrasound scans. 30 Their results demonstrated that ultrasound-based classifiers consistently outperformed those based on fundus features, reinforcing the importance of depth-resolved biomarkers in risk stratification. However, their models did not integrate multimodal information, limiting their ability to capture complementary diagnostic information from fundus photographs and ultrasound. Although fundus photography provides high-resolution views of surface characteristics, its limited capacity to represent three-dimensional structure likely contributed to its lower performance in our study and the study by Iddir et al. 30 The model trained on fundus photographs in our study performed below some recently reported fundus photography-based models, likely reflecting the smaller sample size of our single-center dataset, whereas larger cohorts have reported relatively strong diagnostic accuracy with models trained on fundus photographs only. 21 , 22 , 23 , 31 Nevertheless, fundus photographs fail to capture essential volumetric and acoustic characteristics that inform clinical diagnosis. Our findings indicate that incorporating ultrasound images alongside fundus photographs provides complementary information and more closely mirrors the multimodal evaluation that is standard in clinical practice. To determine the impact of combining fundus photographs and ultrasound images on classification performance, we implemented 2 fusion strategies. We first implemented prediction averaging, a commonly used strategy in multimodal learning and ensemble learning, 32 , 33 , 34 in which the final output is derived by averaging the predicted probabilities from the independently trained single-modality models. This approach outperformed the models trained on fundus photographs only and longitudinal ultrasound images only across all evaluation metrics and showed improved AUC compared with the model trained on transverse ultrasound images only. However, its overall performance remained lower than the model trained on transverse ultrasound images only, in terms of accuracy and F1 score, indicating that simple averaging may not fully leverage the complementary information available across the modalities. As shown in Table 3 , averaging occasionally led to misclassification even when ≥1 modalities produced correct predictions. These cases illustrate a key limitation of the averaging strategy. Confident incorrect predictions can dominate the fusion process and override correct outputs from other modalities, particularly in borderline cases. This highlights the need for adaptive fusion strategies to selectively weight modality contributions based on their relative reliability. To overcome the limitations of prediction averaging, we implemented an attention-based fusion strategy that adaptively integrates features across the fundus photographs and ultrasound images. Compared to the averaging strategy, accuracy increased from 91% to 94%, the F1 score from 0.9125 to 0.9445, and the AUC from 0.9594 to 0.9606. This performance gain reflects the ability of the attention mechanism to assign greater weight to features that are more informative for classification while suppressing less relevant or misleading signals. By enabling a more effective combination of complementary information from each modality, the attention-based model demonstrates the potential for improving the reliability of multimodal deep learning approaches in UM classification. The integration of multimodal imaging using deep learning offers a promising adjunct to clinical decision-making in the diagnosis of UM. A reliable, automated model capable of synthesizing information from multimodal imaging may assist clinicians by providing consistent, objective support in cases with ambiguous or overlapping characteristics. Such a system could reduce diagnostic variability, particularly in nonspecialist settings, and potentially minimize unnecessary referrals, invasive procedures, or delays in treatment. This study has several limitations that should be acknowledged. First, the dataset was derived retrospectively from a single tertiary center, which may limit the generalizability of the findings to broader populations. Second, the dataset size was limited, which represents a constraint on the generalizability of the findings. Also, the ground truth was based on clinical diagnosis by an ocular oncology specialist without mandatory long-term follow-up, which may limit diagnostic certainty and should be addressed in future studies with extended observation. Another limitation is that melanomas in this study were, on average, larger and thicker than nevi, which likely facilitated the model’s classification performance. Although this size disparity partly explains the robust results, the true clinical value of AI lies in distinguishing melanomas from nevi of comparable dimensions, where diagnostic uncertainty is greatest. Future studies should therefore focus on datasets enriched with size-matched lesions to rigorously test the ability of multimodal deep learning to differentiate subtle cases. Lastly, although this work focused on fundus photographs and B-scan ultrasonography, other imaging modalities such as OCT or autofluorescence may provide additional diagnostic value and were not included. Future research should aim to validate these findings in multicenter datasets with greater demographic and imaging diversity. Prospective studies are also needed to assess the applicability of the model in clinical settings. Additionally, integrating other data sources such as OCT, clinical history, or genomic markers may improve classification performance and facilitate personalized risk stratification.

[conclusion] Conclusions Multimodal deep learning that combines fundus photography and ultrasound imaging improves the classification of UM and choroidal nevi. This approach demonstrates feasibility for leveraging the strengths of each modality for automated classification of UM and choroidal nevi.

retrieved_papers.title: Anti-MOG (Myelin Oligodendrocyte Glycoprotein)-Positive Severe Optic Neuritis with Optic Disc Ischaemia and Macular Star.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2015]
[Abstract] A 44-year-old man presented with severe right visual loss. The right fundus examination showed marked optic disc oedema associated with partial macular star. Serological blood tests for infectious agents were all negative. Serum aquaporin-4 antibody was negative but anti-MOG (myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein) was positive. Magnetic resonance revealed extensive lesion in right optic nerve. There was no visual improvement after intravenous therapy. Patient had no further attacks after follow-up. Optic disc oedema with macular star is found in several infectious and non-inflammatory disorders, but it has not been reported in optic neuritis (ON) associated with autoantibodies to myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (anti-MOG).

retrieved_papers.title: AI-based prediction of best-corrected visual acuity in patients with multiple retinal diseases using multimodal medical imaging.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] This study evaluated the performance of artificial intelligence (AI) algorithms in predicting best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) for patients with multiple retinal diseases, using multimodal medical imaging including macular optical coherence tomography (OCT), optic disc OCT and fundus images. The goal was to enhance clinical BCVA evaluation efficiency and precision.
A retrospective study used data from 2545 patients (4028 eyes) for training, 896 (1006 eyes) for testing and 196 (200 eyes) for internal validation, with an external prospective dataset of 741 patients (1381 eyes). Single-modality analyses employed different backbone networks and feature fusion methods, while multimodal fusion combined modalities using average aggregation, concatenation/reduction and maximum feature selection. Predictive accuracy was measured by mean absolute error (MAE), root mean squared error (RMSE) and R² score.
Macular OCT achieved better single-modality prediction than optic disc OCT, with MAE of 3.851 vs 4.977 and RMSE of 7.844 vs 10.026. Fundus images showed an MAE of 3.795 and RMSE of 7.954. Multimodal fusion significantly improved accuracy, with the best results using average aggregation, achieving an MAE of 2.865, RMSE of 6.229 and R² of 0.935. External validation yielded an MAE of 8.38 and RMSE of 10.62.
Multimodal fusion provided the most accurate BCVA predictions, demonstrating AI's potential to improve clinical evaluation. However, challenges remain regarding disease diversity and applicability in resource-limited settings.

retrieved_papers.title: Managing Hot Flushes in Menopausal Women: A Review.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2018]
[Abstract] Hot flushes during menopause are distressing for women and result in poor quality of life. Purpose of the current review was to evaluate the available treatment modalities that should be utilised for the management of hot flushes. Menopause refers to last menses of women life and can be declared after amenorrhea of 12 months. Vasomotor symptoms including hot flushes and night sweats are common after menopause, affecting almost 50 - 85% women older than 45 years. The mean increment in core body and skin temperature is 0.5°C and 0.25 - 3°C during a hot flush attack. Low level of estrogen during menopause and its association in triggering episodes of hot flushes, is still under debate. The most accepted hypothesis is a narrowing of the thermoneutral zone (TNZ) triggered by estrogen fluctuations. Although, hormone replacement therapy (HRT) remains the standard treatment for the alleviation of such symptoms, incidence of life threatening side effects restrained medical professionals from its use. Complications associated with the use of HRT can be avoided by appropriate evaluation of patients before initiating therapy. Several guidelines have also recommended HRT (estrogen and progesterone) to be safe for up to a period of seven years. Both hormonal and non-hormonal treatments are used for the management of hot flushes. Since hot flushes are the least appreciated and neglected complication of menopause, current review provides detailed information on its background, pathophysiology and management, and emphasises the need of its treatment.

[Introduction] Introduction Menopause is the biological process in which a woman's monthly menstrual cycle permanently stops for at least 12 consecutive months [ 1 ]. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), premenopausal women are individuals who have experienced episodes of irregular bleeding within the last year [ 1 ]. Perimenopausal women have experienced either irregular menstruation in the past year or cessation of menstruation for more than three months but less than one year. The onset of menopause exhibits variability among different populations and is subject to the effect of both hereditary and environmental variables. The commonly accepted age range is between 45-55 years, and the occurrence of menopause prior to the age of 40 is referred to as premature menopause [ 1 ]. Menopause is a significant stage in a woman's life that is linked to a range of health issues, including as vasomotor, sexual, and psychological symptoms. These symptoms can have a detrimental effect on both the quality of life and productivity [ 2 ]. The most common menopausal symptoms among women are hot flashes (87.4%), night sweats (66.6%), and sleeplessness (60.1%) [ 3 ]. These symptoms can cause significant distress to many persons. Hormonal replacement therapy (HRT) entails the administration of hormones that are normally depleted during the menopausal phase. Hormone replacement therapy involves the administration of estrogen to alleviate symptoms associated with menopause. If the uterus is still present, progesterone is also added to provide protection for the uterine lining. Routine addition or recommendation of progesterone is not necessary if the uterus is not intact. Administering these medications can effectively diminish or eradicate a wide range of menopausal symptoms, thereby enhancing the overall quality of life [ 3 ]. A previous study in Malaysia and other Asian nations, reported misinterpretations or false beliefs of menopause among women [ 4 ]. In Asian culture, menopause is commonly regarded as a natural consequence of aging, associated with social status and wisdom within the community. Research conducted on women from Asia and Europe has revealed that younger women generally exhibit more favorable perception in comparison to older women who have undergone menopause [ 4 ]. Thai women view menopause as a routine, natural biological process that does not necessitate intervention, but certain Pakistani women regard it as a medical ailment that necessitates medical attention [ 4 ]. Healthcare professionals from all fields (except pediatrics) come across patients experiencing menopause. Nevertheless, their understanding and implementation of menopausal hormone therapy (MHT) still varies [ 5 ]. A study in Australia assessed the knowledge and attitudes of Australian health professionals, including general practitioners, gynecologists, and endocrinologists, on HRT. The study found that only 52% of the participants had a sufficient level of knowledge about non-hormonal therapy, while 72% had a good understanding of menopausal physiology or HRT. Moreover, there were no significant discrepancies in knowledge detected among various disciplines. However, 91% of health providers expressed their willingness to offer HRT to women who are going through menopausal symptoms. Members of menopause societies demonstrated a higher inclination to endorse HRT for prolonged durations. The main obstacle identified in prescription HRT was the concern expressed by clients regarding the possibility of acquiring breast cancer [ 5 ]. According to a survey conducted in 2019 among medical residents specializing in family medicine, internal medicine, and obstetrics and gynecology, only 6.8% of the participants expressed confidence in their ability to effectively handle the medical needs of women going through menopause [ 6 ]. A further study conducted in China involving healthcare professionals uncovered a deficiency in their comprehension of the core principles behind HRT, since nearly half of them were incapable of correctly identifying its contraindications. Enhancing clinicians' expertise and education in the management of menopause is essential for providing complete, evidence-based care. Moreover, the study revealed that receiving training on menopause care has a substantial impact on physicians' attitudes, behaviors, and confidence in identifying menopausal symptoms and offering suitable suggestions for symptom management [ 7 ]. While many physicians acknowledge the necessity of education on menopausal therapy, there exist notable gaps in knowledge that require attention. Ensuring the provision of comprehensive, evidence-based menopausal care necessitates investing in continuous education and training for physicians. Hence, it is crucial to evaluate attitudes, practices, and barriers of physicians in Saudi Arabia, regarding menopausal HRT.

[introduction] Introduction Menopause is the biological process in which a woman's monthly menstrual cycle permanently stops for at least 12 consecutive months [ 1 ]. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), premenopausal women are individuals who have experienced episodes of irregular bleeding within the last year [ 1 ]. Perimenopausal women have experienced either irregular menstruation in the past year or cessation of menstruation for more than three months but less than one year. The onset of menopause exhibits variability among different populations and is subject to the effect of both hereditary and environmental variables. The commonly accepted age range is between 45-55 years, and the occurrence of menopause prior to the age of 40 is referred to as premature menopause [ 1 ]. Menopause is a significant stage in a woman's life that is linked to a range of health issues, including as vasomotor, sexual, and psychological symptoms. Hormonal replacement therapy (HRT) entails the administration of hormones that are normally depleted during the menopausal phase. Hormone replacement therapy involves the administration of estrogen to alleviate symptoms associated with menopause. Routine addition or recommendation of progesterone is not necessary if the uterus is not intact. Administering these medications can effectively diminish or eradicate a wide range of menopausal symptoms, thereby enhancing the overall quality of life [ 3 ]. A previous study in Malaysia and other Asian nations, reported misinterpretations or false beliefs of menopause among women [ 4 ]. In Asian culture, menopause is commonly regarded as a natural consequence of aging, associated with social status and wisdom within the community. Research conducted on women from Asia and Europe has revealed that younger women generally exhibit more favorable perception in comparison to older women who have undergone menopause [ 4 ]. Thai women view menopause as a routine, natural biological process that does not necessitate intervention, but certain Pakistani women regard it as a medical ailment that necessitates medical attention [ 4 ]. Healthcare professionals from all fields (except pediatrics) come across patients experiencing menopause. Nevertheless, their understanding and implementation of menopausal hormone therapy (MHT) still varies [ 5 ]. A study in Australia assessed the knowledge and attitudes of Australian health professionals, including general practitioners, gynecologists, and endocrinologists, on HRT. The study found that only 52% of the participants had a sufficient level of knowledge about non-hormonal therapy, while 72% had a good understanding of menopausal physiology or HRT. Moreover, there were no significant discrepancies in knowledge detected among various disciplines. However, 91% of health providers expressed their willingness to offer HRT to women who are going through menopausal symptoms. Members of menopause societies demonstrated a higher inclination to endorse HRT for prolonged durations. The main obstacle identified in prescription HRT was the concern expressed by clients regarding the possibility of acquiring breast cancer [ 5 ]. According to a survey conducted in 2019 among medical residents specializing in family medicine, internal medicine, and obstetrics and gynecology, only 6.8% of the participants expressed confidence in their ability to effectively handle the medical needs of women going through menopause [ 6 ]. A further study conducted in China involving healthcare professionals uncovered a deficiency in their comprehension of the core principles behind HRT, since nearly half of them were incapable of correctly identifying its contraindications. Enhancing clinicians' expertise and education in the management of menopause is essential for providing complete, evidence-based care. Moreover, the study revealed that receiving training on menopause care has a substantial impact on physicians' attitudes, behaviors, and confidence in identifying menopausal symptoms and offering suitable suggestions for symptom management [ 7 ]. While many physicians acknowledge the necessity of education on menopausal therapy, there exist notable gaps in knowledge that require attention. Ensuring the provision of comprehensive, evidence-based menopausal care necessitates investing in continuous education and training for physicians. Hence, it is crucial to evaluate attitudes, practices, and barriers of physicians in Saudi Arabia, regarding menopausal HRT.

[methods] Materials and methods Study design An online survey was conducted from June to September 2023 to investigate the attitudes, practices, and barriers of physicians in Saudi Arabia, regarding menopausal HRT. Study population The study population consisted of practicing physicians in Saudi Arabia specializing in gynecology, endocrinology, family medicine, internal medicine, and general practice at various levels, including consultants, senior registrars, and residents. There were no restrictions regarding the gender or demographic features of the study participants. Physicians of various specialties or those who are not currently practicing medicine in Saudi Arabia were removed. Sampling technique This study employed a convenient sampling approach. The survey link was distributed to the intended research participants in Saudi Arabia using several social media platforms (Facebook, Twitter, Snapchat, WhatsApp, and Instagram) utilizing a Google Form hyperlink. In addition, a cover letter was enclosed alongside a consent form. Participation was voluntary, granting individuals the freedom to withdraw as they saw fit. The comments were given anonymously, with no tracking of email addresses or any identifiable information. Questionnaire tool This study utilized a pre-existing questionnaire that was created by Yeganeh et al. [ 5 ]. The study questionnaire consisted of five components. The initial section was designed to collect data on the physician's demographic features, including: age category, gender, nationality, specialty, level of expertise, years of experience, practice region, practice location, and the weekly number of perimenopausal or postmenopausal female patients they attend to. The second section examined the physicians' attitude toward hormone replacement therapy. The third section analyzed the practices of physicians regarding HRT. This included their ability to stay updated with current HRT recommendations, their preferred method of obtaining up-to-date information about HRT, their preferred duration of systemic HRT for women with premature menopause, their preferred duration of combined HRT for symptomatic women over 50 years old, their preferred duration of estrogen-only HRT for symptomatic women without a uterus over 50 years old, their preferred treatment for hot flashes, their preferred type of systemic HRT for women with premature menopause without contraindications, and their preferred type of systemic HRT for women over 50 years old without contraindications. The fourth section examined the likelihood of administering systemic hormone replacement HRT to a woman experiencing menopause, while the fifth section explored the challenges associated with HRT. Questionnaire piloting In the pilot phase, the questionnaire was sent randomly to different locations in Saudi Arabia among a sample of 30 participants to evaluate the face validity of the study tool. The degree to which the items measure or assess what it is intended to measure.
These participants did not make any contributions to the findings of the primary investigation. Ethical approval The ethical approval for this study was obtained from the ethical research committee of the Institutional Review Board (IRB) of Imam Muhammed Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU), Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, wherein they reviewed and approved this project (HAPO-01-R-0011; Project No.499/2023). Informed consent was obtained from all participants. Data analysis The data were analyzed utilizing the Statistical Package for Social Science Software (SPSS), especially version 28 (IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, USA). Numerical values and percentages (%) were used to represent categorical variables.

[results] Results Participants’ demographic characteristics A total of 95 physicians were involved in this study. Around 41.1% (n=39) of them were aged 25-34 years. More than half of them (53.7; n=51) were males. The vast majority of the study participants (82.1%; n=78) were Saudis. Around one-third of the study participants (35.8%; n=34) were gynaecologists. Almost 47.4% (n=45) of the study participants were residents. Around 34.7% (n=33) of the study participants reported that they have an experience of less than five years. More than half of the study participants (65.3%; n=62) reported that they practice in the Central region. Around 30.5% (n=29) of the study participants reported that they work at military, security, or national guard hospitals. More than half of the study participants (55.8%; n=53) reported that the number of perimenopausal or postmenopausal women that they see as patients each week is less than five patients. For further details on the demographic and practice characteristics of the study participants refer to Table 1 . A total of 60.0% of the study participants agreed that in general (agreed or strongly agreed), HRT should be offered to menopausal women who have menopausal symptoms. Besides, around 24.2% of them agreed that in general, HRT should be offered to menopausal women who do not have menopausal symptoms. A total of 48.4% (n=46) of the study participants agreed that they find it easy to keep up with current HRT recommendations/guidelines/evidence. The most commonly preferred methods of obtaining up-to-date information about HRT were Ministry of Health publications and journal articles, contributing for 36.8% (n=35) and 24.2% (n=23), respectively. The most commonly preferred duration of systemic (i.e. non-vaginal) HRT for women with premature menopause was one to five years, accounting for 60.0% (n=57). The most commonly preferred treatment for hot flashes was lifestyle modifications (32.6%; n=31).
