[discussion] Discussion In this study, we analyzed the distribution characteristics of ONH VD across different regions and layers, as well as its influencing factors, in highly myopic and non-highly myopic populations using an OCTA imaging system. Our findings demonstrated that the correlation between age and VD exhibited significant tissue-layer specificity: a trend of decreasing VD with age in adults was observed in the SVC and inner retina, consistent with previous findings in the radial peripapillary retinal capillary plexus (RPC). 13 In contrast, the DVC and the choroidal layer did not show a significant correlation with age. We believe the age-related changes in the SVC and inner retinal layers may be associated with degenerative vascular conditions such as atherosclerosis. With aging, arterial walls progressively thicken and narrow, resulting in slower blood flow and reduced VD. 14 As atherosclerosis mainly affects large and medium-sized arteries, this could explain why the SVC is more prominently affected than the DVC. It is noteworthy that gender did not have a significant effect on VD in the three retinal layers. However, statistical significance was observed in the choroidal layer of individuals aged 21 to 40 years and those aged ≥60 years, probably indicating a potential hormonal influence that requires further validation through biochemical and molecular analyses. The effect of AL on VD demonstrated regional and layer specificity. In the different AL subgroups, we observed that VD in all regions of the choroidal layer significantly decreased with increasing AL. However, VD in the inner retina showed no significant change, likely due to the opposing trends observed in the SVC and DVC. Chen et al. 15 investigated the VD in RPC, SVC, and DVC. They reported significant differences in VD in the I, S, and N regions for RPC and SVC, with a decreasing trend associated with increasing AL in regions with group differences. However, no significant differences were found in the DVD across all four peripapillary regions. The influence of AL on VD varied by region within the same retinal layer, with the SVC demonstrating the most sensitive response, making it a key focus for analysis. Inter-regional analysis is particularly important to avoid misleading conclusions, as averaging VD across regions may obscure significant variations in VD trends. The effect of different ALs on retinal VD remains controversial in the literature. Cheng et al. 16 found that the VD in the RPC decreased with increasing AL in all five regions (I, S, N, T, and D). One study indicated that the VD in the D region of the RPC did not differ significantly between AL groups. 17 However, another study showed that average VD of the peripapillary regions in the RPC decreased with AL, whereas the VD in the D region increased. 18 Notably, in our study, the central region of the D region showed paradoxically elevated VD at all layers. This may be due to the fact that the D region is primarily supplied by the Zinn-Haller arterial circle, which has abundant collateral circulation, whereas the other four regions are supplied by the central arteries of the retina. 19 The difference between studies may be attributed to the different partitioning methods and the fact that the studies mentioned did not correct for AL, which standardizes the range of VD observations across different eyes, leading to more objective and accurate results. Additionally, differences in the layers of the VDs observed, the ages of the study populations, and the subgroups of AL may also account for the discrepancies. Consistent with previous studies, 9 the peripapillary choroidal layer VD may serve as a predictor of myopia. A tendency for VD to decrease with AL elongation was observed across all regions. Additionally, the SVC VD may also act as a sensitive predictor of myopia. However, this requires regional analysis due to varying trends in different regions, which still needs to be further validated by additional studies. We further stratified subjects into highly myopic and non-highly myopic groups to conduct a quantitative analysis of VD distribution and regional ordering. In the non-highly myopic group, the anatomic ordering of the VD in the inner retina mirrored the previously reported sequence of retinal artery and vein diameters. 20 The VD ordering in the inner retinal layers and the SVC followed a consistent pattern across the groups, likely due to the large retinal vessels traveling within the SVC, suggesting that perfusion in these two layers is predominantly governed by major retinal vessels. Interestingly, the VD distribution pattern in the DVC appears to be associated with that of the SVC. The IN region, which had the lowest VD in the SVC, showed a relatively higher rank in the DVC, whereas the ST region, which had the highest SVC VD, showed a reduced rank in the DVC. We hypothesize that this may reflect a compensatory vascular mechanism. VD changes may be influenced by transmural pressure, which varies across different vascular layers. Downstream vessels might undergo morphological changes to compensate for upstream flow alterations. 21 However, this speculation requires further investigation. Between the highly myopic and non-highly myopic groups, significant VD alterations were observed in the temporal hemisphere of the inner retinal layers, with an increase in VD in the SVC and a decrease in the DVC, whereas the opposite trend was observed in the nasal hemisphere of the layer, which may result in the two groups of VD being ordered differently in the SVC and the DVC but being ordered consistently in the inner retina due to the counteracting effect. Mo et al. 22 reported significant reductions in retinal VD in the N and T regions of the ONH in highly myopic eyes. These discrepancies may stem from the lack of correction in their analysis and inconsistencies in the segmentation of regions. As the AL increases, the central vascular trunk in the inner retinal layer is dragged nasally. 23 Jonas et al. 24 found that a reduction in the angle between the superior and inferior temporal vascular arches of the ONH may explain the observed increase in the temporal VD in the inner retinal layer. This may be attributed to biomechanical stretching, which could enhance perfusion efficiency in the temporal arcuate fiber region by altering vascular geometry, thereby preserving visual function. 17 The ordering of the choroidal layers showed a significantly higher VD on the nasal side than on the temporal side, with the possible explanation being that the watershed area located between the temporal artery and the area supplied by the posterior nasociliary artery mostly involves the temporal side and rarely the nasal side. 25 Most studies examining the correlation between choroidal blood flow and myopia have focused on the macular region. Changes in choroidal blood flow, which affect CT, may serve as a predictor for myopia progression, as demonstrated in studies on guinea pigs’ optic nerves. 26 Our results also showed that choroidal VD decreases with increasing AL across all five regions, with the most significant reduction in the T hemisphere region especially in the IT region, but there was no change in the ordering of VD, probably due to the absence of significant regional damage to the fundus structures. In addition, choroidal layer VD showed the most stable performance with the smallest standard deviation among all layers. This suggests that choroidal VD may serve as a sensitive indicator for early disease prediction, particularly in the T hemisphere region. Excessive axial elongation of the eyeball leads to biomechanical stretching of the retina and choroid, 27 , 28 which may result in the straightening and narrowing of vessels in highly myopic eyes and a reduction in vessel branching, which in turn leads to lower VD. 29 The reasons for the more pronounced reduction in the VD of the T region of the choroidal layer in highly myopic eyes remain to be further explored. Our study focused on the VD in a population with normal fundus structures and analyzed its influencing factors. Signal strength, a key parameter of OCTA imaging quality, was found to have a significant impact on VD measurements across all layers. Whether signal strength mediates the relationship between AL and choroidal VD is a noteworthy concern. To explore this, we conducted a mediation analysis. The results showed a direct effect of AL on choroidal VD of −0.6673 and an indirect effect of −0.0358 mediated by signal strength, leading to a total effect of −0.7031. The mediating role of signal strength accounted for approximately 5% of the total effect, indicating a limited influence. Therefore, we did not include signal strength as a covariate in the final analysis. After correction according to the AL, we observed no significant association between AL and inner retinal VD, which aligns with the findings of Lv et al., who reported a similar trend in the SVP VD. 30 In our model 1, we identified a positive correlation between RNFL thickness and inner retinal VD, which is consistent with a previous study demonstrating a similar positive correlation between RNFL thickness and SVC VD. Together, these findings reinforce the structural-functional relationship between retinal circulation and neural integrity. 31 CT thinning due to increased AL has been recognized as a strong correlate of decreased choroidal VD in several studies, 32 – 34 and this was confirmed in our univariable model. However, CT was excluded from the multivariable model. Both CT and choroidal VD are influenced by factors such as age and AL, and their physiological link requires further investigation. 35 This aligns with the results of our multivariable model, suggesting that CT may not be a sufficient indicator of choroidal circulation. Smaller LT and deeper ACD may reflect a compensatory mechanism to counteract the optical effects of axial elongation during myopic progression. 36 – 38 However, due to the confounding effects of LT and ACD with age, AL, and other factors, these variables were similarly excluded from the multivariable models 1 and 2. The effect of vertical C/D on the choroidal blood flow has not been previously reported, but a possible explanation is that vertical C/D is negatively correlated with AL, 39 and the exact mechanism needs to be further investigated. DFD and DFA are important parameters of the posterior fundus that collectively characterize the position of the ONH relative to the macula. DFD increased with axial elongation, but DFA did not appear to be significantly correlated with AL. The axial elongation-related increase in DFD was attributed to the enlargement of the parapapillary β / γ zone, which was defined as the parapapillary zone with visible sclera and visible large choroidal vessels and was associated with capillary occlusion. 40 , 41 This may explain the inclusion of DFD in the model 2. In moderately myopic eyes, BMO movement typically occurred, which may lead to passive exposure of the choroid, thereby forming the γ zone. In highly myopic eyes, the enlargement of BMO may directly stretch the choroidal capillaries leading to a decrease in branch density. 42 Therefore, BMO was also included in our multivariate model. Our study further confirmed that CT, ACD, AQD, and DFD all increased with increasing AL across the different AL subgroups, and these findings highlighted the need for further investigation to elucidate the exact mechanisms. Previous studies have suggested that the retinal and choroidal blood flow is influenced by circadian rhythms. 43 , 44 In our study, when we categorized time into ante meridiem and post meridiem as dichotomous variables, we did not find a correlation between time and VD. For the first time, we comprehensively examined the choroidal VD surrounding the optic disc across different regions and analyzed its influencing factors. Additionally, we experimentally collected and sequenced VD data from patients with glaucoma and those with glaucoma combined with high myopia, revealing distinct regional VD ordering patterns among diseased and healthy individuals. This finding highlights the importance of regional hemodynamic studies in disease characterization. Moreover, our partitioning method more accurately reflects vascular alignment and anatomic structures, an approach that has been rarely explored in previous studies. Furthermore, our study corrected for AL-related imaging distortions in OCTA, collected a broad range of ocular parameters, and accounted for the effects of physiological factors such as age, gender, and examination time. Our findings may contribute to the early detection of disease-related microvascular alterations, provide a theoretical foundation for understanding the mechanisms of myopia-associated microcirculatory changes, elucidate the dynamic evolution of VD with axial elongation, and establish a baseline database of VD across different ONH regions and layers in healthy populations. However, several limitations should be acknowledged: First, we used AL instead of diopters as a variable to assess its effect on VD. Second, the highly myopic group had a relatively small sample size of only 55 eyes, representing 16.3% of the total sample, which may limit the generalizability of the findings. Future studies should incorporate larger highly myopic cohorts to enable a more comprehensive assessment of VD alterations. Last, due to the exclusion of patients with hypertension and diabetes, recruiting elderly subjects was challenging. Nonetheless, we accounted for age-related effects when assessing VD. In conclusion, we examined the distribution of retinal and choroidal VD across different regions after correcting for AL and analyzed the factors influencing VD at both layers. Our findings demonstrate that choroidal VD remains relatively stable, exhibiting a progressive decline in all regions with increasing AL, with the most pronounced reduction observed in the temporal hemisphere, but the ordering of the different regions did not change. However, not all retinal regions exhibited similar reductions, expanding our understanding of blood flow dynamics around the optic disc.

[conclusion] Conclusions Inner retinal VD followed T > SN > IN, primarily contributed by the SVC, whereas choroidal VD followed N > T. With increasing AL, choroidal VD declined across all regions, most prominently in the T hemisphere, whereas the inner retinal VD trends varied by region and layer.

retrieved_papers.title: Myopic-Net: Deep Learning-Based Direct Identification of Myopia Onset and Progression.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] Identifying and monitoring the onset and progression of myopia (myopia onset and progression [MOP]) based on the changes in anatomical structures in fundus retinal images has significant clinical application prospects. For this purpose, we tested the performance of deep neural networks.
We established a deep neural network, called Myopic-Net, to detect anatomical changes owing to the MOP from a pair of retinal images collected during different fundoscopies. Myopic-Net was developed using 3964 fundus image pairs without MOP and 2380 fundus image pairs with MOP. Five indicators-accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F1-score-were evaluated on the internal test set and the independent external test set. In addition, we use a deep network visualization method to explore the factors driving Myopic-Net to predict.
On the internal test set, Myopic-Net achieved an accuracy of 87.3%; the precision, recall, and specificity were 86.2%, 80.1%, and 91.9% respectively, while the identification accuracy of two ophthalmologists is only 66.1% and 73.5%, respectively. Even on the external test set, Myopic-Net still achieved an accuracy of 84.1%. In addition, we found that the factors driving Myopic-Net to predict are mainly anatomical changes in the optic disc and surrounding areas.
Myopic-Net has been shown to be able to identify the MOP from fundus image pairs using anatomical changes in optic disc and surrounding areas. And Myopic-Net has good accuracy, reliability, and generalization ability. These factors show that deep neural networks have strong potential in monitoring and final diagnosing the MOP based on fundus image analysis.
With the development of fundus imaging technology based on intelligent mobile terminals, embedding the program based on Myopic-Net has great potential to achieve convenient and fast personalized monitoring of myopia.

[Introduction] Introduction Myopia is a type of refractive error 1 that can cause irreversible visual impairment. 2 Once developing into high myopia, it will greatly increase the risk of multiple complications, causing severe visual impairment and even blindness. 3 – 7 However, myopia is still not fully understood so far, and the most critical mechanism of myopia onset and progression (MOP) remains unclear, 8 which makes it difficult to develop targeted and efficient myopia prevention and control programs. To make matters worse, the prevalence of myopia has increased sharply in recent years. By 2020, myopia has affected nearly 30% of the world's population, and it is expected that by 2050, nearly one-half of the world's population will suffer from myopia. 9 As a result, myopia has been recognized as a major public health problem worldwide. 10 , 11 The MOP usually occur in childhood, 12 and regular vision examinations are an important means to prevent and monitor it. Obviously, the greater the frequency and reliability of vision examinations, the better the proactive prevention and control of MOP. The main way of vision examination is to measure and evaluate the degree of refractive error in the eye. And the spherical equivalent (SE) is a commonly used index to measure the degree of refractive error, which can be calculated as the sum of the spherical power and one-half of the cylinder power. 13 According to whether a cycloplegic agent is used, the measurement results can be divided into noncycloplegic SE and cycloplegic SE. Because children's ciliary muscles have strong active adjustment ability, 14 , 15 noncycloplegic refraction will easily lead to the overestimation of myopia or underestimation of hyperopia, 13 , 16 – 19 making the measured noncycloplegic SE lack accuracy. Therefore, the assessment of refractive error of the eye in the setting of cycloplegia is considered the gold standard for refractive error measurement in children. 20 , 21 However, there are many difficulties in administering cycloplegia to children 13 : (1) guardians may be reluctant to allow their children to receive cycloplegic agents owing to the possible side effects 22 , 23 or the inconvenience caused by blurred vision, photophobia 18 , 24 , 25 ; and (2) children may resist and refuse to cooperate owing to nervousness and fear. Therefore, owing to possible side effects, complicated examination procedures, long examination times, and high examination costs, 26 cycloplegic refraction is usually difficult to perform frequently. In contrast, noncycloplegic refraction avoids the problems associated with the use of cycloplegic agents. At present, measuring and evaluating the refractive status of children's eyes in the absence of cycloplegia remains a widely used method in most preventive regular examinations. 7 , 27 – 29 However, the SE measurement bias caused by the absence of cycloplegia is difficult to accurately quantitatively model and correct. 13 The reason is that the adjustment ability of the ciliary muscle varies greatly between different individuals, different ages, different sexes, and even different degrees of refractive error, 30 - 33 and it is difficult to quantitatively model and accurately evaluate. However, it is worth noting that, although the changes in SE before and after the use of cycloplegic agents are difficult to quantitatively evaluate, the anatomical structure of the fundus retina does not change depending on whether the ciliary muscle is paralyzed. Moreover, the fundus retina also undergoes corresponding anatomical changes with MOP, 34 – 40 such as the shape of the optic disc changing from an approximately circular shape to a vertical oval, and the parapapillary gamma zone expanding at the edge of the temporal disc. Thanks to the rapid development of fundus retinal imaging technology, clear fundus retinal images with a wide field of view can still be collected without cycloplegia, 41 – 43 which can fully meet the needs of subsequent image processing and analysis. In addition, smartphone-based fundus imaging technology is constantly developing, 44 making it easier, faster, and more convenient for people to collect fundus retinal images by themselves. Therefore, monitoring the MOP based on fundus retinal images will become increasingly important and have more and more application prospects and study value. In this study, deep learning technology was used to identify the MOP on a pair of retinal images collected during different fundoscopies, which explores the feasibility of applying deep learning in monitoring the MOP based on fundus retinal images. Specifically, our contributions are as follows. (1) A classification convolutional neural network was developed to detect anatomical changes that are closely related to the MOP in fundus retinal image pairs. (2) We quantitatively evaluated the performance of the established convolutional neural network in identifying the MOP on internal test set and independent external test set, and compare with the manual identification results of two experienced ophthalmologists. (3) The interpretability of the established convolutional neural network was evaluated qualitatively using a deep network visualization method to identify relevant features that drive the model to make predictions.

[introduction] Introduction Myopia is a type of refractive error 1 that can cause irreversible visual impairment. 2 Once developing into high myopia, it will greatly increase the risk of multiple complications, causing severe visual impairment and even blindness. 3 – 7 However, myopia is still not fully understood so far, and the most critical mechanism of myopia onset and progression (MOP) remains unclear, 8 which makes it difficult to develop targeted and efficient myopia prevention and control programs. To make matters worse, the prevalence of myopia has increased sharply in recent years. By 2020, myopia has affected nearly 30% of the world's population, and it is expected that by 2050, nearly one-half of the world's population will suffer from myopia. 9 As a result, myopia has been recognized as a major public health problem worldwide. 10 , 11 The MOP usually occur in childhood, 12 and regular vision examinations are an important means to prevent and monitor it. Obviously, the greater the frequency and reliability of vision examinations, the better the proactive prevention and control of MOP. The main way of vision examination is to measure and evaluate the degree of refractive error in the eye. And the spherical equivalent (SE) is a commonly used index to measure the degree of refractive error, which can be calculated as the sum of the spherical power and one-half of the cylinder power. 13 According to whether a cycloplegic agent is used, the measurement results can be divided into noncycloplegic SE and cycloplegic SE. Because children's ciliary muscles have strong active adjustment ability, 14 , 15 noncycloplegic refraction will easily lead to the overestimation of myopia or underestimation of hyperopia, 13 , 16 – 19 making the measured noncycloplegic SE lack accuracy. Therefore, the assessment of refractive error of the eye in the setting of cycloplegia is considered the gold standard for refractive error measurement in children. 20 , 21 However, there are many difficulties in administering cycloplegia to children 13 : (1) guardians may be reluctant to allow their children to receive cycloplegic agents owing to the possible side effects 22 , 23 or the inconvenience caused by blurred vision, photophobia 18 , 24 , 25 ; and (2) children may resist and refuse to cooperate owing to nervousness and fear. Therefore, owing to possible side effects, complicated examination procedures, long examination times, and high examination costs, 26 cycloplegic refraction is usually difficult to perform frequently. In contrast, noncycloplegic refraction avoids the problems associated with the use of cycloplegic agents. At present, measuring and evaluating the refractive status of children's eyes in the absence of cycloplegia remains a widely used method in most preventive regular examinations. 7 , 27 – 29 However, the SE measurement bias caused by the absence of cycloplegia is difficult to accurately quantitatively model and correct. 13 The reason is that the adjustment ability of the ciliary muscle varies greatly between different individuals, different ages, different sexes, and even different degrees of refractive error, 30 - 33 and it is difficult to quantitatively model and accurately evaluate. However, it is worth noting that, although the changes in SE before and after the use of cycloplegic agents are difficult to quantitatively evaluate, the anatomical structure of the fundus retina does not change depending on whether the ciliary muscle is paralyzed. Moreover, the fundus retina also undergoes corresponding anatomical changes with MOP, 34 – 40 such as the shape of the optic disc changing from an approximately circular shape to a vertical oval, and the parapapillary gamma zone expanding at the edge of the temporal disc. Thanks to the rapid development of fundus retinal imaging technology, clear fundus retinal images with a wide field of view can still be collected without cycloplegia, 41 – 43 which can fully meet the needs of subsequent image processing and analysis. In addition, smartphone-based fundus imaging technology is constantly developing, 44 making it easier, faster, and more convenient for people to collect fundus retinal images by themselves. Therefore, monitoring the MOP based on fundus retinal images will become increasingly important and have more and more application prospects and study value. In this study, deep learning technology was used to identify the MOP on a pair of retinal images collected during different fundoscopies, which explores the feasibility of applying deep learning in monitoring the MOP based on fundus retinal images. Specifically, our contributions are as follows. (1) A classification convolutional neural network was developed to detect anatomical changes that are closely related to the MOP in fundus retinal image pairs. (2) We quantitatively evaluated the performance of the established convolutional neural network in identifying the MOP on internal test set and independent external test set, and compare with the manual identification results of two experienced ophthalmologists. (3) The interpretability of the established convolutional neural network was evaluated qualitatively using a deep network visualization method to identify relevant features that drive the model to make predictions.

[methods] Explanation of Predictions With Deep Network Visualization Method We use all pairs of fundus retinal images in the test sets as input to Myopic-Net and apply the Grad-CAM method to determine which part of retinal anatomical area in the input contributes most to the identification of MOP. The visualization results are shown in Figure 4 . We randomly selected eight fundus retinal image pairs for display. Areas in the image coded with warm colors represent more relevant anatomy, that is, red indicates the greatest degree of correlation. The results show that the anatomical changes of the optic disc and surrounding areas in fundus retinal image are the main factors driving Myopic-Net to predict the MOP. Specifically, as shown in Figure 5 , for fundus retinal image pairs with MOP, the anatomical changes that occur in these retinal areas mainly include the following. (1) The shape of the ophthalmoscopically visible optic disc changes from approximately circular to vertically oval, the diameter of the ophthalmoscopically visible horizontal optic disc is shortened, the size of the ophthalmoscopically visible optic disc is reduced. (2) Parapapillary atrophy occurs, or parapapillary atrophy, further expands. (3) The curvature of retinal blood vessels is reduced significantly. Figure 4. Apply the Grad-CAM method to visualize the main retinal anatomical regions that drive Myopic-Net to predict the MOP. The first two columns contain four pairs of fundus retinal images of the left eyes, and the last two columns contain four pairs of fundus retinal images of the right eyes. The first two rows are four pairs of fundus retinal images with MOP, and the last two rows are four pairs of fundus retinal images without MOP. Warmer colors in the image represent greater contributions to the prediction results. The visualization results show that anatomical changes in the optic disc and surrounding areas are the main factors driving Myopic-Net to predict the MOP. Figure 5. The anatomical changes that contribute the most to predict the MOP. These two fundus retinal images have MOP. The black curves in the figure are drawn along the retinal blood vessels for highlighting. The white circle in the first image represents the approximate area where the optic disc is located. The white circle in the second image represents the approximate area of optic disc and parapapillary atrophy, and the white arc in this area represents the approximate dividing line between optic disc and parapapillary atrophy. In contrast, for a fundus retinal image pair without MOP, these anatomical changes will not occur in these retinal areas, and the retinal anatomy between the two images is basically the same.

[results] Results This section shows the results of evaluating the well-trained Myopic-Net on the internal test set and the independent external test set. Quantitative Metrics As shown in Table 2 , we first report the quantitative evaluation results of Myopic-Net on two test sets. The overall accuracy on internal test set (external test set) is 87.3% (84.1%), with good performance. In addition, among the fundus retinal image pairs identified as with MOP, the proportion of fundus retinal image pairs actually with MOP is 86.2% (71.4%). Among the fundus retinal image pairs that are actually with MOP, the proportion of fundus retinal image pairs that can be identified correctly is 80.1% (78.1%). Among the fundus retinal image pairs that are actually without MOP, the proportion of fundus retinal image pairs that can be identified correctly is 91.9% (86.7%). The specific confusion matrices on two test sets are shown in Table 3 . Table 2. Quantitative Evaluation Results of Myopic-Net on Two Test Sets Evaluation Results Metrics Internal Test Set External Test Set Accuracy 87.3% 84.1% Precision 86.2% 71.4% Recall 80.1% 78.1% Specificity 91.9% 86.7% F1-score 83.0% 74.6% Table 3. Confusion Matrix of Myopic-Net Prediction Results on Internal Test Set (External Test Set) Prediction MOP 1 0 GT MOP 1 442 (25) 110 (7) 0 71 (10) 803 (65) GT, ground truth. For the fundus retinal image pairs with MOP in the testing data, we further subdivided them into two parts based on whether the first image in the pair is in the myopic state: myopia onset (MO) and myopia progression (MP). The former corresponds with the fundus retinal image pair from nonmyopia to myopia, and the latter corresponds with the fundus retinal image pair from myopia to more myopia. We count the prediction results of Myopic-Net on these two parts of data as shown in Table 4 . There are a total of 552 (32) fundus retinal image pairs with MOP in the internal test set (external test set), including 303 (10) groups with MO and 249 (22) groups with MP. The values of prediction accuracy are 78.2% (70.0%) and 82.3% (81.8%), respectively. Table 4. Statistics of Prediction Results of Myopic-Net on Two Parts of Data, MO and MP Internal Test Set External Test Set Items MO MP MO MP Total 303 249 10 22 Correct predictions 237 205 7 18 Accuracy 78.2% 82.3% 70.0% 81.8% Explanation of Predictions With Deep Network Visualization Method We use all pairs of fundus retinal images in the test sets as input to Myopic-Net and apply the Grad-CAM method to determine which part of retinal anatomical area in the input contributes most to the identification of MOP. The visualization results are shown in Figure 4 . We randomly selected eight fundus retinal image pairs for display. Areas in the image coded with warm colors represent more relevant anatomy, that is, red indicates the greatest degree of correlation. The results show that the anatomical changes of the optic disc and surrounding areas in fundus retinal image are the main factors driving Myopic-Net to predict the MOP. Specifically, as shown in Figure 5 , for fundus retinal image pairs with MOP, the anatomical changes that occur in these retinal areas mainly include the following. (1) The shape of the ophthalmoscopically visible optic disc changes from approximately circular to vertically oval, the diameter of the ophthalmoscopically visible horizontal optic disc is shortened, the size of the ophthalmoscopically visible optic disc is reduced. (2) Parapapillary atrophy occurs, or parapapillary atrophy, further expands. (3) The curvature of retinal blood vessels is reduced significantly. Figure 4. Apply the Grad-CAM method to visualize the main retinal anatomical regions that drive Myopic-Net to predict the MOP. The first two columns contain four pairs of fundus retinal images of the left eyes, and the last two columns contain four pairs of fundus retinal images of the right eyes. The first two rows are four pairs of fundus retinal images with MOP, and the last two rows are four pairs of fundus retinal images without MOP. Warmer colors in the image represent greater contributions to the prediction results. The visualization results show that anatomical changes in the optic disc and surrounding areas are the main factors driving Myopic-Net to predict the MOP. Figure 5. The anatomical changes that contribute the most to predict the MOP. These two fundus retinal images have MOP. The black curves in the figure are drawn along the retinal blood vessels for highlighting. The white circle in the first image represents the approximate area where the optic disc is located. The white circle in the second image represents the approximate area of optic disc and parapapillary atrophy, and the white arc in this area represents the approximate dividing line between optic disc and parapapillary atrophy. In contrast, for a fundus retinal image pair without MOP, these anatomical changes will not occur in these retinal areas, and the retinal anatomy between the two images is basically the same.

[discussion] Discussion In this study, we take a pair of retinal images collected during different fundoscopies as input, and use the proposed deep neural network (Myopic-Net) to detect and analyze retinal anatomical changes that are closely related to the MOP, thereby achieving classification and identification of MOP. Here, we discuss four fundamental issues with the method proposed in this study: (1) performance analysis of the proposed method; (2) the reliability of Myopic-Net predictions; (3) the application prospects and value of the proposed method in modern clinical environments; and (4) the limitations and shortcomings of the proposed method. Performance Analysis We asked two experienced ophthalmologists to manually identify whether the image pairs in the internal test set have MOP. The accuracy rates of ophthalmologist 1 and ophthalmologist 2 are 66.1% and 73.5%, respectively. In contrast, Myopic-Net achieves a prediction accuracy of 87.3% on the internal test set, with much better performance. In addition, the prediction accuracy of ophthalmologist 1 for the two categories of data with and without MOP is 91.3% and 50.2%, respectively. This result shows that ophthalmologist 1 has a relatively strong subjectivity and tends to judge fundus retinal image pairs with no obvious anatomical changes as having the MOP. And the prediction accuracy of ophthalmologist 2 for the two categories of data with and without MOP is 60.5% and 81.7%, respectively. This result shows that ophthalmologist 2 has stricter subjective criteria for judging fundus retinal image pairs as having MOP. In contrast, the prediction accuracy of Myopic-Net for the two categories of data with and without MOP is 80.1% and 91.9%, respectively. The gap between the former (80.1%) and the latter (91.9%) is significantly smaller, which indicates that the judgment criteria of Myopic-Net are more objective and stable. And we think the main reason for this gap is that the degree of MOP in some data is small, and the corresponding retinal anatomical changes are not obvious. Moreover, the fundus retinal image pairs with MOP are further subdivided into two parts: MO and MP, and the prediction accuracy is 78.2% and 82.3%, respectively. The former (78.2%) has some gaps compared with the latter (82.3%), and this phenomenon also appears in the evaluation results of two ophthalmologists (ophthalmologist 1: MO, 89.8% and MP, 93.2%; ophthalmologist 2: MO, 58.1% and MP,63.5%). Considering that the longitudinal study results of Lee et al., 54 on the changes in parapapillary atrophy in myopic children show that the changes in parapapillary atrophy are small in the early stages of myopia, and some even have almost no changes. Therefore, we think the main reason is that this phenomenon of insignificant retinal anatomical changes occurs more often in the stage of MO. What's more, Myopic-Net still achieves an accuracy of 84.1% on the independent external test set, and the gap between it and the accuracy (87.3%) on the internal test set is totally within the acceptable range. The remaining evaluation results of Myopic-Net on the external test set also fully comply with this analysis. These further demonstrate the accuracy, stability, and generalization ability of Myopic-Net. All in all, the overall performance of the method proposed in this study is good. Differences in prediction performance for different types of data are in line with the characteristics of the data and the network itself, and are highly interpretable. Considering that larger-scale, higher-quality, and more reasonably distributed training data will further improve the performance of Myopic-Net, we believe that the proposed method is promising in future applications. Reliability of Myopic-Net Predictions The suitability of deep neural networks for clinical applications does not only depend on acceptable quantitative evaluation results. More important, it also depends on whether the relevant anatomical features that drive the network to predict are physiologically reasonable and meaningful. This study applies the Grad-CAM method to determine that the anatomical changes of the optic disc and surrounding areas in fundus retinal image pairs are the main factors driving Myopic-Net to predict the MOP. In previous related studies, Guo et al., 34 Jonas et al., 35 , 36 , 39 , 55 and Dai et al., 37 reported that the parapapillary gamma zone develops and expands with the MOP. Studies on the optic disc and MOP conducted by Samarawickrama et al., 56 Kim et al., 57 and Jonas et al., 58 , 59 show that, as myopia progresses, the ophthalmoscopic shape of the optic disc will gradually change from the original approximately circular shape to a vertical oval structure, with the horizontal diameter shortening and the size decreasing. Regarding retinal blood vessels, Jonas et al., 60 reported that, in highly myopic eyes that undergo MP, the course of retinal blood vessels toward the optic disc becomes straighter. The results of this study show that the predictions of Myopic-Net are physiologically reasonable, which greatly ensures the reliability of Myopic-Net in predicting the MOP based on fundus retinal image pairs. In addition, we summarize the experience of two ophthalmologists in identifying the MOP based on fundus retinal image pairs as follows: (1) whether a tessellated fundus appears and whether the size of the leopard spots changes; (2) whether the optic disc becomes smaller, flattened, tilted, or occurs torsion; (3) whether the parapapillary atrophy occurs and whether there are changes in width and height; and (4) whether the curvature of the blood vessels decreases and the blood vessels become straighter. As we can see, the experience of ophthalmologists and the focus area of Myopic-Net are highly consistent, which also proves the reliability of Myopic-Net to a certain extent. Prospects and Value of Clinical Application The method proposed in this study is suitable for longitudinal monitoring tasks of MOP. As we all know, timely myopia intervention and control are effective means to prevent the onset and severe progression of myopia. And myopia monitoring as frequently and long-term as possible is the prerequisite for timely and effective myopia intervention and control. Therefore, the task of longitudinal monitoring of MOP is particularly important in current and future clinical applications. Thanks to the development and popularization of electronic medical records, long-term myopia monitoring for people has become increasingly convenient and feasible. Moreover, our method has multiple advantages in the application of myopia monitoring tasks, especially compared with refraction, mainly including the following. (1) The acquisition of fundus retinal images is more convenient and faster, especially with the development of fundus imaging technology based on intelligent mobile terminals, Myopic-Net can achieve low-cost and high-frequency monitoring of MOP, which helps to achieve more timely and effective myopia intervention and control.; (2) The measurement results of refractive power are usually affected by factors such as the subject's own condition, measurement device, cycloplegic agent, and environment, whereas fundus images are an imaging of the inherent retinal anatomical structure and have better stability. (3) Fundus retinal images contain rich information and can simultaneously monitor the retinopathy caused by high myopia or pathological myopia, allowing for early detection and treatment to reduce the risk of blindness. Therefore, the proposed method has good application prospects and value in future clinical environments. Limitations Data with a larger scale, higher quality, and more even distribution of categories will further improve the performance of deep learning-based methods. At present, the proposed method has poor prediction performance for data with a small degree of MOP. This is mainly attributed to the fact that this part of data accounts for a small proportion of the overall data, so the network is not sufficiently trained on this part of data. However, owing to constraints, such as cost, time, medical resources, and other practical factors, at present, the time interval for myopia follow-up examinations is relatively long, which makes it difficult to collect enough data that have a smaller degree of MOP. In addition, to balance the size and quality of the dataset, we retain a considerable portion of data with relatively poor image quality, and only remove data with very poor image quality during the data preparation stage. This verifies the robustness and applicability of Myopic-Net to a certain extent, but it is undoubtedly also an important factor restricting the quantitative performance of Myopic-Net. We believe that broader cooperation through the scientific community may be an effective way to solve the above problem. Moreover, fundus retinal images collected during different examinations will inevitably have large differences in contrast, brightness, hue, and so on, which has a certain impact on image analysis based on deep neural networks. We believe that designing reasonable self-supervised learning tasks to guide the deep neural network to pay more attention to spatial structure information, such as texture and gradient in images, may be an effective way to solve this problem. This is also one of the focus of our follow-up study. Last, the refractive error in the dataset used in this study are measured in the noncycloplegic condition. Although the SE threshold and UCVA threshold are jointly set to define myopia to reduce the impact of inaccurate refractive error measurement, there may still be errors, which may have an adverse effect on the performance of Myopic-Net. We believe that using a dataset with a large amount of cycloplegic SE to train Myopic-Net or exploring more effective criteria for defining myopia in the noncycloplegic condition may be an effective way to further alleviate this problem.

[conclusion] Conclusions In this study, we develop a new artificial intelligence tool, Myopic-Net, that predicts the MOP by extracting anatomical change information between two fundus retinal images. The experimental results show that Myopic-Net relies on the anatomical change information of the optic disc and surrounding areas in the decision-making process, which is physiologically reasonable. Myopic-Net achieves good quantitative evaluation results in identifying the MOP, and the overall accuracy on the internal test set and the independent external test set has reached 87.3% and 84.1%, respectively. As a convenient, accurate, and efficient myopia monitoring tool, the proposed method has the potential to promote timely detection of MOP. The code has been made publicly available at https://github.com/ZengshuoWang/Myopic-Net .

retrieved_papers.title: Measurement of normal retinal vascular pulse wave attenuation using modified photoplethysmography.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2020]
[Abstract] Pulse wave attenuation characteristics reflect compliance and resistance properties of the vessel wall as well as initial pulse generation factors. Recently, it has become possible to measure and map the retinal vessel wall pulse wave amplitudes. Predictable pulse wave amplitude distribution may allow inferences to be made concerning vascular compliance and resistance. Twenty-eight eyes from sixteen subjects (8 male and 8 female) were examined using modified retinal photoplethysmography with simultaneous ophthalmodynamometry. This allowed the assessment of vessel wall pulsation amplitudes under a dynamic range of intraocular pressures. Pulse amplitudes were calculated using harmonic regression analysis. The pulse wave attenuation was measured under different ranges of ophthalmodynamometric force (ODF) as a function of distance along the vessel (VDist), which in turn was calculated in disc diameters (DD) from the center of the optic disc. A linear mixed-effects model with randomized slopes and intercepts was used to estimate the correlations between the logarithmically transformed harmonic regression wave amplitude (HRWa) and the Fourier trigonometric coefficients with the predictors (VDist and ODF). The retinal venous harmonic regression wave attenuation (coefficient value±standard error) -0.40±0.065/DD, (p-value < 0.00001, 95% confidence interval (CI) -0.53 to -0.27), which was approximately twice that of the arterial -0.17±0.048/DD, (p-value < 0.0004, 95% CI = -0.27 to -0.08). There was a positive correlation between attenuation of the harmonic regression wave and ophthalmodynamometric force in both vascular systems. The attenuation of all but the sine coefficient of the second Fourier harmonic (bn2) achieved statistical significance in the correlation with VDist. The cosine coefficient of the first Fourier harmonic an1 was the only coefficient to achieve statistical significance in the correlation with the predictors VDist and ODF in both vascular systems. The an1 coefficient value in the correlation with VDist was -3.79±0.78 and -1.269±0.37 (p < 0.0006), while this coefficient value in the correlation with ODF was 0.026±0.0099 and 0.009±0.04 (p < 0.01) in both the retinal veins and arteries respectively. The predictable attenuation characteristics in normal subjects suggest that this technique may allow the non-invasive quantification of retinal vascular compliance and other hemodynamic parameters.

[Introduction] Introduction The vascular pulse wave is a mechanical pressure wave propagating in the wall of the blood vessel at a velocity different to that of blood flow but physiologically coupled to flow through the radially directed transmural pressure and a shear stress force, which is oriented in a parallel direction to the blood vessel wall [ 1 , 2 ]. The retinal venous pulse is a useful parameter in the management of ophthalmic and systemic disorders such as glaucoma [ 3 – 5 ], increased intracranial pressure [ 6 , 7 ], thyroid orbitopathy [ 8 ], retinal vein occlusion [ 9 – 12 ] and diabetic retinopathy [ 13 , 14 ]. The physiologic mechanisms of the retinal venous pulse are poorly understood; being generated by the heart and transmitted via the cerebrospinal fluid space, it propagates in the wall of the central retinal vein in a retrograde direction to blood flow, entering the eye through the optic cup, where it attenuates (decays in amplitude) rapidly [ 13 , 15 ]. The arterial pulse is the result of a wave of vascular wall distention, initiated by the impact of the stroke volume ejected into a closed system [ 16 ]. Amplification of the arterial pulse wave contour at points downstream is attributable to vessel wall taper. Further modification of the pulse wave contour occurs as a consequence of pulse wave reflection from junctions and discontinuities in the arterial tree [ 17 ]. In addition, there may be a frequency-dependent attenuation of the vascular pulse wave amplitude and propagation velocity by the mural viscoelastic elements. Vascular compliance is the main factor determining pulse wave attenuation through a positively correlated relationship (i.e. the higher the compliance, the higher the pulse wave attenuation) [ 18 – 21 ]. Compliance varies with vessel wall viscoelasticity and tension in the wall of the blood vessel per unit length (T); expressed through Laplace’s law [ 2 , 22 – 25 ] as a product of the transmural pressure (p tm ) and the radius (r) of the blood vessel ( T = p tm ⋅ r ). To date the Dynamic Vessel Analyzer is the only commercially available method to measure the retinal vascular pulse wave properties synchronized with the cardiac cycle, which theoretically can measure retinal vascular pulse wave attenuation [ 26 – 28 ]. In principle it assesses retinal vessel diameter by analyzing the brightness profile of the vessel using video sequences obtained with a conventional fundus camera. To achieve an optimum contrast for vessel visualization, a green filter is inserted into the illumination pathway of the fundus camera. It is available in two versions: the retinal vessel analyzer (RVA), which is designed for research purposes and the dynamic vessel analyzer (DVA), intended for clinical use and includes a capacity to provide flicker light stimulation as a provocative test. Its adaptive algorithm, based on variation in brightness, compensates for reflections during measurement and, additionally, the instrument can automatically compensate for artifactual movements. Images deemed to be of poor quality are automatically rejected from the analysis. As with any optical device measurement accuracy is degraded with optical opacity and in patients with limited fixation. However, the main limitations include the inability to perform measurements where vessels are in close proximity or when the vessel cross-sectional diameter is <90μm [ 29 ]. Retinal vessel diameters are reported in relative units of measurement as determined by the Gullstrand eye, which can result in scaling errors if the eye has a refractive error or its dimensions vary from that of a schematic eye. Although provocative tests are possible with the DVA, there is limited data on the reproducibility of flicker responses [ 30 ]. Chen et al. described an imaging system consisting of a standard Zeiss 30° fundus camera with monochromatic red-free photographs, synchronized with the cardiac cycle using an ECG monitor connected to the camera through a time delay switch, which was set to release the camera shutter at a chosen period within 1/8th of the cardiac cycle. The changes in retinal vascular diameter were measured manually at consecutive intervals. They concluded that both retinal arterial and venous diameters underwent a change concomitant with the events of the cardiac cycle. The arterial diameter reached a maximum in mid systole, which is just after the period of peak aortic pressure, whereas the increase in intraocular pressure during systole, lead to a reduction in the retinal venous distending pressure and consequently resulted in a passive reduction of the venous diameter [ 31 ]. Limitations of this method include the manual technique used to measure vessel diameters at the same site in different image sequences and, in addition, only a single point in each vessel was examined [ 31 ]. Kumar et al. modified this technique using an image analysis method coined “Vesselness Mapping of Retinal Image Sequence”, which detected and filtered vessel boundaries enhanced by the scale-space analysis of the eigenvectors of the image Hessian matrix. This technique was used to examine a longer segment of the vessel and, consequently, several pulsatile features other than spontaneous venous pulsations were detected including serpentine movements, vessel displacement and mechanical coupling. However, identification of the pulsatile segment was required before measurement [ 32 ]. Moret et al. used near-infrared slit-lamp ophthalmoscopy (HRA-OCT Spectralis) and applied principal component analysis to evaluate the image sequence. They also reported the detection of various forms of vascular pulsation including serpentine movement of the principal arteries, spontaneous venous pulsation, pulsatile movement of the entire optic nerve head, vessel displacement and mechanical coupling. Although the near-infrared illuminating light beam precluded the need for mydriasis, the advantage of this technique was offset by a low frame rate of 9 frames/second [ 33 ]. Recently swept-source optical coherence tomography was used to measure the retinal vascular axial expansion during the cardiac cycle, by analyzing the increase of the retinal thickness caused by the reversible expansion of the vessels. Using this method, Spahr et al. reported the detection of a separate retinal arterial and venous pulse wave. They detected the time delay between the two waves and estimated the pulse wave velocity in the retinal arteries to be 620±50 mm/s [ 34 ]. This is about 1,500 times faster than that measured using the RVA [ 27 ]. However, the technique failed to measure the retinal vein pulse wave velocity [ 34 ]. Photoplethysmography (PPG) is a non-invasive optical technique used to detect blood volume changes in tissue microvascular networks. The basic form of PPG technology consists of a light source to illuminate the tissue, and a photodetector to measure the variations in the intensity of transmitted or reflected light associated with changes in perfusion in the tissue volume of interest [ 35 ]. PPG technology has been used in a wide range of commercially available medical devices including the measurement of oxygen saturation [ 36 ], blood pressure [ 37 – 39 ], cardiac output [ 40 , 41 ], the assessment of autonomic function [ 42 , 43 ] and the detection of peripheral vascular disease [ 44 – 46 ]. The principle of operation is described by the Beer-Lambert Law, which describes the relationship between a uniform medium containing an absorbing substance and light attenuation [ 36 ]. We modified this system to objectively measure vessel pulsation amplitude from sectors of retinal images centered on the optic disc and from optic disc surface vessels, using retinal video recordings spanning three cardiac cycles [ 47 , 48 ]. Modified photo-plethysmography (mPPG) allows the estimation of the attenuation of the retinal vascular pulse wave in both the retinal arteries and veins, within one disc diameter of the optic disc. In addition to being non-invasive and low cost, it offers further advantages by continuously measuring both the arterial and venous pulsation amplitudes separately. The transparent ocular refractive media allows mapping and display of the spatial distribution of the mPPG data in two dimensions. Pulse oximetry, recorded simultaneously with the optic nerve video recording, provides the necessary audio feedback, which is then used for the extracted rasterized image frames to be timed with the cardiac cycle. Time-series analysis is then used to quantify the retinal vascular pulse wave parameters. Additionally, ophthalmodynamometry adds a dynamic aspect, by altering the retinal vascular pulse amplitude over a range of intraocular pressures [ 47 – 49 ]. The purpose of the study was to estimate the retinal vascular attenuation non-invasively and provide the response of the attenuation characteristics to changes with induced intraocular pressure in a group of normal subjects.

[introduction] Introduction The vascular pulse wave is a mechanical pressure wave propagating in the wall of the blood vessel at a velocity different to that of blood flow but physiologically coupled to flow through the radially directed transmural pressure and a shear stress force, which is oriented in a parallel direction to the blood vessel wall [ 1 , 2 ]. The retinal venous pulse is a useful parameter in the management of ophthalmic and systemic disorders such as glaucoma [ 3 – 5 ], increased intracranial pressure [ 6 , 7 ], thyroid orbitopathy [ 8 ], retinal vein occlusion [ 9 – 12 ] and diabetic retinopathy [ 13 , 14 ]. The physiologic mechanisms of the retinal venous pulse are poorly understood; being generated by the heart and transmitted via the cerebrospinal fluid space, it propagates in the wall of the central retinal vein in a retrograde direction to blood flow, entering the eye through the optic cup, where it attenuates (decays in amplitude) rapidly [ 13 , 15 ]. The arterial pulse is the result of a wave of vascular wall distention, initiated by the impact of the stroke volume ejected into a closed system [ 16 ]. Amplification of the arterial pulse wave contour at points downstream is attributable to vessel wall taper. Further modification of the pulse wave contour occurs as a consequence of pulse wave reflection from junctions and discontinuities in the arterial tree [ 17 ]. In addition, there may be a frequency-dependent attenuation of the vascular pulse wave amplitude and propagation velocity by the mural viscoelastic elements. Vascular compliance is the main factor determining pulse wave attenuation through a positively correlated relationship (i.e. the higher the compliance, the higher the pulse wave attenuation) [ 18 – 21 ]. Compliance varies with vessel wall viscoelasticity and tension in the wall of the blood vessel per unit length (T); expressed through Laplace’s law [ 2 , 22 – 25 ] as a product of the transmural pressure (p tm ) and the radius (r) of the blood vessel ( T = p tm ⋅ r ). To date the Dynamic Vessel Analyzer is the only commercially available method to measure the retinal vascular pulse wave properties synchronized with the cardiac cycle, which theoretically can measure retinal vascular pulse wave attenuation [ 26 – 28 ]. In principle it assesses retinal vessel diameter by analyzing the brightness profile of the vessel using video sequences obtained with a conventional fundus camera. This technique was used to examine a longer segment of the vessel and, consequently, several pulsatile features other than spontaneous venous pulsations were detected including serpentine movements, vessel displacement and mechanical coupling. However, identification of the pulsatile segment was required before measurement [ 32 ]. They also reported the detection of various forms of vascular pulsation including serpentine movement of the principal arteries, spontaneous venous pulsation, pulsatile movement of the entire optic nerve head, vessel displacement and mechanical coupling. Although the near-infrared illuminating light beam precluded the need for mydriasis, the advantage of this technique was offset by a low frame rate of 9 frames/second [ 33 ]. Using this method, Spahr et al. reported the detection of a separate retinal arterial and venous pulse wave. They detected the time delay between the two waves and estimated the pulse wave velocity in the retinal arteries to be 620±50 mm/s [ 34 ]. This is about 1,500 times faster than that measured using the RVA [ 27 ]. However, the technique failed to measure the retinal vein pulse wave velocity [ 34 ]. Photoplethysmography (PPG) is a non-invasive optical technique used to detect blood volume changes in tissue microvascular networks.
