The solid lines represent the best‐fit regression line for the atropine 0.01% (blue) and placebo (red) groups. Shading indicates the tertiles of axial eye growth (c) and myopia progression (d): Slow (green shading), medium/moderate (yellow shading), and fast (red shading) progression. 3.3 COVID ‐19 impact In keeping with the reported primary and secondary outcomes of the trial (Loughman et al., 2023 ), change in subfoveal ChT was significantly different between atropine 0.01% and placebo groups in the high COVID impact group at 24‐ (difference = 9.08 μm, p = 0.035), but not 12‐ (difference = 0.55 μm, p = 0.90) and 18‐month (difference = 6.37 μm, p = 0.14) visits, after adjusting for baseline age, sex, and baseline ChT. Larger treatment effects were observed in the low COVID impact group, with statistically significant differences observed between atropine 0.01% and placebo groups at 12‐ (difference = 24.69 μm, p < 0.001), 18‐ (difference = 13.23 μm, p = 0.044) and 24‐month (difference = 15.71 μm, p = 0.017) visits, after adjusting for baseline age, sex, and baseline ChT (Figure S1 in Supplementary Material). A similar trend was observed for para‐ and perifoveal subfields (Figure S1 Supplementary Material). 3.4 Mediation analysis between atropine 0.01%, axial length, and SER on choroidal thickness changes The mediation analysis demonstrated that atropine 0.01% had a significant direct effect on ChT, contributing 68.3% of the total effect, with the remaining 31.7% mediated through AL changes (Figure 6 ). In SER‐mediated pathway, the direct effect of 0.01% atropine on ChT accounted for 80% of the total effect, while the indirect effect via SER changes contributed 20% (Figure 6 ). FIGURE 6 Mediation analysis between atropine 0.01%, axial length (top) and spherical equivalent refraction (SER) (bottom) on choroidal thickness (ChT). 3.5 Factors associated with two‐year changes in choroidal thickness, SER , and axial length Assignment to the atropine 0.01% group ( β = 0.815; 95% CI: 0.045 to 1.588; p = 0.039) was significantly associated with an increase in subfoveal ChT over the 24‐month period. Within the atropine group, the treatment compliance rate was not associated with changes in subfoveal ChT ( p = 0.36). A thicker baseline ChT ( β = −0.198; 95% CI: −0.259 to −0.139; p < 0.001), older age at baseline ( β = −0.174; 95% CI: −0.330 to −0.016; p = 0.03), longer baseline axial length ( β = −0.394; 95% CI: −0.750 to −0.049; p = 0.023), and two‐year changes in AL ( β = −6.223; 95% CI: −7.364 to −5.082; p < 0.001) were significantly associated with greater thinning of subfoveal ChT over the 2 years (Table S2 in Supplementary Material). Change in ChT was negatively associated with axial elongation and positively associated with spherical equivalent change, such that each 10 μm increase in ChT over a 2year period was associated with an additional 0.014 mm reduction in AL growth ( β = −0.014; 95% CI: −0.017 to −0.011; p < 0.001), and 0.012D reduction in myopia progression ( β = 0.012; 95% CI: 0.007 to 0.017; p < 0.001) (Table S2 in Supplementary Material). Subgroup analyses showed that these ChT associations were consistent for the atropine 0.01% and placebo groups, separately (Tables S3 and S4 in Supplementary Material).

[discussion] 4 DISCUSSION The key findings of the current study are that myopic participants treated with 0.01% atropine eye drops for 2 years showed a stable ChT compared with placebo‐treated children, who demonstrated progressive macular choroidal thinning over the 2‐year study period. These ChT changes related negatively and positively to the degree of AL and myopic progression, respectively. Myopic participants, irrespective of the treatment group, who demonstrated slower axial eye growth and myopia progression exhibited choroidal thickening whereas those with faster axial eye growth and myopia progression exhibited choroidal thinning. Atropine 0.01% impacted ChT through both direct and indirect pathways involving AL and SER changes. The significant direct effect on ChT suggests a pharmacological action of atropine on the choroid, possibly via muscarinic receptor modulation or vascular effects (Xu et al., 2023 ), which may help mitigate risks associated with choroidal thinning in progressive myopia. Therefore, the choroid may be of biological significance in participants on atropine treatment given that there is clinical value in preventing choroidal thinning, irrespective of its association with myopia progression, due to its link with macular disease. Compared with our findings, previous studies have reported equivocal results about the effect of 0.01% atropine eye drops on ChT in myopic children. Hao and Zhao (Hao & Zhao, 2021 ) reported an increase (mean ± SE; 8.09 ± 1.47 μm) in subfoveal ChT after 12 months of 0.01% atropine eye drops, higher than observed at the same time point in our study (mean ± SE; 1.28 ± 2.26 μm). Our result is similar to a 2‐year clinical trial in Hong Kong, where Yam and colleagues (Yam, Jiang, et al. 2022 ) reported stable (mean ± SD; −0.30 ± 27.15 μm) subfoveal ChT in myopic children treated with 0.01% atropine eye drops for 2 years. However, since their study did not have a placebo group for the second year of the study, it is difficult to know the relative choroidal thickening effect of 0.01% atropine at 24 months. The Hong Kong study also showed atropine dose‐dependent choroidal thickening, with 0.05% and 0.025% atropine eye drops exhibiting greater choroidal thickening effects similar to the dose‐dependent myopia treatment efficacy (i.e. atropine 0.05% and 0.025% had greater treatment effects on AL and SER, compared with 0.01% atropine) they had earlier reported (Yam, Zhang, et al. 2022 ). An overall relatively stable ChT was observed in the 0.01% atropine group over the 24‐month period in our study. Conversely, the placebo group exhibited consistent thinning throughout the 2‐year period. A recent study has also demonstrated that children treated with topical atropine for myopia control for 2–4 years have thicker choroids and less tessellated fundi 10–20 years later when compared with untreated children (Li et al., 2024 ). This suggests a possible long‐term benefit of the short‐term changes observed in our study. Despite atropine 0.01% causing a stable ChT over the 2‐year period in this cohort of myopic participants, our analyses suggest that the effect of atropine 0.01% on ChT may result from a combined influence of atropine 0.01% and slow axial eye growth/myopia progression, rather than only a direct, independent effect of atropine on the choroid as shown in the mediation analysis. Notably, children who exhibited the slowest and fastest axial eye growth and myopia progression also demonstrated choroidal thickening and thinning, respectively, over the 2‐year period, regardless of treatment group. Furthermore, larger myopia treatment effects were observed in the low COVID‐19 impact participants (Loughman et al., 2023 ), who also exhibited the greatest ChT effects. Conversely, high COVID‐19 impact participants who demonstrated poor atropine treatment response (Loughman et al., 2023 ), particularly in the first year of treatment, also exhibited choroidal thinning in both atropine 0.01% and placebo treatment groups. Taken together, these findings suggest that the ChT effect was driven by the combination of a direct atropine effect on the choroid as well as the degree of axial eye growth and myopia progression, which was reduced by atropine 0.01% treatment, consistent with earlier reports that choroidal thinning may be linked with axial elongation during active stages of myopia progression (Read et al., 2015 , 2019 ). Alternatively, short‐term signalling‐related changes in ChT, documented in human (Li et al., 2019 ) and animal studies (Troilo et al., 2000 ), have been noted to occur prior to axial elongation and tend to diminish over time, highlighting the need for long‐term investigations into the potential pathological implications of ChT changes and axial elongation. Our findings are important from a mechanistic perspective and have significant clinical implications given that a thin choroid has been identified as an independent risk factor for myopic maculopathy (Li et al., 2021 ). Therefore, as choroidal thinning is associated with myopic maculopathy, our results suggest that any myopia treatment that limits axial elongation and myopia progression could lead to slowing choroidal thinning and potentially mitigate the risk of developing myopic maculopathy or limit disease severity. As part of the 3rd year of follow‐up of the MOSAIC, we will assess how the use of a higher concentration (0.05%) impacts ChT and whether group differences in ChT are maintained after ceasing atropine 0.01% eye drops. Differential treatment effects among ethnicities may be attributed to differences in baseline characteristics between White and non‐White participants (mean age: 11.4 vs. 11.0 years, p = 0.036; AL: 24.9 mm vs. 24.7 mm, p = 0.024), as well as differences in ocular pigmentation that affect drug absorption. Higher melanin levels in darker eyes, such as in non‐White participants, may affect atropine penetration due to its high melanin‐binding affinity (German et al., 1999 ), potentially reducing its effects on ChT. Additionally, myopia control efficacy was significant in participants with lighter‐coloured irides, particularly blue, compared with those with brown eyes (Loughman et al., 2023 ). COVID‐19‐related behaviours and differences in baseline characteristics likely impacted ChT changes between the high and low COVID‐19 impact groups. The high COVID‐19 impact group was older (mean age = 11.60 vs. 10.74 years, p < 0.001), had longer AL (mean = 24.93 vs. 24.60 mm, p < 0.001), and had more myopia (mean SER = −3.45 D vs. −3.16 D, p = 0.003), all of which are factors associated with thinner choroids. Furthermore, severe behavioural restrictions during the initial lockdown could have contributed to greater myopia progression and choroidal thinning in the high COVID‐19 impact group, explaining the smaller treatment effects in this group compared with the low COVID‐19 impact group. Our atropine and placebo subgroup analyses (Table S4 in Supplementary Material) provide interesting insights into the role of ChT in AL progression. Participants, particularly those in the placebo group, with thicker baseline ChT exhibited slower AL progression relative to those with thinner baseline ChT, consistent with a previous longitudinal study in France (Fontaine et al., 2017 ). This suggests that thicker choroids at baseline could potentially be considered a protective factor against AL progression. Thus, monitoring ChT at an early stage could be used as a predictive marker and possibly as a diagnostic tool to identify individuals at higher risk of experiencing significant axial eye growth to tailor treatment approaches for those at higher risk of progression. Future studies should provide ChT normative data for emmetropic and non‐progressive myopic patients to understand thresholds for at‐risk patients. Although the nasal and supero‐temporal choroid were thinnest and thickest, respectively, at baseline (Kobia‐Acquah et al., 2023 ), the greatest thinning and least treatment effect over the 2 years was observed in the perifoveal supero‐temporal region (Figure 4 ). The greater peripheral thinning observed may reflect that the superior and temporal choroid were generally thicker than other regions; indeed, the relative changes in perifoveal temporal vs. nasal ChT at 24 months is approximately similar (4.7% and 4.2%, respectively). This may, however, have clinical implications given that diseases, such as posterior staphyloma, and myopic maculopathy typically result from mechanical stretching of the posterior pole, with the choroid overlying the temporal macular region undergoing more significant thinning and structural changes compared with the nasal macular region in myopic maculopathy (Moon et al., 2023 ; Park et al., 2021 ). In addition, recent evidence has advanced the dynamic role of choroidal blood vessel mechanisms in choroidal thinning, such that localised alterations in vascular perfusion, particularly in the perifoveal macular regions may be responsible for myopia‐related disease pathogenesis (Li et al., 2022 ; Zhou et al., 2021 ). Our study represents one of the first long‐term investigations of the effects of low‐dose atropine eye drops on ChT in a predominantly White, European population. Previous longitudinal studies, mostly in Asian populations, investigating long‐term atropine effects have only analysed subfoveal ChT. Therefore, a further strength of our study is that it provides evidence of the long‐term changes of para‐ and perifoveal ChT macular regions in response to 0.01% atropine eye drops. Another notable strength of our study was the combined use of automatic segmentation and manual review of segmentation boundaries to ensure accurate choroidal delineation. There were some limitations to this study. For instance, lack of strict control over external factors including intake of caffeine, water, exercise, body position, outdoor activities, and near‐work tasks, could have potentially impacted the ChT measurements. However, it is unlikely that factors, such as exercise and near‐work exposure significantly affected the outcomes, given participants' minimal engagement in intense near‐work activities and limited movement as they were confined to examination rooms during study visits. Furthermore, such factors would not be expected to correlate with participants masked treatment status.

[conclusion] 5 CONCLUSION Myopic participants treated with 0.01% atropine displayed stable ChT over the 2‐year study period, whereas the placebo group showed consistent thinning. The impact of atropine 0.01% treatment on the choroid was not fully explained by changes in AL or SER, indicating a direct effect of atropine on ChT. As ChT is inversely related to diseases such as myopic maculopathy, these findings suggest that a positive treatment efficacy of atropine 0.01% on the choroid and in slowing axial eye growth and myopia progression could potentially mitigate the risk of developing myopic maculopathy.

retrieved_papers.title: Association between serous macular detachment and choroidal vascular architecture in treatment-Naïve branch retinal vein occlusion.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] Branch retinal vein occlusion (BRVO) is a common retinal vascular disorder that can cause visual impairment through macular edema and ischemia. The primary aim of this study was to investigate choroidal structural changes in treatment-naïve BRVO patients. The secondary aim was to assess the impact of serous macular detachment (SMD) on choroidal parameters.
This single-center, retrospective cross-sectional study included 88 patients with unilateral BRVO. Eyes with BRVO, fellow eyes, and age- and sex-matched controls were classified as Groups 1, 2, and 3, respectively; Group 1 was further subdivided by SMD presence. Choroidal assessment included best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA), central macular thickness (CMT), subfoveal and parafoveal choroidal thicknesses-subfoveal choroidal thickness (SFCT), subfoveal nasal choroidal thickness (SFNCT), subfoveal temporal choroidal thickness (SFTCT), submacular nasal choroidal thickness (SMNCT), and submacular temporal choroidal thickness (SMTCT)-and choroidal vascularity index (CVI). Group comparisons used Student's t-test or Mann-Whitney U test for two groups, and one-way ANOVA or Kruskal-Wallis test for three groups. Correlations were analyzed using Pearson or Spearman coefficients. p < 0.05 was considered significant.
Group 1 (BRVO eyes) showed significantly lower subfoveal and submacular CVI values compared with Groups 2 (fellow eyes) and 3 (controls) (p < 0.001). SFCT was significantly higher in Group 1 than in Groups 2 and 3 (p < 0.001 and p = 0.004, respectively). Within Group 1, eyes with SMD had lower BCVA and subfoveal CVI than those without SMD (p = 0.003 and p = 0.009, respectively).
Our findings suggest that in BRVO, the presence of SMD may be associated with reduced subfoveal CVI (SFCVI), indicating potential choroidal vascular alterations that may vary according to the type of macular edema. These observations warrant further investigation and may guide future, more comprehensive studies to better understand BRVO pathophysiology.

retrieved_papers.title: Late Postoperative Evaluation of Retinal and Choroidal Thickness and Retinal Vessel Caliber after Surgical Repair of Corneal Perforation.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2015]
[Abstract] To examine the late period retinal and choroidal alterations in the posterior pole of eyes that underwent primary suturing due to traumatic corneal perforation.
This cross-sectional case series included 21 eyes of 21 patients. The fellow eyes served as the control group. Macular thickness, peripapillary retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) thickness, choroidal thickness, and retinal vessel caliber measurements were performed by spectral-domain optical coherence tomography (SD-OCT).
The mean RNFL thickness was 102.1±10.9 µm in the perforated eyes and 99.5±8.5 µm in the fellow eyes (p=0.29). The mean central macular thickness was 300.1±25.6 µm in the perforated eyes and 295.6±23.2 µm in the fellow eyes (p=0.62). The choroidal thickness and retinal vascular caliber measurements were also similar between the groups (p>0.05).
Operated traumatic corneal perforations do not cause significant posterior pole retinal and choroidal SD-OCT thickness changes in the late postoperative period.

[Introduction] INTRODUCTION Corneal perforation is an important cause of ocular morbidity and vision loss, especially in developing countries. 1 Various types of keratitis, trauma and some immune diseases are the main causes of corneal perforation. 2 It is a surgical emergency that requires early repair of the wound to prevent possible blindness. There are several methods for the treatment of corneal perforation, including primary suturing, application of a bandage contact lens, gluing, amniotic membrane transplantation, the use of conjunctival flaps, and keratoplasty. 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 If not properly managed, corneal perforation can lead to complications such as synechiae, glaucoma, cataract and endophthalmitis. 1 , 4 Even after successfully operated corneal perforations, the visual quality and acuity of the patients do not reach the level of the fellow eyes. The reasons for low visual acuity are mainly irregular astigmatism and corneal opacities. Since the main problem is in the cornea, detailed macular and optic nerve examinations are sometimes underestimated. In this study, we wanted to examine the posterior pole of patients who underwent operations due to corneal perforation. We performed our detailed posterior pole retinal and choroidal examinations after the removal of the corneal sutures in order to lessen astigmatism and increase measurement quality. Our aim was to reveal whether chronic macular edema, posterior pole atrophy, choroidal alterations, or any other retinal diseases occurred in the late stage of operated corneal perforation. We wanted to identify the late period retinal problems that might contribute to the low visual quality of these eyes.

[introduction] INTRODUCTION Corneal perforation is an important cause of ocular morbidity and vision loss, especially in developing countries. 1 Various types of keratitis, trauma and some immune diseases are the main causes of corneal perforation. 2 It is a surgical emergency that requires early repair of the wound to prevent possible blindness. There are several methods for the treatment of corneal perforation, including primary suturing, application of a bandage contact lens, gluing, amniotic membrane transplantation, the use of conjunctival flaps, and keratoplasty. 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 If not properly managed, corneal perforation can lead to complications such as synechiae, glaucoma, cataract and endophthalmitis. 1 , 4 Even after successfully operated corneal perforations, the visual quality and acuity of the patients do not reach the level of the fellow eyes. The reasons for low visual acuity are mainly irregular astigmatism and corneal opacities. Since the main problem is in the cornea, detailed macular and optic nerve examinations are sometimes underestimated. In this study, we wanted to examine the posterior pole of patients who underwent operations due to corneal perforation. We performed our detailed posterior pole retinal and choroidal examinations after the removal of the corneal sutures in order to lessen astigmatism and increase measurement quality. Our aim was to reveal whether chronic macular edema, posterior pole atrophy, choroidal alterations, or any other retinal diseases occurred in the late stage of operated corneal perforation. We wanted to identify the late period retinal problems that might contribute to the low visual quality of these eyes.

[methods] MATERIALS AND METHODS Twenty-six patients who underwent surgical repair of corneal perforation not extending to the sclera were initially included. From these, five patients with low-quality optical coherence tomography (OCT) measurements were excluded. In total, 21 eyes of 21 patients who underwent surgery due to traumatic corneal perforations 3 mm or larger in size were evaluated. The fellow eyes of these patients were accepted as controls. The design of the study was a cross-sectional case series. The examinations were performed in a single tertiary referral center between 2012 and 2014. The study was conducted in accordance with the ethical standards of the Declaration of Helsinki and was approved by the Institutional Ethics Committee. Study Population All of the patients underwent surgery to repair unilateral traumatic corneal perforations. The fellow eyes of the patients were healthy and served as the control group. The patients had open globe injuries only in Zone 1 according to the open globe injury classification system (i.e. cornea and limbus). 7 Raw points in the ocular trauma score ranged from 66 to 76 for all the traumatized eyes. 7 The patients who had corneal scars that impeded adequate retinal visualization were excluded. Scleral perforations were excluded, since the choroid, retina and vitreous were usually injured during the scleral perforation. Patients who had persistent high intraocular pressure (IOP) and/or endophthalmitis after surgery were also excluded. The patients had no systemic diseases and no history of ocular surgery in either eye before the corneal perforation repair. Surgical Techniques All the operations were performed under general anesthesia within the first 24 hours after the ocular injury. Perforations were managed successfully with primary closure after maintaining the anterior chamber with viscoelastic substance. Glues, conjunctival flaps, amniotic membrane transplantation and corneal transplantation were not used in any of the patients. Corneal suturing was performed with 10/0 nylon sutures. In cases of anterior lens capsule rupture and cataract formation, lens removal was done by phacoemulsification in the first two weeks after the trauma. The corneal sutures were removed 3 to 4 months after the corneal wound repair. Measurement Techniques The examinations were performed in the late postoperative period (between 6 and 18 months after the operations). The rationale for this was to wait until corneal suture removal and corneal remodeling, since our aim was to find the reasons for low visual quality other than anterior segment problems (i.e., chronic macular edema, retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL) atrophy). Spectral-domain OCT (Spectralis, Heidelberg, Germany) was used to analyze macular thickness and peripapillary RNFL thickness. In order to measure peripapillary choroidal thickness (PCT), a 360-degree 3.4 mm diameter peripapillary circle scan was performed using the standard protocol for RNFL evaluation, and only nasal PCT values were recorded for analysis. PCT was measured manually from the outer part of the hyperreflective line corresponding to the RPE to the inner part of the sclera ( Figure 1 ). For retinal vascular caliber (RVC) analysis, retinal arterioles and venules passing through an area one-half to one-disc diameter from the optic disc margin were measured, and vessel calibers of the largest eight were selected for the analysis ( Figure 2 ). Statistical Analysis For statistical analysis, SPSS 17.0 software for Windows (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA) was used to analyze outcomes. Level of significance was accepted as α=0.05. The Mann-Whitney U test was used for comparison of studied parameters for the corneal perforated and fellow eyes. The Bonferroni correction was made when the patients with lens extraction were taken as a separate group.

[results] RESULTS The mean age of the patients was 30.7±21.2 years (range, 6-75 years). The mean time interval between the corneal perforation operations and the posterior pole examinations was 11.1±5.0 months (range, 6-18 months). The etiologic agents of the corneal perforations were metallic objects in 10 eyes, wooden objects in 5 eyes, sharp edges of stones in 3 eyes, plastic objects in 2 eyes and glass particles in 1 eye. Some of the demographic and clinical characteristics of the patients are shown in Table 1 . The distribution of wound lengths was as follows: 3 mm in 3 patients, 4 mm in 9 patients, 5 mm in 5 patients, 6 mm in 2 patients and 7 mm in 2 patients. Six patients had anterior lens capsule rupture at presentation and underwent phacoemulsification within 2 weeks of the trauma. At the final visit, the mean best corrected visual acuity (BCVA) was 0.28±0.22 logMAR in the perforated eyes and 0.005±0.02 logMAR in the fellow (control) eyes (p<0.001). All the operated eyes had irregular astigmatism ranging from 1.50 to 8.00 diopters. Segmental peripapillary RNFL thickness (inferior, superior, nasal and temporal) measurements are shown in Table 2 . There was no statistically significant interocular difference in any of the RNFL quadrants. The mean RNFL thickness was 102.1±10.9 µm in the perforated eyes and 99.5±8.5 µm in the fellow eyes (p=0.29). The mean IOP was 14.0±2.7 mmHg in the perforated eyes and 13.7±1.7 mmHg in the fellow eyes (p=0.92). The mean central macular thickness was 300.1±25.6 µm in the perforated eyes and 295.6±23.2 µm in the fellow eyes (p=0.62). None of the patients had cystoid macular edema, RNFL atrophy, or any other retinal abnormalities. The PCT and RVC measurements are shown in Table 3 . The choroidal thickness and retinal arteriolar and venular caliber values were similar in the perforated eyes and the fellow eyes. We repeated all the analyses after excluding the patients with lens perforation, and again there were no statistically significant differences between the perforated and fellow eyes in the studied parameters (RNFL, macular thickness, PCT, RVC) (p>0.05). Also, there were no statistically significant differences when the patients who underwent traumatic cataract surgery were taken as a separate group (p>0.05).

[discussion] DISCUSSION Our results show that operated clear corneal and limbal perforations do not cause chronic macular edema, peripapillary RNFL atrophy, choroidal thickness changes, or RVC alterations in the late post-operative period. Of course, this statement is valid only for uncomplicated corneal perforations that do not include posterior segment damage initially. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report in the literature related to the impact of corneal perforation on posterior pole structures. Although corneal perforation primarily affects the anterior segment of the eye, it is possible that retinal damage occurs by several mechanisms. In perforating corneal injuries, concomitant choroidal detachment was frequently seen, and this would have an impact on retinal morphology and function. 4 Also, hypotony is common in corneal perforations both before and after surgical repair, so it is possible that hypotony maculopathy and its sequelae might occur to some extent. Since OCT is accepted as the best method for evaluating the posterior pole, we performed OCT in both eyes of the patients. Our rationale for investigating RNFL was that optic nerve axonal loss is possible after severe ocular traumas, and this damage is shown clearly with spectral-domain OCT. 8 The other mechanisms of peripapillary RNFL damage due to corneal perforation might be mechanical distortion of the eyeball during the injury causing optic nerve head damage, IOP fluctuations after the trauma, release of inflammatory mediators due to trauma and the effects of possible posterior pole edema. These factors might cause peripapillary RNFL atrophy in the chronic period. Potential applications of OCT in posterior segment trauma have been reported in the literature, 9 but there is lack of research about the indirect effects of anterior segment penetrating injuries on the retina using OCT. In this study, we did not observe any retinal abnormalities like cystoid macular edema, retinoschisis, retinal tears or macular holes in the late postoperative period. The macular thickness values of the perforated eyes and the fellow eyes were similar. Also, the peripapillary RNFL thickness of the perforated eyes did not differ much from that of the fellow eyes. Singh et al. 4 reported that choroidal detachment was present in nearly half of eyes with perforated corneal ulcers, occurring more frequently in larger perforations, and that it resolved after hypotony ended or by surgical drainage. It should be expected that some choroidal alterations as well as retinal vascular changes might occur in the late postoperative period of corneal perforation surgery. However, we did not find significant differences between the perforated eyes and the fellow eyes with respect to choroidal thickness or RVC. The selection of an appropriate wound repair technique is mostly based on the size and location of the corneal perforation. If the corneal wound caused by a perforating injury is more than 3 mm in length, suturing is required. 2 Gluing, bandage contact lens or amniotic membrane transplantation can be used for corneal perforations of 3 mm or less. 10 , 11 , 12 Since the length of the wound was ≥3 mm in all the cases and concomitant iris prolapse was present in most of the cases, we performed only suturing technique with 10/0 nylon. We did not perform keratoplasty in the follow-up periods because the corneal scars were not on the visual axis. In terms of corrected final visual acuity, self-sealing corneal injuries had a better prognosis, whereas corneal perforations requiring surgical repair usually led to poor vision. 13 Visual outcome following treatment of traumatic corneal perforation may not be optimal due to the presence of irregular astigmatism. Titiyal et al. 14 reported that rigid gas-permeable contact lenses are better than spectacles in the visual rehabilitation of eyes that have scars caused by perforating corneal injuries. Unfortunately, we did not prescribe rigid contact lenses to our patients. Visual adaptation may occur with time in some ocular conditions such as trauma. Corneal remodeling and lessening of the astigmatism after suture removal might also have a positive effect on the visual acuity of patients who undergo corneal perforation repair in the chronic period. We waited for six months in order to maintain standardization and to allow time for visual adaptation and corneal remodeling after suture removal. Also, the ocular trauma score system is focused on the interpretation of visual acuity six months after trauma. 15 Our study has several limitations. First of all, the sample size could have been larger. Second, early postoperative retinal and choroidal OCT measurements would have been beneficial. Lastly, visual acuity measurements should have been included after prescribing rigid contact lenses to the patients. In conclusion, operated corneal perforations do not cause statistically significant retinal or choroidal alterations in the late postoperative period as measured by OCT. This statement does not mean that detailed retinal examination is not necessary after successfully operated corneal perforations. As expected, the low visual quality in the perforated eyes seems to be related to corneal problems. Further studies including corneal-scleral perforations should also provide insight into that related topic.

[conclusion] Conclusion: Operated traumatic corneal perforations do not cause significant posterior pole retinal and choroidal SD-OCT thickness changes in the late postoperative period.

retrieved_papers.title: Exploration of choroidal vascular changes in juveniles with different degrees of myopia: The application of swept-source optical coherence tomography angiography.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] To investigate the vascular characteristic changes in the macular retina and choriocapillaris, as well as the choroidal vascularization of eyes with different degrees of myopia in juveniles using Swept-source Optical Coherence Tomography (SS-OCT), aiming to understand the pathophysiological characteristics of myopic eyes further.
We conducted a retrospective case-control study involving 103 patients (198 eyes) aged 6-17 years who had undergone SS-OCT examination. Based on equivalent spherical power, they were divided into four groups: emmetropia group, low myopia group, moderate myopia group, and high myopia group. The density of superficial capillary plexuses (SCP), choriocapillaris (CC), choroid vessel (CV)layers, and subfoveal choroidal thickness (SFCT) in the macular area were measured using SS-OCT. Multivariate regression analysis was employed to determine the ocular and demographic factors associated with retinal and choroidal blood flow indicators.
In the temporal parafoveal area, the superficial capillary plexus (SCP) density was significantly lower in the moderate myopia group compared to the low myopia group (47.27 ± 2.21 vs. 48.51 ± 1.8, p = 0.002). Conversely, subfoveal choroidal vessel (CV) density was substantially higher in highly myopic eyes than in emmetropic eyes (56.98± 3.65 vs. 53.70 ± 4.31, p = 0.023). The multiple linear regression analysis showed that in the area under the fovea, thinner choroidal thickness is associated with higher choroidal vessel density. Specifically, as choroidal thickness decreases, the density of choroidal vessels increases (p = 0.024).
For juveniles, with the progression of myopia, the density of retinal superficial blood vessels and choroidal capillaries remains at a relatively stable level, while the choroidal vessel density under the fovea gradually increases. The main cause of changes in choroidal vessel density is the thickness of choroid under the fovea.

retrieved_papers.title: Evaluation of retinal nerve fibre layer, ganglion cell layer and choroidal thickness with optical coherence tomography in migraine patients: a case-control study.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2018]
[Abstract] Evaluation of retinal nerve fibre layer (RNFL), ganglion cell layer (GCL) and choroidal thickness (CT) with optical coherence tomography (OCT) in chronic migraine patients, to compare with healthy controls.
Ninety-four eyes of 47 chronic migraine patients (Group 1) and 68 eyes of 34 healthy individuals (Group 2) were included in this prospective case-control study. The right and left eyes were separately evaluated. Mean peripapillary RNFL thicknesses, mean GCL measured from superior and inferior quadrants, and mean CT were measured at three different regions (central, 500 μm nasal and temporal region of the fovea).
There was no statistically significant differences in RNFL between the two groups (p > 0.05), while CT values were significantly higher and GCL values were significantly lower in chronic migraine groups (p < 0.05). There were no statistically significant differences between migraine duration, frequency and length of attacks, presence of aura, relation to menstrual cycle, white matter lesions in cranial magnetic resonance imaging and RNFL, GCL and CT (p > 0.05).
In this study, we observed chronic migraine disease does not have any effect on peripapillary RNFL thickness; however, increases in CT and decreases in GCL thickness were observed in migraine patients.

[methods] Methods This study is a hospital-based comparative, cross-sectional study conducted in the departments of ophthalmology and neurology of a tertiary care hospital in eastern India from June 2023 to July 2024. Institutional ethical committee clearance was obtained before starting the study (IEC/IMS.SH/SOA/2023/553 dated June 27, 2023). The study adhered to the basic tenets of the Helsinki Declaration. Written informed consent was obtained from each patient before enrolling them in the study. All patients aged between 18 and 50 years diagnosed with migraine as per the International Classification of Headache Disorders, third edition (ICHD-3) classification were included in the study.[ 6 7 ] Healthy age- and sex-matched individuals without any ocular and systemic abnormalities constituted the control group. Patients with the following were excluded from the study: best corrected visual acuity <20/20, N6, spherical or cylindrical errors >±2 D, history of systemic disorders like diabetes, hypertension, anemia, neurologic diseases like multiple sclerosis, Parkinsonism, Alzheimer’s, and ocular diseases like glaucoma, uveitis, significant cataract, retinal and optic nerve disorders. Patients with a past or present history of smoking, history of any previous ocular trauma or surgery, and intake of any systemic medications like isotretinoin, hydroxychloroquine, thioridazine, etc., which could affect the study parameters, were also excluded from the study. After obtaining informed written consent, a detailed history including the patient’s name, age, sex, occupation, address, presenting symptom, progression, and associated symptoms was recorded. A detailed history of the type of headache, duration of the headache, duration of each episode, and laterality was obtained from each patient. Detailed information about the severity of migraine was obtained using the headache impact test-6 (HIT-6) questionnaire.[ 14 ] Best corrected visual acuity was measured using Snellen’s chart. Ishihara’s chart was used to assess color vision. A thorough evaluation of the anterior segment was done in each case using the slit lamp. Intraocular pressure was measured using Goldmann’s applanation tonometer. Pupils of both eyes were dilated using a combination of 0.8% tropicamide and 5% phenylephrine. Posterior segment evaluation was done using an indirect ophthalmoscope and a 20-D lens. Measurement of pRNFL thickness, ONH, and macular parameters was done using the 3D disc, 3D macula, and 3D macula (V) modes of the spectral domain OCT machine (3D OCT-1 Topcon Maestro version 8.3; Topcon, Tokyo, Japan). RNFL and ONH details were obtained using the 3D disc mode having a scan length of 6.0 × 6.0 mm and a resolution of 512 × 128. Macular parameters were studied using the 3D macula (V) and 3D macula modes having a scan length of 7.0 × 7.0 and 6.0 × 6.0 mm, respectively. The resolution of both the above macular scans was 512 × 128. The RNFL parameters studied were average, superior, inferior, nasal, and temporal thicknesses. The ONH parameters included were disc area, cup area, rim area, vertical cup disc ratio, linear cup disc ratio, and rim volume. Central macular thickness (CMT), superior macular RNFL, inferior macular RNFL, superior GCL, and inferior GCL thicknesses were the various macular parameters studied. The thickness calculated in each cell was compared with a normative database that had already been fed into the machine. Values within the normal range were represented as green, values in the borderline as yellow, and values outside the normal limits were shown in red. In our study, migraine patients were divided into episodic and chronic as per the ICHD-3 criteria.[ 6 7 ] According to the HIT-6 score,[ 14 ] the severity of migraine is classified as mild (little-to-no) impact: HIT-6 score: 36–49; moderate impact: HIT-6 score: 50–59; and severe impact: HIT-6 score: 60–78. Statistical analysis All analyses were done using Statistical Package for the Social Sciences software, version 24.0. The analysis included profiling patients using different demographic and clinical parameters. For all statistical purposes, values of the right eye of both study and control groups were used. Descriptive analysis of quantitative parameters was expressed as means and standard deviation. Categorical data was expressed as absolute numbers and percentages. Independent Student’s t -test was used for testing the mean between independent groups, whereas paired Student’s t -test and analysis of variance were used to calculate statistical significance between two or more groups. Cross tables were generated, and the Chi-squared test was used to test associations. P value <0.05 was considered statistically significant.

[results] Results The present study enrolled 200 subjects (100 migraine patients and 100 healthy subjects). The mean age in migraine and control groups was 31.01 ± 8.56 and 30.80 ± 8.65 years, respectively. Most subjects were females (84% in migraine group and 82% in the control group). Of the 100 migraine patients, only three patients had migraine with aura. In 43%, 32%, and 25% of cases, mild, moderate, and severe migraine were seen, respectively. Episodic migraine was seen in 71% of cases, with chronic migraine making up the rest. The mean duration of migraine in study subjects was 6.56 ± 5.08 years. The mean duration of individual attacks was 9.33 ± 13.41 h, and the mean number of migraine attacks each month was 9.31 ± 6.57. Salient demographic details are presented in Table 1 . Table 1 Salient demographic characteristics Variable Migraine ( n =100) Control ( n =100) Age (years) 31.01±8.56 30.80±8.65 Sex Male (16), female (84) Male (18), female (82) Intraocular pressure (mmHg) 14.10±2.61 14.14±2.49 Aura Present (3), absent (97) - Severity of migraine Mild- 43 Moderate- 32 Severe- 25 - Type of migraine Episodic- 71 Chronic- 29 - Headache duration (years) 6.56±5.08 - Individual attack duration (h) 9.33±13.41 - Attack frequency (months) 9.31±6.57 - Analysis of RNFL thickness The average pRNFL thickness in migraine patients and controls was 91.62 ± 15.70 and 106.19 ± 10.46 µm, respectively [ Table 2 ]. Migraine patients had statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinner pRNFL (average and all quadrants) compared to age- and gender-matched healthy controls. Compared to patients with episodic migraine, chronic migraine patients exhibited statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinning in pRNFL (average and all quadrants). pRNFL (average and all quadrants) was found to be statistically thinner ( P < 0.05) in severe migraine patients compared to those with mild and moderate migraine. Compared to migraine patients with a disease duration of less than 5 years, patients with a disease duration of more than 5 years had statistically significant thinning in pRNFL (average and all quadrants). Optic disc area, cup area, vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume were more in migraine patients compared to healthy controls, and this difference was statistically significant ( P < 0.05). All the ONH parameters, that is, optic disc area, cup area, rim area, linear and vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume, were more in chronic migraine patients than in episodic migraine patients. However, this difference was statistically insignificant ( P > 0.05). Optic disc area, cup area, rim area, linear and vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume were more in severe migraine patients than in mild and moderate migraine patients. However, this difference was statistically significant ( P < 0.05) only in optic disc area and rim volume. In patients with migraine for more than 5 years, there was an increase in optic disc area, cup area, rim area, linear and vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume. However, this difference was found to be statistically insignificant ( P > 0.05). Analysis of macular parameters Mean CMT was 207.67 ± 16.92 µm min migraine patients and 228.09 ± 13.58 µm in healthy controls. This difference was found to be statistically significant ( P < 0.05). Migraine patients had statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinner macular parameters, including superior and inferior macular RNFL thicknesses, CMT, and inferior GCL thickness, compared to age- and gender-matched healthy controls. Chronic migraine patients had statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinning in all macular parameters compared to episodic migraine patients. Compared to patients with mild and moderate migraine, those with severe migraine exhibited statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinner macular parameters. Similarly, all macular parameters were found to be significantly ( P < 0.05) thinner in migraine patients with a longer duration of the disease (>5 years). [discussion] Discussion The present study was a hospital-based comparative, cross-sectional study that included 100 migraine patients and 100 healthy subjects. In our study, the mean age in the migraine group was 31.0 ± 8.56 years and in the control group was 30.80 ± 8.65 years ( P > 0.05), which was comparable to the findings reported by Simsek[ 15 ] (study group: 35.7 ± 9.5, control group: 40.9 ± 12.7 years), Gunes et al .[ 13 ] (mean age: 33.05 ± 8.83 years), and Tunç et al .[ 16 ] (migraine group: 30.1 ± 7.36 years). Female preponderance was seen in both groups. Female preponderance has been reported in most of the studies.[ 15 17 ] Females suffer from migraines at about twice the rate of men. Although both biological and psychosocial factors contribute to the sex differences in migraine, it seems that sex hormones (estrogen) are the most important. Migraine attacks are associated with fluctuating hormonal levels. High estrogen levels, high estrogen fluctuations, and hormonal replacement therapy are associated with the worst migraine outcomes.[ 18 ] Compared to the studies performed by Simsek[ 15 ] on 40 migraine patients (18- migraine with aura and 22- migraine without aura) and Kanar et al .[ 19 ] on 77 migraine patients (37- migraine with aura and 40- migraine without aura), our study had a very contrasting feature: only three patients out of 100 had migraine with aura and the rest had migraine without aura. There was no explanation for the discrepancy in the results between our study and other studies, which is a limitation of our study. Due to its simplicity and ease of use, the HIT-6 questionnaire was used to determine the severity of migraine in our study, in contrast to the Migraine disability assessment test (MIDAS) score used in the studies of Simsek[ 15 ] and Kanar et al .[ 19 ] Out of 100 migraine cases, 43% had mild, 32% had moderate, and 25% had severe migraine. Moreover, 71% of the study subjects had episodic migraine. In comparison, 29% had chronic migraine, in contrast to Reggio et al .,[ 12 ] who reported episodic and chronic migraine in 43% and 57% of their cases, respectively. Certain factors like stress, menstrual cycle changes, sleep disturbances, and alcohol trigger migraine attacks. Most likely, most of our patients had an intermittent presence of these predisposing factors, leading to a higher incidence of episodic migraine compared to chronic migraine. The mean duration of migraine in study subjects was comparable to the values reported in the studies of Simsek[ 15 ] (6.2 ± 5.6 years) and Kanar et al .[ 19 ] (8.05 ± 4.7 years). Our study’s mean duration of individual migraine attacks was 9.33 ± 13.41 h. Studies by Reggio et al .[ 12 ] (28.1 ± 22.8 h) and Sorkhabi et al .[ 20 ] (25.2 ± 31.2 h) have reported considerably higher durations of individual migraine attacks. Probable reasons for the shorter duration of the individual migraine attacks in our study can be patients presenting earlier to the neurologist, a correct diagnosis, and early institution of treatment. The higher number of patients with episodic migraine in the present study could be the reason for the lesser mean number of migraine attacks per month (9.31 ± 6.57/month). This is similar to that reported in the study of Demircan et al .[ 21 ] (8.05 ± 3.16/month), but differs from that reported by Sorkhabi et al .[ 20 ] (13.6 ± 10.2/month). Migraine is a neurovascular and progressive disorder. During a migraine attack, there is vasoconstriction of the retrobulbar artery, leading to optic nerve hypoperfusion and, eventually, ganglionic cell death. Kara et al .[ 22 ] used color Doppler imaging to demonstrate that migraine patients had greater vascular resistance in the central retinal and posterior ciliary arteries during both headache and headache-free periods. Supporting this hypothesis, our study showed statistically significant ( P < 0.05) thinner pRNFL (average and all quadrants) in migraine patients compared to age- and sex-matched healthy controls. Martinez et al .[ 11 ] found thinning in the temporal quadrant, Simsek[ 15 ] and Sorkhabi et al .[ 20 ] in the nasal quadrant, Reggio et al .[ 12 ] in the nasal, temporal, and inferior quadrants, while Gunes et al .[ 13 ] and Yülek et al .[ 23 ] found no differences. This discrepancy could be attributed to the use of different OCT machines, racial variations, and the absence of standardization in the inclusion and exclusion criteria of the participants. This study examined ONH parameters since recurring constriction of the retinal and ciliary arteries can cause ischemic damage and changes to the optic disc. We found that optic disc area, cup area, vertical cup disc ratio, and rim volume were higher in migraine patients compared to healthy controls. This difference was statistically significant ( P < 0.05). The results of our study contradicted the findings of Michael et al .,[ 17 ] Martinez et al .,[ 11 ] and Yener and Korucu.[ 24 ] They reported no difference in ONH parameters. This disparity might have arisen from including individuals with supranormal values in the migraine group, which would have affected intergroup comparisons. It can also be attributed to differences in the OCT machines used in different studies. Changes in central retinal artery circulation can alter the inner retinal layers, which may be reflected in macular thickness. In the macular region, ganglionic cells form stratified multicellular layers. Thus, loss of axons due to ischemia will lead to GCL thinning. Macular parameters like superior and inferior RNFL thicknesses, CMT, and inferior GCL thickness were lesser in migraine patients ( P < 0.05) compared to healthy controls. In contrast to the present study, Demircan et al .[ 21 ] and Hamurcu et al .[ 25 ] reported no difference in the mean macular thickness between the migraine and healthy groups. Kanar et al .[ 19 ] found superior and inferior GCL thickness reduction, while Tunç et al .[ 16 ] reported no difference in GCL measurements. CMT in our study group was comparable to that reported in the study by Cankaya and Tecellioglu[ 26 ] (211.07 ± 7.36 µm). Hamurcu et al .[ 25 ] reported no difference in RNFL thickness, CMT, or optic disc area, although the optic disc rim area was larger in the migraine group. In chronic migraine, due to more frequent attacks and resultant episodes of vasospasm and hypoperfusion, there are ischemic changes in the retina and optic nerve. These changes correlate with the number of headache episodes.[ 27 28 ] Our research findings showed that individuals with chronic migraine had a significant ( P < 0.05) decrease in pRNFL thickness (average and all quadrants) and macular parameters, including CMT, superior and inferior RNFL thicknesses, as well as superior and inferior GCL thicknesses, when compared to individuals with episodic migraine. Our findings differ from those of Gunes et al .,[ 13 ] who found no differences in RNFL values, but thinner GCL values. Like the present study, Reggio et al .[ 12 ] have reported thinner RNFL, especially in the superior and inferior sectors, and GCL in chronic migraine patients compared to patients with episodic migraine. In contrast to our findings, a study by Kirbas et al .[ 29 ] reported thinning in superior quadrant RNFL thickness only (no difference in other quadrants RNFL and macular parameters) in patients with chronic migraine (CM). Since migraine is a chronic condition with varying severity, the duration and intensity of the disease may have a degrading effect on RNFL and GCL. The study’s findings revealed that migraine patients with a duration of disease >5 years exhibited thinning in pRNFL and macular parameters. Specifically, all quadrants and average values of pRNFL, as well as superior and inferior GCL thicknesses, superior and inferior macular RNFL thicknesses, and CMT showed statistically significant differences between patients with duration <5 years and >5 years ( P < 0.05). However, the ONH parameters did not show significant changes. The study suggests that as the duration of migraine increases, there is a greater extent of vasoconstriction, impacting blood flow to the choroid and retina. Several studies have reported similar results. Gunes et al .[ 13 ] found an inverse correlation between headache duration and superior and temporal RNFL, Yülek et al .[ 23 ] found an inverse correlation between duration and average RNFL thickness, and Abdellatif and Fouad[ 30 ] found an inverse correlation of GCL thickness with duration. However, the findings of Gipponi et al .,[ 31 ] Kirbas et al .[ 29 ] (no correlation with RNFL), and Cankaya and Tecellioglu[ 26 ] (no correlation with CMT) contradicted our study. Our study found an inverse correlation of severity with pRNFL thickness (average and all quadrants) and macular parameters (CMT, superior and inferior GCL, and superior and inferior RNFL). Similar to our study, Kanar et al .[ 19 ] and Abdellatif and Fouad[ 30 ] found that severity (MIDAS score) was inversely correlated with superior and inferior GCL; the former found a significant inverse correlation with average, superior, and nasal RNFL thicknesses and the latter found a significant inverse correlation with inferior and temporal RNFL thicknesses only. The present study has a few limitations. The study was limited to a specific geographic area, and the results cannot be generalized to migraine patients from other areas. Since the study was conducted in an urban setting at a tertiary institute, the study population might not reflect true representatives of the general population of rural areas. OCT angiography could have helped in supporting our hypothesis of chronic ischemia in migraine being responsible for changes in retinal and ONH parameters. However, choroidal thickness and assessment of retinal vascular plexuses could not be done because of the absence of the requisite technology in our center. We also did not perform visual field analysis in our subjects, the addition of which could have further increased the value of the study. The study is cross sectional in nature; the effect of disease progression on the retinal parameters could not be assessed.

[conclusion] Conclusion The present study reports a significant variation in pRNFL, ONH, and macular parameters in migraine patients compared to age- and gender-matched healthy individuals. Patients with a history of longer duration (>5 years) of migraine had significantly thinner pRNFL. Based on our findings, we recommend careful examination of retinal and ONH parameters in migraine patients at regular intervals to avoid any future complications. Conflicts of interest There are no conflicts of interest.

retrieved_papers.title: The influence of coping strategies and behavior on the physiological response to social stress in women: The role of age and menstrual cycle phase.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2017]
[Abstract] There is information indicating that the variations induced by the menstrual cycle may influence the capacity of young women to respond to stress. The physiological response to stress changes across the stages of the lifespan; however, in spite of the great increase in life expectancy, the way women react after menopause, a period characterized by a dramatic decline in sex hormones, has not been sufficiently studied. The main objective of the study was to examine the capacity to respond to and recover from an acute social stressor in post-menopausal women compared to young women. The second objective was to investigate the consequences of behavior on the self-regulatory systems. We measured behavior, cortisol, and heart rate during a speaking task in front of a committee in sixty-seven women: 36 post-menopausal and 31 pre-menopausal (follicular group n=14; luteal group n=17). No differences in heart rate reactivity between three groups were found. Post-menopausal women showed less cortisol reactivity to stress; they also displayed a higher percentage of Gestures during the speaking task, reflecting a clearer pattern of active coping compared to the young women. In post-menopausal women, behaviors that reflect active coping strategies were related to better autonomic regulation. By contrast, in pre-menopausal women, cortisol changes seemed to be modulated by passive and reactive behaviors such as Submission and Assertion. These results emphasize the importance of considering age and Hormonal Status in coping processes, including reactivity and recovery from stressful situations.

[Introduction] 1. Introduction Major depressive disorder (MDD) represents a major component of disease burden [ 1 ], with a global prevalence of 10–20% [ 2 ]. Due to inadequate targeting of the pathological processes involved [ 3 ], current treatments are at best 50% effective [ 4 ]. The presence of multiple co-morbidities, such as cardiovascular and endocrine disorders as well as other psychiatric disorders [ 5 ], often complicates diagnosis and treatment. In this regard, anxiety and reward-based manifestations, e.g., anhedonia, anxiety, excitability [ 6 , 7 , 8 ], are not only common but have different biological underpinnings [ 3 ]. Animal models with robust translational validity are an important tool in drug discovery [ 3 , 6 , 7 ]. As with humans, appropriate housing conditions are critical for psychosocial wellbeing in rodents [ 7 ]. Poor housing conditions, like wet bedding, a lack of food and water, switching the lights on and off, light–dark cycle reversal, and noise, represent significant stressors for these animals [ 7 ]. Indeed, this lack of care engenders adverse behavioral manifestations akin to those observed in MDD [ 6 , 7 , 8 ]. The unpredictable chronic mild stress (UCMS) model has strong predictive and face validity with respect to the cumulative depressogenic and anxiogenic effects induced by chronic adversity, such as work stress, interpersonal conflicts, financial problems, isolation and loneliness, and/or loss of a loved one, especially if these persist over an extended period [ 9 ]. An example of this is the increased prevalence of MDD and anxiety experienced during and following the COVID-19 pandemic [ 10 , 11 , 12 ]. Therefore, adverse environmental experiences, as experienced in the UCMS rodent model, have strong translational validity for human mental health disorders, such as MDD [ 9 ], and therefore hold value as a preclinical drug discovery tool [ 7 ]. The specific pathophysiological mechanisms underlying the above-mentioned behavioral responses are generally ascribed to a dysregulated stress response. During acute stress, cortisol is released from the hypothalamic pituitary adrenal (HPA) axis to heighten arousal, improve and focus attention and cognition, accelerate motor responses, and mount specific metabolic and anti-inflammatory responses [ 13 , 14 ]. This process is under negative feedback control that maintains homeostasis [ 15 , 16 ]. However, during uncontrolled chronic stress, this negative feedback system is compromised [ 17 ], leading to long-term deleterious effects on the brain. The resulting allostatic load leads to reduced synaptic connectivity, hippocampal shrinkage and dysfunction, chronic neuro-inflammation, and monoamine deficits [ 18 , 19 ]. It is therefore not surprising that MDD invariably presents with hypercortisolemia [ 20 ] and shortfalls in monoaminergic function that contribute to the mentioned cognitive and socio-behavioral impairments [ 3 ]. Chronic inflammation activates the tryptophan–kynurenine pathway [ 21 , 22 ] and increases levels of the glutamate agonist, quinolinic acid, which in turn decreases the tryptophan necessary for serotonin (5-HT) synthesis. In addition, increased oxidative stress and mitochondrial damage increase pro-inflammatory cytokines [ 23 ], e.g., interferon-gamma (IFN-γ) and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α), and the expression of phosphodiesterase 4 (PDE4), the latter hastening cyclic adenosine monophosphate (cAMP) breakdown [ 24 ]. Reduced cAMP impacts adenylate cyclase-dependent receptor signaling, e.g., 5-HT 1A , thereby compromising serotonin and other monoamine receptor pathways [ 3 ]. Reduced cAMP also decreases anti-inflammatory and increases pro-inflammatory cytokine release, thereby worsening the neuro-inflammatory state [ 24 , 25 ]. Importantly, the inhibition of the cAMP pathway decreases brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) expression, important in synaptogenesis and neuroplasticity [ 24 , 26 ] and hypothesized to be involved in the neuro-progression of depressive symptomology [ 27 ]. PDE4 therefore plays a central role in the neuroimmune response to stress and underlies the complex pathophysiology of MDD. The South African succulent plant, Sceletium tortuosum (ST), has shown potential antidepressant and anxiolytic activity, albeit in healthy populations [ 28 ]. ST is associated with various antidepressant-relevant mechanisms, including the upregulation of vesicular monoamine transporter (VMAT) 2 [ 28 ], the inhibition of the 5-HT transporter (SERT), monoamine oxidase (MAO), and PDE4 activity [ 29 ], as well as the modulation of glucocorticoid production [ 30 ]. Said pharmacological actions are attributed to the ST alkaloids, mesembrine (MES), mesembrenol, mesembranol, and mesembrenone, each of which possesses unique pharmacological properties and synergistic potential [ 28 ]. In this regard, MES alone may contribute significantly to the anxiolytic-like effects of ST, while its antidepressant-like activity is likely the result of pharmacological synergism [ 31 ]. While clinical and preclinical (in vivo and in vitro) studies have supported the antidepressant properties of ST [ 28 ], the lack of studies in patients diagnosed with MDD has increased the need for validated animal models to provide the necessary translational evidence and support [ 32 ]. Based on the overlap of the pathophysiological underpinnings of the UCMS rat model with MDD, this study investigates the antidepressant and/or anxiolytic effects of a standardized extract of ST, Zembrin ® (ZEM). Male and female Wistar rats were therefore subjected to a UCMS protocol [ 33 , 34 ], since including both sexes represents a more clinically relevant subject population [ 35 ]. UCMS does not appear to show a consistent sex bias, with studies reporting conflicting results [ 36 , 37 , 38 , 39 ]. With the hippocampus (HC) and frontal cortex (FC) critical in mood regulation and cognitive processing [ 40 ], monoamines, PDE4 expression, oxidative stress, and anti- and pro-inflammatory cytokines were assessed in these brain areas, along with various behavioral parameters akin to MDD. Since MES may be a more significant contributor to the anxiolytic effects of ZEM [ 31 ], MES and the selective SERT inhibitor (SSRI), escitalopram (ESC), were used as reference compounds. Our working hypothesis was that a UCMS protocol would induce depressive-like behavior, in particular, despair, anhedonia-, and anxiety-like symptoms, and cognitive impairment in Wistar rats independent of sex. Moreover, simultaneous changes in brain PDE4, 5-HT, and dopamine (DA) concentrations, as well as inflammation and oxidative stress markers, would provide an important link to causation. These changes would furthermore be reversed by ZEM and MES administration through multitarget involvement, with biobehavioral effects being comparable or superior to ESC.
