[discussion] Discussion In this study, regardless of the degree of anisometropia, the CVV and CSV in more myopic eyes were lower than those in less myopic eyes; however, there was no significant difference in 3D-CVI. The degree of reduction in the HA group was higher than that in the LA and MA groups. Furthermore, for both △CVV and △CSV, most of the regions were closely correlated with interocular difference in △AL and △SE, and the nasal regions showed the strongest correlation. To the best of our knowledge, this study was the first to investigate the differences of three-dimension choroidal parameters in subjects with different degrees of myopic anisometropia. Besides, we also find higher retinal vessel density of more myopic eyes in HA group. These findings may offer a new perspective for further understanding the pathophysiology of myopia. The consistent reduction in CVV and CSV in more myopic eyes across all groups underscores the pivotal role of choroidal vascular and stromal atrophy in myopia progression. This observation aligns with prior studies reporting choroidal thinning in myopia but highlights the need for volumetric analyses to capture the full extent of structural changes [ 17 , 23 , 24 , 35 , 36 ]. The pronounced reduction in CVV and CSV in the HA group further supports the hypothesis that severe anisometropia exacerbates choroidal degeneration, potentially due to prolonged mechanical stretching and hypoxia-driven remodeling [ 10 , 15 ]. Notably, the absence of significant differences in 3D-CVI between fellow eyes suggests that while both vascular and stromal components diminish proportionally in myopic eyes, the volumetric density of choroidal vasculature remains relatively stable. The relationship between 3D-CVI and myopia severity remains controversial. Liu et al. [ 23 ]demonstrated that both CVV and CSV decrease with increasing myopia severity, but the reduction in CSV appears more pronounced than that in CVV, leading to a progressive elevation of 3D-CVI with myopia progression. However, contradictory findings were documented in a study by Liu and his colleagues, which revealed lower 3D-CVI values in high myopia compared to low-to-moderate myopia [ 24 ]. We hypothesize that these conflicting results may be attributed to population heterogeneity across studies, given that demographic variables including age, height, and weight were known confounders of 3D-CVI measurements [ 37 – 39 ]. Notably, this study employed a paired-eye comparative design within the same subjects, effectively controlling for most confounding factors through intra-individual comparisons. This methodological approach significantly enhances the reliability of our findings. The strong correlations between △CVV/△CSV and interocular differences in △SE/△AL, particularly in nasal regions, suggest localized susceptibility of the choroid to axial elongation. The nasal quadrant, being anatomically adjacent to the optic nerve and biomechanically distinct, may experience greater tensile stress during eye growth, leading to accelerated vascular and stromal loss [ 24 ]. This regional vulnerability aligns with earlier reports of nasal choroidal thinning in high myopia [ 18 , 23 ]. Although 3D-CVI remained unchanged between fellow eyes and did not correlate with ΔAL or ΔSE (Table 4 ), CVV and CSV were consistently reduced in the more myopic eye and showed strong correlations with both ΔAL and ΔSE. This suggests that absolute choroidal volume loss, rather than volumetric vascular density, better reflects myopic severity in anisometropia. Instead, absolute reductions in choroidal volume may better correlate with disease severity, emphasizing the importance of three-dimensional metrics in clinical assessments. The elevated deep capillary plexus density observed in more myopic eyes of the HA group introduces an intriguing paradox. While choroidal perfusion declines, retinal microvascular compensation might transiently counteract hypoxia in myopia. Similar compensatory mechanisms have been proposed in our previous research, which found that the deep capillary plexus density in individuals with high myopia and super-high myopia was significantly higher than in the control group and low-to-moderate myopia groups [ 40 ]. The inner segments of photoreceptors, the outer plexiform layer, and the deep portion of the inner plexiform layer constitute the primary oxygen-consuming regions of the retina [ 41 ]. A reduction in overall ocular blood flow may lead to a prioritized blood supply to these regions covered by the deep retinal vascular network. On the other hand, this phenomenon might also explain why myopia, particularly high myopia, serves as a protective factor against diabetic retinopathy [ 42 , 43 ]. This study has limitations. Its cross-sectional design precludes causal inferences between choroidal changes and myopia progression. Further longitudinal studies, particularly cohort studies, are required to establish temporal relationships. Additionally, all participants were aged 18 years or older, having passed the peak period of myopia progression (typically 6–12 years). Whether these findings generalize to pediatric populations undergoing active axial elongation should be validated in future studies. Furthermore, most participants were ICL surgery candidates with high myopia, which may limit the generalizability of our findings to populations with moderate/low myopia or hyperopic anisometropia. Future studies incorporating broader refractive error spectra are warranted to validate our observations. Moreover, given the large number of statistical comparisons performed, some of the observed ‘significant’ findings may simply represent false-positive results arising by chance. Consequently, these particular analyses should be regarded as exploratory rather than conclusive. Besides, although this study included a relatively large sample compared to similar investigations, a formal power analysis was not conducted prior to enrollment. This may limit the generalizability of the findings and should be addressed in future prospective studies. Finally, although our sample size was relatively large compared to similar studies, it remains limited for detecting nuanced associations and further studies should enroll more subjects.

[conclusion] Conclusion This study shows that 3D choroidal volumetric parameters—CVV and CSV—were significantly reduced in more myopic eyes across all degrees of anisometropia. These reductions correlated strongly with interocular differences in spherical equivalent and axial length, especially in nasal regions. The findings suggest that CVV and CSV may serve as sensitive biomarkers for myopia severity. Longitudinal studies are needed to confirm their role in tracking progression.

retrieved_papers.title: Evaluation of Macular Neurovasculature and Choroidal Blood Flow Following Inferior Oblique Myectomy Using Optical Coherence Tomography Angiography.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2024]
[Abstract] To evaluate the short-term effects of inferior oblique myectomy on the retinal neurovasculature, choroidal thickness, and choroidal vascularity index at the macula.
Patients older than 5 years who were candidates for inferior oblique muscle myectomy surgery participated in the study. Patients with any systemic or ocular disease that could affect the macular neurovasculature were not included in the study. After recording demographic data, including age and gender, and conducting a complete ophthalmic examination, macular optical coherence tomography (OCT), enhanced depth imaging OCT, and OCT angiography imaging (AngioVue software (V.2017.1.0.151; Optovue, Inc) were performed before (1 day to 1 week) and in the specific time intervals (1 week, 1 month, and 3 months) after the surgery for all participants.
Eighteen patients (13 male and 5 female) who underwent inferior oblique muscle myectomy, with a mean ± standard deviation age of 24.22 ± 18.14 years, were included in this study. The baseline mean ± standard deviation of subfoveal choroidal luminal area and subfoveal total choroidal area were 0.390 ± 0.03 and 0.539 ± 0.04 mm2, respectively. The changing pattern of the subfoveal choroidal vascularity index and subfoveal choroidal luminal area was statistically significant (P = .013 and .035, respectively).
Inferior oblique myectomy can lead to changes in choroid hemodynamics in the short term. However, these changes seem to be temporary. [J Pediatr Ophthalmol Strabismus. 2024;61(4):235-244.].

retrieved_papers.title: Age and gender-related changes in choroidal thickness: Insights from deep learning analysis of swept-source OCT images.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] The choroid is a vital vascular layer of the eye, essential for maintaining ocular health. Understanding its structural variations, particularly choroidal thickness (CT), is crucial for the early detection of diseases, such as age-related macular degeneration (AMD), high myopia (HM), and diabetes mellitus (DM). Recent advancements in deep learning have significantly improved the segmentation and measurement of choroidal layers.
This study aims to investigate age- and gender-related changes in CT and its components through deep learning analysis of swept-source optical coherence tomography (SS-OCT) images.
A total of 262 participants (136 females and 126 males) were recruited from Peking University International Hospital. Exclusion criteria included ocular pathologies and systemic conditions. SS-OCT was utilized for CT, Sattler layer-choriocapillaris complex thickness (SLCCT), and Haller layer thickness (HLT) measurements. auto-measurement method, based on deep learning algorithms, ensured accuracy. Ethics approval and informed consent were obtained from all participants.
Significant thinning of CT and SLCCT was observed after the age of 60, with HLT declining after the age of 30. Females exhibited marked thinning between the ages of 40 and 50, while males began to show thinning at age 60.
This research highlights age-related changes in choroidal thickness, with a particular emphasis on gender differences. The findings suggest that females experience earlier thinning, potentially attributable to hormonal changes. Additionally, the study validates the efficiency of deep learning algorithms in measuring choroidal thickness, thereby enhancing the reliability of clinical practice.

retrieved_papers.title: Bruch's Membrane Opening Changes in Eyes With Myopic Macular Degeneration: AIER-SERI Adult High Myopia Study.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2024]
[Abstract] The purpose of this study was to assess the choroidal thickness and the Bruch's membrane opening size and their relationship to visual acuity in eyes with myopic macular degeneration (MMD).
This was a population-based, cross-sectional study. Patients over the age of 30 years with high myopia (spherical equivalent ≤-5 diopters [D]) were recruited. The eyes were grouped according to the International Meta-Analysis for Pathologic Myopia (META-PM) classification based on fundus photographs and diffuse atrophy was subdivided into peripapillary diffuse choroidal atrophy (PDCA) or macular diffuse choroidal atrophy (MDCA). Swept-source optical coherence tomography imaging was performed and then the subfoveal choroidal thickness (SFCT) and Bruch's membrane opening diameter (BMOD) were measured.
Of the 470 study participants recruited, 373 patients (691 eyes), with a mean age of 42.8 ± 7.2 years, were eligible for the study and included in the analysis. There was no significant difference in SFCT between MDCA and patchy atrophy (M3) groups (P = 1.000), and the BMOD enlarged significantly from no myopic macular lesions to M3 (the P values of multiple comparison tests were all <0.005). Simple linear regression analysis showed that BMOD correlated positively with age (P < 0.001) and axial length (P < 0.001). Multiple linear regression analysis showed that best corrected visual acuity (BCVA) was significantly correlated with age (P = 0.041), axial length (P = 0.001), and BMOD (P = 0.017), but not with SFCT (P = 0.231).
The significant variation of BMOD among MMD groups and the correlation between BMOD and BCVA in MMD eyes suggest that BMOD may be an imaging biomarker for monitoring MMD.

[methods] Methods Study Population The AIER-SERI adult high myopia study is a 10-year, population-based, prospective cohort study conducted at Changsha Aier Eye Hospital in Hunan Province, China, since August 2021. The aim of the cohort study was to investigate the natural history of high myopia, and its risk factors for developing pathological complications. For these purposes, patients over 30 years of age with high myopia were recruited. High myopia was defined as SER ≤−5 D. This study follows the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki and received ethical approval from the Ethics Committee of Aier Eye Hospital, Changsha (approval number: AIER2020IRB05). All subjects signed informed consent forms regarding the nature of the study and the possible consequences of the measurements involved. The data analyzed in this report originated from a dataset of patients enrolled between August 2021 and October 2023. The exclusion criteria from the dataset included diabetic retinopathy, age-related macular degeneration, central serous chorioretinopathy, retinal vascular abnormalities, uveitis, retinitis pigmentosa, amblyopia, history of refractive and intraocular surgery, severe cataract, intraocular pressure (IOP) > 21 mm Hg, and glaucoma. Eyes that did not complete optical coherence tomography (OCT) examination or that had poor image quality were also excluded. Both eyes from the same patient could be included in the study. Ophthalmic Examinations All subjects underwent comprehensive ophthalmic examinations, including refractive error assessment with an autorefractor instrument (Nidek ARK-1, Nidek Technologies, Gamagori, Japan) after cycloplegia, measurement of BCVA and IOP (NCT CT-80A; Topcon), and slit-lamp examinations. The SER is equal to the spherical dioptric power plus half of the cylindrical dioptric power. BCVA was described as the logarithm of the minimum angle of resolution (logMAR). The axial length was measured using a Lenstar 900 (Haag-Streit USA, Mason, OH, USA). Measurement of Subfoveal Choroidal Thickness and Bruch's Membrane Opening Diameter The Topcon DRI OCT-1 Triton (DRI OCT-1 Triton; Topcon Corp., Tokyo, Japan) instrument with a light source of 1050 µm and a scanning speed of 100,000 A-scans per second was used for swept-source OCT. The scanning protocols included a standard optic disc scan covering an area of 6.0 mm × 6.0 mm centered on the optic disc (3-dimensional disc protocol) and a standard macular scan covering an area of 7.0 mm × 7.0 mm focused on the fovea (3-dimensional macula protocol). The resolution of both scanning protocols was 512 × 256. Due to the limited axial length compensation range (≤26.5 mm) of the DRI OCT-1 Triton, all OCT examinations for patients with high myopia were first performed under the default setting of the instrument (axial length = 24.39 mm), and then the Bruch's membrane opening diameter (BMOD) was measured with manual magnification correction. 22 OCT images centered on the fovea were viewed using the IMAGEnet software, a built-in ophthalmic data system. This software provides the function of automatic segmentation of the choroidal layer. The outcome would be reviewed by an OCT expert reader (author L.L.W.) and corrected manually if inaccurate segmentation was observed. 23 Then, the subfoveal choroidal thickness (SFCT), defined as the vertical distance between BM and the inner border of the sclera at the fovea ( Fig. 1 ), was manually measured by the same investigator consistently throughout the study. BMOD was defined as the length of the line between the two innermost BM terminals in the horizontal section ( Fig. 2 ). Figure 1. Example OCT image and measurement of subfoveal choroidal thickness in a highly myopic eye (axial length = 26.5 mm, and refractive error is −6.375 diopters). The IMAGEnet software automatically segmented the choroidal layer in the OCT B-scan passing through the center of fovea and the subfoveal choroidal thickness was measured as the vertical distance between Bruch's membrane and the inner border of the sclera at the fovea using the ruler within the IMAGEnet software manually. Figure 2. A horizontal cross-sectional OCT images passing through the center of the optic disc. The y ellow dots represent the innermost terminals of Bruch's membrane; and the red line represents the Bruch's membrane opening diameter. We used the open-source software ImageJ (version 1.53t; https://imagej.nih.gov/ij/ ) for magnification correction of the horizontal cross-sectional OCT images passing through the center of the optic disc according to Littmann's formula ( t = p × q × s , where t is the actual scan size, p is the magnification factor determined by the OCT imaging system, q is the magnification factor related to axial length, and s is the default swept source-OCT [SS-OCT] measurement). 22 The length of the BMOD was determined using the ruler within ImageJ. Both SFCT and BMOD measurements were performed by the same well-trained investigator (author L.L.W.), who did not know the MMD grading of the eyes at the time of the measurements. Intra-observer reliability was measured as the intraclass correlation coefficient (ICC) on 40 randomly selected eyes. Based on OCT images, we also documented myopic traction maculopathy (MTM), a high myopia-associated fundus lesion that included the presence of inner foveoschisis, outer foveoschisis, foveal detachment, full-thickness macular hole, and/or retinal detachment. 24 Classification of Myopic Macular Degeneration Color fundus photographs centered on the macula were acquired using a digital retinal camera within the same SS-OCT system. According to the META-PM Study Group, 25 MMD was classified into five different categories based on fundus photographs as follows: no myopic macular lesions (M0), tessellated fundus (M1), diffuse chorioretinal atrophy (M2), patchy chorioretinal atrophy (M3), and macular atrophy (M4). Lacquer cracks (LCs) and myopic choroidal neovascularization (CNV) were classified as “plus lesions.” As shown in Figure 3 , based on the extent and degree of the lesion, diffuse chorioretinal atrophy was divided into peripapillary diffuse chorioretinal atrophy (PDCA; defined as a diffuse atrophy limited to the region temporal to the optic disc) and macular diffuse chorioretinal atrophy (MDCA; defined as a diffuse atrophy observed in the entire posterior fundus beyond the macular area). 26 The classification and grading of MMD were performed by two independent, well-trained graders (authors L.L.W. and Z.Q.H.). If there was any disagreement between the two graders, arbitration was performed by a third senior ophthalmologist (author Q.V.H.). Figure 3. Fundus photographs of two patients with diffuse chorioretinal atrophy. ( A ) Diffuse chorioretinal atrophy ( arrows ) is observed temporal to the optic disc in a 38-year-old patient. Refractive error is 7.0 diopters and axial length is 27.0 mm. ( B ) In the right eye of a 51-year-old patient, diffuse atrophy ( arrows ) covered the macular area. Refractive error is 16.0 diopters and axial length is 29.3 mm. Statistical Analyses The statistical analysis was conducted using SPSS 26.0 (IBM Corporation, Chicago, IL, USA). Categorical variables were expressed as counts or proportions, and continuous variables were expressed as the mean and standard deviation. Chi-square tests were used to analyze differences in gender distribution between the different MMD groups. The differences in other parameters among the different categories of MMD were analyzed using a 1-way analysis of variance with Bonferroni multiple comparison tests. Based on the generalized estimating equations (GEEs), simple linear regression models were used to analyze the associations of age, axial length, and spherical equivalent refraction with BMOD and SFCT. The results were then used to create multivariable models with BMOD and SFCT as the outcomes. Simple regression models (based on the GEEs) were also used to investigate the relationship between BCVA and ocular parameters, and the results from the simple regression models were then used to create a final multivariable model with BCVA as the outcome. Two-sided P <0.05 was considered statistically significant.

[results] Results Demographics and Characteristics We screened 940 eyes of 470 patients with high myopia who were enrolled at the Clinical Research Center of Aier Eye Hospital, Changsha, between August 2021 and October 2023. A total of 249 eyes were excluded. Of these, 32 eyes did not meet the criteria for high myopia, 56 eyes did not have documented BCVA, 39 eyes had poor OCT-image qualities or did not have OCT examinations, 27 eyes had a history of retinal surgery, 39 eyes had a history of refractive surgery, 34 eyes had either glaucoma, Posner-Schlossman syndrome, or abnormal intraocular pressure, and 22 others. Ultimately, 373 patients (691 eyes) with a mean age of 42.8 ± 7.2 years were included in the analysis. Among them, 249 (66.76%) were female patients and the mean axial length was 27.08 ± 1.75 mm. The baseline characteristics of the 691 eyes are shown in Table 1 . Significant differences were found in age, axial length, SER, and BCVA in different MMD groups (all P < 0.001). From M0 to MDCA, the axial length gradually lengthened and the degree of myopia gradually increased (all P < 0.001). The mean age was 44.3 ± 7.6 years in the PDCA group, 48.9 ± 10.7 years in the MDCA group, and 47.6 ± 8.9 years in the M3 group, which is older than 41.8 ± 5.4 years in the M0 group and 41.3 ± 7.0 years in the M1 group (all P < 0.02). There were no significant differences in age (48.9 ± 10.7 and 47.6 ± 8.9 years, respectively), axial length (30.43 ± 1.60 and 30.77 ± 1.81 mm, respectively), and SER (−16.8 ± 4.8 and −17.7 ± 5.2 D, respectively) between the MDCA and M3 groups (all P = 1.000). The BCVA of the M0 (0.010 ± 0.039 logMAR), M1 (0.020 ± 0.061 logMAR), and PDCA (0.034 ± 0.083 logMAR) groups was better than that of the MDCA (0.224 ± 0.247 logMAR) and M3 (0.253 ± 0.394 logMAR) groups (all P < 0.001), but there was no significant difference in BCVA between the MDCA and M3 groups ( P = 1.000), nor did the M0, M1, and PDCA groups ( P > 0.50). There were no significant differences in IOP ( P = 0.075) and gender composition ( P = 0.150) among the MMD groups. CNV was found in 2 of 691 eyes. The detection rates of LC and MTM were 16.5% (114/691) and 10.0% (69/691), respectively. Table 1. Characteristics of Highly Myopic Eyes with Different Types of Myopic Macular Degeneration Characteristic Total ( n = 691) M0 ( n = 288) M1 ( n = 245) PDCA ( n = 93) MDCA ( n = 41) M3 ( n = 24) M4 ( n = 0) Age, y 42.8 ± 7.2 41.8 ± 5.4 41.3 ± 7.0 44.3 ± 7.6 48.9 ± 10.7 47.6 ± 8.9 NA Male, N (%) 229 (33.1) 94 (32.6) 76 (31.0) 31 (33.3) 21 (51.2) 7 (29.2) NA IOP, mm Hg 14.13 ± 2.44 14.39 ± 2.21 13.86 ± 2.60 13.83 ± 2.40 14.39 ± 2.83 14.42 ± 2.50 NA Axial length, mm 27.08 ± 1.75 26.10 ± 0.97 27.02 ± 1.16 27.89 ± 1.49 30.43 ± 1.60 30.77 ± 1.81 NA Spherical equivalent, D −9.0 ± 3.9 −7.1 ± 1.7 −8.4 ± 2.6 −10.5 ± 3.3 −16.8 ± 4.8 −17.7 ± 5.2 NA BCVA, logMAR units 0.038 ± 0.125 0.010 ± 0.039 0.020 ± 0.061 0.034 ± 0.083 0.224 ± 0.247 0.253 ± 0.394 NA BMOD, µm 2340.7 ± 937.5 1892.1 ± 310.4 2184.3 ± 531.7 2757.7 ± 844.0 4106.6 ± 1439.7 4687.4 ± 960.2 NA SFCT, µm 152.2 ± 74.9 208.9 ± 58.4 139.1 ± 47.7 90.3 ± 41.9 39.6 ± 12.0 37.4 ± 20.4 NA Plus lesions, N (%) CNV 2 (0.3) 0 0 0 1 (2.4) 1 (4.2) NA LC 114 (16.5) 4 (1.4) 21 (8.6) 33 (35.5) 37 (90.2) 19 (79.2) NA Other fundus lesions, N (%) MTM 69 (10.0) 8 (2.8) 12 (4.9) 18 (19.4) 20 (48.8) 11 (45.8) NA Data are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation or percentage. BCVA, best-corrected visual acuity; BMOD, Bruch's membrane opening diameter; CNV, choroidal neovascularization; D, diopters; IOP, intraocular pressure; LC, lacquer cracks; logMAR, logarithm of minimal angle of resolution; M0, no myopic maculopathy; M1, tessellated fundus; M3, patchy chorioretinal atrophy; M4, macular atrophy; MDCA, macular diffuse chorioretinal atrophy; MTM, myopic traction maculopathy; NA, not available; PDCA, peripapillary diffuse chorioretinal atrophy; SFCT, subfoveal choroidal thickness. SFCT and BMOD for Each Type of MMD The SFCT and BMOD were measured in all 691 eyes. The ICCs for the BMOD and SFCT were 0.957 (95% confidence interval [CI] = 0.921–0.977) and 0.988 (95% CI = 0.976–0.994), respectively, indicating very good intra-observer reliability. The SFCT and BMOD measurements of each MMD group are shown in Table 1 . To show the variation trend of BMOD and SFCT more intuitively, the results of the post hoc comparison between the two adjacent groups are presented in Figure 4 . It has been shown that BMOD gradually increased from M0 to M3, with statistical differences between each adjacent group (all P < 0.004). In contrast, the SFCT showed a tendency to become thinner with increasing severity of MMD. However, there was no significant difference in SFCT between the MDCA and M3 groups ( P = 1.000). Figure 4. Box-and-whisker plots showing the BMOD and SFCT among different MMD groups. ( A ) BMOD and ( B ) SFCT. The median is represented by the middle line within each box, and the second and third quartiles are represented by the lower and upper segments of the box, respectively. The whiskers in the plot represent the minimum ( bottom whisker ) and the maximum ( top whisker ). BMOD, Bruch's membrane opening diameter; SFCT, subfoveal choroidal thickness; * P < 0.05; NS, nonsignificant. Simple regression models (based on the GEEs) showed that BMOD was positively correlated with age ( P < 0.001) and axial length ( P < 0.001) but negatively correlated with SER ( P < 0.001). In contrast, SFCT was negatively correlated with age ( P < 0.001) and axial length ( P < 0.001) but positively correlated with SER ( P < 0.001; Table 2 ). Table 2. Simple Linear Regression Models Based on Bruch's Membrane Opening Diameter and Subfoveal Choroidal Thickness BMOD SFCT Parameters β 95% CI P Value β 95% CI P Value Age, y 0.261 0.142 to 0.380 <0.001 −0.259 −0.341 to −0.178 <0.001 AL, mm 0.788 0.700 to 0.875 <0.001 −0.585 −0.650 to −0.520 <0.001 SER, D −0.749 −0.848 to −0.651 <0.001 0.566 0.501 to 0.632 <0.001 β, standardized coefficients; AL, axial length; BMOD, Bruch's membrane opening diameter; D, diopters; SER, spherical equivalent refraction; SFCT, subfoveal choroidal thickness. Because there was a significant negative correlation between SER and axial length ( r = −0.835, P < 0.001), we included only axial length but not SER in the multiple linear regression analysis. With age and axial length as independent variables, we conducted multiple linear regression analyses (based on GEEs) on SFCT and BMOD for different MMD groups. The results are shown in Table 3 . It seems that the thinning rate of SFCT with axial elongation from M1 to MDCA gradually reduced but accelerated in the M3 group compared with the MDCA group (M1 = −13.229 µm/mm; PDCA = −11.323 µm/mm; MDCA = −2.932 µm/mm; and M3 = −7.917 µm/mm). The axial elongation-related expansion rate of BMOD gradually increased from M1 to MDCA; however, the rate decreased in the M3 group compared with the MDCA group (M1 = 283.175 µm/mm; PDCA = 406.481 µm/mm; MDCA = 636.902 µm/mm; and M3 = 531.689 µm/mm). Table 3. The Results of Linear Regression Analysis Using Age and Axial Length as Independent Variables to Predict Bruch's Membrane Opening Diameter and Subfoveal Choroidal Thickness for Each Type of Myopic Macular Degeneration BMOD SFCT B 95% CI P Value B 95% CI P Value M0 Age, y −1.589 −8.335 to 5.157 0.644 −0.738 −2.446 to 0.969 0.397 AL 134.481 91.779 to 177.184 <0.001 −9.697 −18.328 to −1.065 0.028 M1 Age, y 4.857 −6.059 to 15.773 0.383 −1.854 −2.764 to −0.944 <0.001 AL 283.175 208.321 to 358.028 <0.001 −13.229 −18.814 to −7.644 <0.001 PDCA Age, y 4.448 −18.092 to 26.989 0.699 −0.496 −1.739 to 0.747 0.434 AL 406.481 283.523 to 529.439 <0.001 −11.323 −16.237 to −6.409 <0.001 MDCA Age, y 48.494 20.140 to 76.849 0.001 0.070 −0.237 to 0.378 0.653 AL 636.902 348.963 to 924.841 <0.001 −2.932 −5.509 to −0.355 0.026 M3 Age, y 27.081 −33.989 to 88.151 0.385 0.057 −0.733 to 0.847 0.888 AL 531.689 52.248 to 1011.130 0.030 −7.917 −10.431 to −5.404 <0.001 AL, axial length; B, unstandardized coefficients; BMOD, Bruch's membrane opening diameter; M0, no myopic maculopathy; M1, tessellated fundus; M3, patchy chorioretinal atrophy; MDCA, macular diffuse chorioretinal atrophy; PDCA, peripapillary diffuse chorioretinal atrophy; SFCT, subfoveal choroidal thickness. Correlations Among BCVA and SFCT, BMOD, and Other Parameters Simple regression models (based on the GEEs) showed that a reduced BCVA was associated with older age ( P = 0.002), longer axial length ( P < 0.001), larger BMOD ( P < 0.001), and thinner SFCT ( P < 0.001), but not with sex ( P = 0.498; Table 4 ). Parameters that were significantly correlated with BCVA were included in the multiple regression models. In the final multiple regression models with BCVA as the outcome, only BMOD ( P = 0.017), age ( P = 0.041), and axial length ( P = 0.001) were significant factors for BCVA. Table 4. Linear Regression Models Based on Best-Corrected Visual Acuity Simple Regression Analysis Multiple Regression Analysis Parameters β 95% CI P Value β 95% CI P Value Sex 0.074 −0.140 to 0.289 0.498 — — — Age, y 0.254 0.092 to 0.415 0.002 0.148 0.006 to 0.291 0.041 AL, mm 0.496 0.311 to 0.682 <0.001 0.293 0.120 to 0.467 0.001 BMOD, mm 0.485 0.289 to 0.681 <0.001 0.261 0.046 to 0.477 0.017 SFCT, mm −0.286 −0.402 to −0.170 <0.001 0.050 −0.032 to 0.131 0.231 β, standardized coefficients; AL, axial length; BMOD, Bruch's membrane opening diameter; SFCT, subfoveal choroidal thickness.

[discussion] Discussion In this cross-sectional study, SS-OCT was used to evaluate SFCT and BMOD in different MMD groups. The differences in SFCT among different MMD groups observed in this study were consistent with the results of Fang et al. 10 Furthermore, it was observed the significant variation in BMOD among different stages of MMD and its axial elongation-related change rate in different MMD groups. We also found that BCVA was significantly associated with BMOD but not with SFCT, suggesting that BMOD may be an important imaging biomarker for monitoring MMD. The finding that the severity of MMD is related to the size of BMOD is consistent with Zhang et al.’s report that BMO enlarges linearly with axial elongation after the axial length of the eye exceeds 26 mm. 19 However, contrary to their finding of no significant association between BMOD and age in people over 50 years, we found a significant positive correlation between BMOD and age in the MDCA group when the axial length was added to the multiple regression equation. We speculate that the decrease in elasticity of the BM with age due to increased collagen cross-linking, calcification of the elastic fibers, and advanced glycation end products (AGEs)-mediated oxidative stress damage may be responsible for this correlation. 27 Because both studies included a narrow age range of study subjects, future studies involving a larger age range may provide conclusive findings about the relationship between BMOD and age. From M1 to M3, SFCT showed a similar trend in the axial elongation-related change rate, as reported by Fang et al. 10 It was found that as the axial length increased, the ratio of the choriocapillaris thickness to total choroid thickness increased, whereas the ratio of Sattler's and Haller's layer (containing the middle and large-sized choroidal blood vessels) thicknesses to total CT decreased. 28 Therefore, it may be that the continuous thinning of the Haller and Sattler layers mainly reduces the SFTC to the extent that its capacity to thin further without effects on the choriocapillaris is reduced, leading to a gradual slowing down of the axial elongation-related thinning rate of SFCT from M1 to MDCA, whereas choriocapillaris is relatively spared from the axial extension-related thinning during this period. Meanwhile, as the globe expanded, the tension within the BM increased, possibly causing an acceleration in the expansion rate of the BMOD as the severity of MMD increased from M1 to MDCA. Interestingly, we found that the change rates of BMOD and SFCT both turned at the M3 stage, which may be related to the characteristics of patchy atrophy, including the presence of MBMDs and the complete loss of the choriocapillaris. 20 We speculated that the breakdown of the biomechanical support structure of BM in the MBMD region may result in the residual medium and large choroidal vessels being more directly affected by IOP during M3 than MDCA, which, coupled with the complete loss of the choriocapillaris in the MBMD region, leads to faster thinning of SFCT with axial elongation in the M3 stage than in the MDCA stage. Because MBMD occurs only in eyes with an axial length equal to or longer than 27 mm, 29 a value very close to the critical axial length flexion point (27.26 mm) for the nonlinear change of the choroidal vascularity index (CVI; the ratio of luminal area of choroid to the total choroidal area) with axial elongation, 30 one may wonder whether the nonlinear changes of CVI with axial elongation is also related to the different changes in the choriocapillaris in early and late MMD. In this study, it was observed that the expansion rate of BMOD reduced in the M3 stage. Jonas et al. 17 proposed a hypothesis with regard to the role of BM during myopia development. They speculated that the process of myopic axial elongation with an enlargement of the posterior half of the globe leads to an increased strain within BM. The BMO initially enlarges in attempt to mitigate the rising strain, but with the progressing enlargement and continuously increased strain, BMO would be eventually overcome and lead to MBMD, a hallmark of the M3 stage. 17 , 19 , 20 Thus, based on this hypothesis, we presume that the reduced expansion rate of BMOD in the M3 stage might be due to the relieved tension within the BM through the formation of MBMD. This is supported by the report that higher prevalence of MBMD was found to be associated with a smaller increase in horizontal BMOD in the highly myopic eyes within the same axial length subgroup. 19 However, it is still unclear whether the apparent thinning of the choroid in the area of diffuse atrophy may primarily lead to a decreased support to and the increased vulnerability of the BM, which may then rupture in the eyes with patchy atrophy, or the increased tension within the ocular wall may lead to the thinning of the choroid and the rupture of the BM simultaneously, or it may be that the formation of MBMD in M3 eyes leads to the tearing and complete loss of choriocapillaris, which is tightly attached to the BM. Future studies applying OCT angiography will not only provide us with answers to this question, but also reveal whether choroidal and BM changes differ in different types of M3, including (1) M3 that develops from lacquer cracks; (2) M3 that develops within the area of an advanced diffuse chorioretinal atrophy; and (3) M3 that can be seen along the border of the posterior staphyloma, according to a natural history study of the long-term progression pattern of MMD. 31 As the middle layer of the RPE-BM-choriocapillaris complex, the BM is closely related to the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE; the primary caretaker of photoreceptor health and function) and choriocapillaris (the innermost layer of the choroid that directly nourishes the RPE and photoreceptors and the only pathway for material exchange in the foveal avascular zone), which may be the reason why BMOD can predict BCVA. 32 – 34 Until now, the relationship between BCVA and choroid thickness in high myopia eyes is still inconclusive, 8 , 15 , 16 , 35 assumably because of the lack of sufficiently sensitive test of visual function. Thus, utilization of a more sensitive test or combined visual function tests to investigate the relationship with choroidal thickness, particularly that of the choriocapillaris layer, in different MMD groups may help draw more reliable conclusions. Limitations of this study should be mentioned. First, eyes with macular atrophy were not available in the current study, and only baseline data from patients with high myopia were analyzed. However, we believe that this did not significantly affect our results, given that the main goal of this study was to assess SFCT and BMOD in the MMD groups. In the future, results from an ongoing, longitudinal study involving a larger sample of eyes with macular atrophy may offer additional insights. Second, both SFCT and BMOD were measured manually, which might have induced measurement bias. However, consistently having the same well-trained investigator and a high ICC has assuredly reduced bias to the minimum level. Third, SFCT and BMOD are 2D measurements, and the development and application of noninvasive 3D imaging technology in the future will help reveal more details. In conclusion, BMOD may be an imaging biomarker for monitoring MMD that deserves closer attention in clinical practice because of its significant variation among different MMD groups and its correlation with BCVA in eyes with MMD. The axial elongation-related change rates of BMOD and SFCT both turned at the M3 stage, suggesting that the close relationship between BM and choriocapillaris may play an important role in the pathological mechanism of late MMD.

[conclusion] Conclusions The significant variation of BMOD among MMD groups and the correlation between BMOD and BCVA in MMD eyes suggest that BMOD may be an imaging biomarker for monitoring MMD.

retrieved_papers.title: Myopic maculopathy among Chinese children with high myopia and its association with choroidal and retinal changes: the SCALE-HM study.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2024]
[Abstract] To investigate myopic maculopathy in Chinese children with high myopia and its association with choroidal and retinal changes.
This cross-sectional study included Chinese children aged 4-18 years with high myopia. Myopic maculopathy was classified by fundus photography and retinal thickness (RT) and choroidal thickness (ChT) in the posterior pole were measured by swept-source optical coherence tomography. A receiver operation curve was used to determine the efficacy of fundus factors in classifying myopic maculopathy.
In total, 579 children aged 12.8±3.2 years with a mean spherical equivalent of -8.44±2.20 D were included. The proportions of tessellated fundus and diffuse chorioretinal atrophy were 43.52% (N=252) and 8.64% (N=50), respectively. Tessellated fundus was associated with a thinner macular ChT (OR=0.968, 95% CI: 0.961 to 0.975, p<0.001) and RT (OR=0.977, 95% CI: 0.959 to 0.996, p=0.016), longer axial length (OR=1.545, 95% CI: 1.198 to 1.991, p=0.001) and older age (OR=1.134, 95% CI: 1.047 to 1.228, p=0.002) and less associated with male children (OR=0.564, 95% CI: 0.348 to 0.914, p=0.020). Only a thinner macular ChT (OR=0.942, 95% CI: 0.926 to 0.959, p<0.001) was independently associated with diffuse chorioretinal atrophy. When using nasal macular ChT for classifying myopic maculopathy, the optimal cut-off value was 129.00 µm (area under the curve (AUC)=0.801) and 83.85 µm (AUC=0.910) for tessellated fundus and diffuse chorioretinal atrophy, respectively.
A large proportion of highly myopic Chinese children suffer from myopic maculopathy. Nasal macular ChT may serve as a useful index for classifying and assessing paediatric myopic maculopathy.
NCT03666052.

[Introduction] Introduction Myopia is a common eye disease worldwide. 1 The prevalence of myopia and high myopia is particularly high in countries and regions in East and Southeast Asia, such as China, Hong Kong, Korea, Japan and Singapore, with a strong and developed education system. 1–5 Current predictions indicate that nearly 5 billion people worldwide will have myopia by 2050 and that 1 billion will be highly myopic. 6 Myopia and high myopia are also the main causes of visual impairment and blindness in the world, 7–9 and impose an increasingly heavy economic burden. High myopia is often accompanied by diverse pathological changes, but these changes may vary between children and adults due to the varied course and severity of the condition. For example, the reported prevalence of posterior staphyloma and chorioretinal atrophy is lower in children than in adults. 10 11 In addition, the prevalence and severity of myopic maculopathy are much higher in adults than in children, 12 as its progression rate. 13 14 Nevertheless, a recent study also reported a high prevalence of myopic maculopathy, even in children with myopia and a progression rate of nearly 20%. 15 Therefore, recognition of the early signs of pathological changes in childhood may be critical for the early prediction, diagnosis and treatment of severe pathological changes later in life. Nevertheless, studies focused on the relationship between myopic maculopathy and chorioretinal changes in children are limited. According to previous studies, children with high myopia may present with thinning of the retina and choroid in the posterior pole. 16–18 However, the relationships with myopic maculopathy have not been further discussed. One study has suggested that peripapillary diffuse chorioretinal atrophy temporal to the peripapillary region in childhood could develop into advanced myopic chorioretinal atrophy in adulthood. 19 However, the children included in that study had attended a high myopia clinic very early in life and may not be representative of the general condition. Nevertheless, these discoveries have suggested that both the choroid and retina in the posterior pole play important roles in the development of high myopia and that significant changes in the choroid and retina may be early signs of myopic maculopathy in childhood, which deserves further exploration. Therefore, the aim of the present study was to explore paediatric myopic maculopathy and its relationship with choroidal and retinal changes in the posterior pole in a large population of highly myopic Chinese children.

[methods] Materials and methods High myopia registration study: the Shanghai Child and Adolescent Large-scale Eye Study (SCALE-HM) is a prospective, examiner-masked study. As we described in our previously published methodology paper, the database of the SCALE study (a city wide, school-based study covering over one million children in Shanghai and the methodology of which has been published elsewhere 20 ) was used to select participants of the current study. The detailed protocol has been described in our previous methodological article. 21 Prior to the commencement of the study, the study rationale was explained to all children and their parents/guardians. Written informed consent was obtained from all parents/guardians and from children aged 12 or older, while oral consent was obtained from children younger than 12. Children who met the diagnostic criteria of high myopia, had no organic eye diseases, were in good general condition and resided in Shanghai were included in the study. Children with organic eye diseases, including strabismus, moderate–severe ptosis, congenital cataract or glaucoma, fundus diseases other than myopia-related fundus lesions and other cases not suitable for the study were excluded. Those who had undergone intraocular or refractive surgeries were also excluded. In the current study, children and adolescents aged 4–18 years were included in the final analysis. Because myopia develops with age, children with differing myopic levels were enrolled based on their ages. Based on the progression of myopia in children, we set our definition of high myopia and the range of refractive error as follows: for children aged between 4 and 5 years old, at least one eye of spherical equivalent (SE, defined as spherical power+0.5×cylindrical power)≤−0.5 D was included; for those aged between 6 and 8 years old, at least one eye of SE≤−3.0 D was included; for those aged between 9 and 12 years old, at least one eye of SE≤−5.0 D was included; and for those aged≥13 years old, at least one eye of SE≤−6.0 D was included. 21 Before measuring refractive status, cycloplegia was performed according to the protocol in our methodology article. 21 Given that some family members of the highly myopic children were also suffered from myopia or high myopia, these family members were also allowed to have comprehensive ocular examinations together with these highly myopic children and the examinational data were recorded in the data system. During data analysis, data from these myopic family members were excluded from the output dataset due to their age. Finally, a total of 735 subjects applied to participate in the study from 2018 to 2019, and 126 subjects were excluded due to age and the mean age of these excluded participants were 49.0 years (ranging from 20 to 78 years old). Among the remaining participants with required age, 30 participants were further excluded due to their refractive status. Among these 30 participants, 2 participants were aged between 4 and 5 years old with SE of +0.38 D; 4 participants were aged between 6 and 8 years old with a mean SE of −0.81 D (ranging from −2.00 D to +0.50 D); 12 participants were aged between 9 and 12 years old with a mean SE of −2.32 D (ranging from −4.88 D to +0.63 D); and 12 participants were older than or equal to 13 years with a mean SE of −3.89 D (ranging from −5.88 D to +0.63 D). The image quality was strictly controlled by both the examiner and the inspector, and the examinations were repeated if the quality was below the standard. Therefore, no subject was further excluded due to poor image quality during data analysis. Totally, 579 subjects met the eligibility criteria and were enrolled in the final analysis. Fundus grading Fundus grading was performed independently by two experts (JD, TC) in the field based on fundus photographs and facilitated by optical coherence tomography (OCT) images. 22 23 The experts were masked to the personal information of the participants. Any discrepancy between the two experts was recorded and discussed later by an expert panel. The META-analysis for Pathologic Myopia (META-PM) classification system for myopic maculopathy was used in this study; therefore, myopic maculopathy was classified as ‘no myopic retinal degenerative lesion’ (category 0), ‘tessellated fundus’ (category 1, well-defined choroidal vessels that can be observed clearly around the fovea as well as around the arcade vessels), ‘diffuse chorioretinal atrophy’ (category 2, yellowish white atrophic lesions in the posterior pole), ‘patchy chorioretinal atrophy’ (category 3, well-defined, greyish white lesions) and ‘macular atrophy’ (category 4, a well-defined, round greyish white or whitish chorioretinal atrophic lesion that is centred on the central fovea). 24 Three ‘plus’ lesions, namely lacquer cracks, myopic choroidal neovascularisation and Fuchs spots, were also assessed. This definition has been adopted widely in studies of high myopia and was discussed in the recent International Myopia Institute (IMI) report. 25 A vision-threatening condition occurring in people with myopia, usually high myopia, that comprises diffuse or patchy macular atrophy with or without lacquer cracks, macular Bruch’s membrane defects, choroidal neovascularisation, and Fuchs spot was also called myopic macular degeneration according to the IMI report. 25 Measurements of choroidal thickness (ChT) and retinal thickness (RT) in the posterior pole Fundus structural changes were assessed using fundus photography and swept-source OCT (SS-OCT, DRI OCT Triton, Topcon, Tokyo, Japan) in both the macular and the peripapillary regions. The OCT examination was performed between 10:00 and 15:00 each day to minimise the influence of diurnal variation. Spherical power, cylindrical power, axial length (AL) and cornea curvature radius were input into the OCT system before image collection. Littmann’s method was used by the OCT system to compensate for magnification factors. A signal strength≥60 and image quality≥90 were required for each qualified OCT image. Each SS-OCT examination included 12 radial scan lines focused on the centre of the fovea or the optic disc. Each scan line was 9 mm long and was separated from the adjacent lines by 15°. Sixteen B-scan OCT images were obtained on each scan line and were averaged with built-in software to create an averaged image. The automatic segmentation was carefully inspected, and manual correction was performed when the software misjudged the borderlines. 16 The thickness of each fundus layer was measured using SS-OCT ( online supplemental figure 1 ). The Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) grid was overlaid to define the sectors in both the macular and peripapillary regions. The diameters for the inner, middle and outer circles of the ETDRS grid were 1, 3 and 6 mm, respectively, and the inner ring (also called the parafoveal region in the macula) and the outer ring (also called the perifoveal region in the macula) were further divided into four quadrants—the temporal, superior, nasal and inferior sectors. In the macular region, all nine sectors of the grid were applied; however, in the peripapillary region, only four sectors in the outer ring were used in the final analysis. 16 10.1136/bjo-2022-321839.supp1 Supplementary data Statistical analysis Statistical analysis was performed using SPSS (V.22.0) and R (R core team, 2020). Continuous variables were described as the mean±SD, while categorical variables were described as counts (proportions). Intergroup differences were assessed with t-tests or analysis of variance, and the Bonferroni method was used for post hoc tests. Multivariate regression analysis was performed to determine the independent factors of myopic maculopathy. Three main analyses were performed. First, the severity of myopic maculopathy was described based on the fundus photographs. Second, the SS-OCT measurements in each sector of the macular and peripapillary regions were compared among children with different levels of myopic maculopathy. Third, the distribution of ChT and RT was compared across different levels of myopic maculopathy, and multivariate regression analysis was performed to determine the independent factors for myopic maculopathy. A multicategory receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve was used to determine the cut-off values of ChT for different myopic maculopathy categories. 26 27 The mean nasal macular ChT was adopted, calculated as the weighted average of the parafoveal nasal ChT and perifoveal nasal ChT, with weights of 8 and 27 (based on their area), respectively. The hypervolume under the manifold (HUM) was calculated in multicategory ROC analysis to correspond to the accuracy of the whole classification. The area under the curve (AUC) was used to assess the accuracy of prediction in two-by-two classifications. Statistical significance was defined as p<0.05 (two tailed).

[results] Results General characteristics of the participants The study included 579 children and adolescents (271 boys and 308 girls) with a mean age of 12.8±3.2 years. Their general characteristics are shown in table 1 . The parameters of the bilateral eyes were moderately to highly correlated (R values ranging from 0.59 to 0.86) ( online supplemental figure 2 ). Therefore, only the right eyes were included for analysis. For the involved right eyes, the mean AL and SE were 26.65±1.16 mm and −8.44±2.20 D, respectively. Table 1 General information for the participants in the High myopia registration study: the Shanghai Child and Adolescent Large-scale Eye study N Mean±SD Percentiles 25 50 75 Age, year 579 12.8±3.2 11.0 13.0 15.0 Height, cm 579 157.8±16.8 151.0 162.0 169.0 Weight, kg 579 51.6±18.0 40.6 50.2 61.3 R-UCVA 578 0.11±0.08 0.05 0.10 0.12 L-UCVA 578 0.12±0.12 0.05 0.10 0.12 R-AL, mm 579 26.65±1.16 25.95 26.66 27.36 L-AL, mm 579 26.58±1.20 25.89 26.60 27.33 R-IOP, mm Hg 573 15.58±2.69 14.00 15.30 17.00 L-IOP, mm Hg 572 15.59±2.74 14.00 15.00 17.00 R-SE, Diopter 579 −8.44±2.20 −9.63 −8.38 −7.00 L-SE, Diopter 579 −8.27±2.25 −9.63 −8.25 −7.00 R-BCVA 577 0.93±0.18 1.00 1.00 1.00 L-BCVA 577 0.93±0.18 1.00 1.00 1.00 AL, axial length; BCVA, best-corrected visual acuity; IOP, intraocular pressure; L, left eye; R, right eye; SE, spherical equivalent; UCVA, uncorrected visual acuity. The proportions of tessellated fundus and diffuse chorioretinal atrophy were 43.52% (N=252) and 8.64% (N=50), respectively. No severe atrophic myopic maculopathy (ie, patchy chorioretinal atrophy and macular atrophy), myopic traction maculopathy or neovascular myopic maculopathy were present. The demographics and basic clinical characteristics of the three groups are shown in table 2 . The distribution of tessellated fundus and diffuse chorioretinal atrophy by age is depicted in figure 1 . In general, children with older age and longer AL tended to have higher rates of myopic maculopathy. The proportion of diffuse chorioretinal atrophy in children younger than 7 years old was extremely high, and this may be a unique clinical characteristic of early onset high myopia. Figure 1 Distribution of myopic maculopathy by age in children with high myopia. The distribution of myopic maculopathy was compared among five age groups (group 1: 4–7 years old, N=48; group 2: 8–10 years old, N=76; group 3: 11–13 years old, N=186; group 4: 14–16 years old, N=203; group 5: ≥17 years old, N=66), and each group was further divided into two subgroups according to the axial length (≤26.0 mm and >26.0 mm). Statistical significance was tested using analysis of variance, and the Bonferroni method was used for post hoc tests. Statistical significance was defined as p<0.05 (two tailed). AL, axial length; BCVA, best-corrected visual acuity; IOP, intraocular pressure; SE, spherical equivalent; UCVA, uncorrected visual acuity. Significant choroidal thinning with limited retinal changes in the peripapillary region Table 3 and figure 2A–2C show the thickness of each fundus layer in the peripapillary region. The ChT became significantly thinner in all four sectors of the peripapillary region in children with more severe myopic maculopathy (all p values<0.001). This tendency was most noticeable in the temporal sector (127 µm vs 59 µm, a 53.5% decrease in ChT) and least pronounced in the nasal sector (148 µm vs 90 µm, a 39.2% decrease in ChT) when comparing the groups without myopic maculopathy and those with diffuse chorioretinal atrophy ( figure 2F ). A similar thinning tendency was observed between the groups without myopic maculopathy and those with tessellated fundus ( figure 2D ) and between the groups with tessellated fundus and those with diffuse chorioretinal atrophy ( figure 2E ). Figure 2 Comparisons of choroidal thickness (ChT) in the posterior pole among different grades of myopic maculopathy. (A–C) ChT of children without myopic maculopathy, with tessellated fundus and with diffuse chorioretinal atrophy. (D–F) Difference in ChT between the groups without myopic maculopathy and with tessellated fundus, between the groups with tessellated fundus and diffuse chorioretinal atrophy and between the groups without myopic maculopathy and with diffuse chorioretinal atrophy. The left Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study (ETDRS) grid was located in the macular region, the right ETDRS grid was located in the optic disc region and the ellipse represented the entire posterior pole. The white circle inside the right ETDRS grid represented the optic disc, and the choroid was lacking. The thickness data of the entire ETDRS grid in the macula and the outer ring of the ETDRS grid in the optic disc was measured using swept-source optical coherence tomography and was used for quantitative analysis, while the thickness data of the remaining areas in the figures was fitted by the adjacent area and was used to display the variation tendency. Statistical significance was defined as p<0.05 (two tailed). Group 0—without myopic maculopathy; group 1—tessellated fundus; group 2—diffuse chorioretinal atrophy. For post hoc tests, superscript a refers to significant difference between group 0 and group 1 (P<0.05), superscript b refers to significant difference between group 0 and group 2 (P<0.05), and superscript c refers to significant difference between group 1 and group 2 (P<0.05) ChT, choroidal thickness; RT, retinal thickness; GCL+, combined thickness of the ganglion cell layer and the inner plexiform layer; RNFLT, retinal nerve fibre layer thickness; S, superior; I, inferior; N, nasal; T, temporal.; By contrast, the RT remained similar across different levels of myopic maculopathy. The RT became slightly thinner in the superior and nasal sectors (p=0.021 and 0.003, respectively) in children with more severe myopic maculopathy and remained similar in the inferior and temporal sectors (p=0.134 and 0.369, respectively) in children with different categories of myopic maculopathy. The retinal nerve fibre layer thickness (RNFLT) became thinner in the nasal sector (p=0.001) and thicker in the temporal sector (p<0.001) in the peripapillary region with more severe myopic maculopathy ( table 3 ). Significant thinning of the retina and choroid in the macular region The distribution of macular RT and ChT for different severities of myopic maculopathy is shown in table 4 . Similar to the peripapillary region, the choroid in the macular region also became significantly thinner in children as the severity of myopic maculopathy increased ( figure 2A–2C , all Ps<0.001), with a 40%–60% decrease in ChT observed when comparing the groups without myopic maculopathy with those with diffuse chorioretinal atrophy ( figure 2F ) and a 20%–30% decrease comparing the groups without myopic maculopathy with those with tessellated fundus ( figure 2D ). This tendency was most pronounced in the nasal sectors and least pronounced in the temporal sectors. Combining the discoveries in the peripapillary region, the ChT became thinner most significantly in the area between the fovea and the optic disc, and this thinning tendency then weakened towards the more peripheral area ( figure 2D–2F ). Statistical significance was defined as p<0.05 (two tailed). For post hoc tests, superscript a refers to significant difference between group 0 and group 1 (p<0.05), superscript b refers to significant difference between group 0 and group 2 (p<0.05), and superscript c refers to significant difference between group 1 and group 2 (p<0.05). Group 0—without myopic maculopathy; group 1—tessellated fundus; group 2—diffuse chorioretinal atrophy. Centre, central fovea; ChT, choroidal thickness; ETDRS, Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study; I, inferior; In, inner circle of the ETDRS grid; N, nasal; Out, outer circle of the ETDRS grid; RT, retinal thickness; S, superior; T, temporal. The macular RT decreased slightly in children with a greater degree of myopic maculopathy, with a 7 μm to 15 µm decrease in each sector when compared with the groups without myopic maculopathy with those with diffuse chorioretinal atrophy (all Ps<0.05, table 4 ). Additionally, for the layers inside the retina, such as the combined thickness of the ganglion cell layer and the inner plexiform layer (GCL+) and the RNFL, the distribution of each layer thickness was similar for the different degrees of myopic maculopathy. Associated factors of myopic maculopathy, ChT and RT Factors associated with the severity of myopic maculopathy are shown in table 5 . Deletion of variables with collinearity revealed that thinner macular ChT, thinner macular RT, longer AL, female gender and older age were independently associated with the presence of tessellated fundus when compared with children without myopic maculopathy, and only thinner macular ChT was independently associated with the presence of diffuse chorioretinal atrophy when compared with children with tessellated fundus. Given the close association of fundus changes in the nasal macular area and myopic maculopathy, the associations between nasal ChT, RT and myopia maculopathy categories were also analysed ( online supplemental table 1 ) and gave results similar to those observed in the whole macular area. Table 5 Logistic regression models to explore the factors associated with myopic maculopathy B SE Wald P OR 95% CI of OR lower limit upper limit Constant * −0.573 4.191 0.019 0.891 Age, year 0.126 0.041 9.534 0.002 1.134 1.047 1.228 IOP, mm Hg −0.012 0.043 0.080 0.778 0.988 0.909 1.074 Axial length, mm 0.435 0.130 11.242 0.001 1.545 1.198 1.991 Macular ChT, μm −0.033 0.004 84.743 <0.001 0.968 0.961 0.975 Macular RT, μm −0.023 0.010 5.789 0.016 0.977 0.959 0.996 Gender=male −0.573 0.246 5.417 0.020 0.564 0.348 0.914 Gender=female 0.000 . . . . . .
