[results] Results Steeper declines in phonemic fluency were observed in women who had been pregnant (adjusted mean difference: ‐0.05, 95% confidence interval (95% CI)‐0.05,‐0.04) (reference: had not been pregnant), had two (‐0.03, 95% CI ‐0.03,‐0.02) or more children (‐0.05, 95% CI ‐0.05,‐0.05) (reference: no children), had their first child at 30‐34 years (‐0.06, 95%CI ‐0.07, ‐0.04) (reference: aged 20‐29 years), experienced cycle lengths of ≤23 days (‐0.02, 95%CI ‐0.03,‐0.009) (reference: cycle length 23‐35 days), underwent menopause (‐0.1, 95%CI ‐0.1,‐0.1) (reference: still menstruating), or stopped menstruating at 40‐49 years (‐0.06, 95% CI ‐0.07, ‐0.06) (reference: stopped menstruating aged ≥50 years). In contrast, having children at age 35+ years (0.05, 95% CI 0.03,0.06), oral contraceptive use (0.07, 95% CI 0.06,0.08), and cycle lengths of 35+ days (0.1, 95% CI 0.1,0.1) were associated with less declines over 25 years. Similar steeper memory declines were observed among women who had been pregnant (‐0.06, 95% CI ‐0.06,‐0.05), had 3+ children (‐0.1, 95% CI ‐0.2,‐0.1), had a first pregnancy at 30‐34 years (‐0.05, 95% CI ‐0.06,‐0.04) and underwent menopause (‐0.07, 95% CI ‐0.07,‐0.06).

[conclusion] Conclusion These findings demonstrate potential long‐term associations between hormonal and reproductive events on cognitive aging. Further research is needed on the underlying mechanisms and the potential interactions with risk factors that exacerbate these associations.

retrieved_papers.title: The influence of sex hormones and the menstrual cycle on women's athletic performance: A literature review.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] The cyclical nature of female sex hormones - primarily estradiol and progesterone - exerts a multidimensional influence on physiological processes relevant to sports performance. This narrative review summarizes the current understanding of how different phases of the menstrual cycle affect energy metabolism, cardiovascular dynamics, neuromuscular function, thermoregulation, and psychological responses in physically active women. Estrogen-dominant phases, especially the late follicular stage, are generally associated with enhanced aerobic capacity, improved vasodilation, mood stabilization, and more efficient motor control. Conversely, progesterone-dominant phases - most notably the mid-to-late luteal stage - are characterized by increased core temperature, fluid retention, fatigue, and greater reliance on carbohydrate metabolism. The review also evaluates the effects of hormonal contraceptives, particularly oral contraceptives, on athletic performance and training adaptations. While individual responses vary, hormonal fluctuations create predictable patterns of performance variability that can be leveraged through phase-aware training. Based on the reviewed literature, the paper suggests practical training strategies, including phase-specific adaptations, nutritional support, and attention to menstrual symptoms. The review further highlights methodological limitations in the literature, including small sample sizes, inconsistent hormone validation, and underrepresentation of elite athletes. A personalized understanding of menstrual physiology enables more effective planning of training loads and recovery strategies, benefiting both professional and recreationally active women.

retrieved_papers.title: Dietary Intakes of Vitamin B-2 (Riboflavin), Vitamin B-6, and Vitamin B-12 and Ovarian Cycle Function among Premenopausal Women.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2020]
[Abstract] Riboflavin, vitamin B-6, and vitamin B-12 are key players in one-carbon metabolism as enzymatic cofactors, and deficiency of these nutrients may influence reproductive outcomes possibly through affecting reproductive hormones.
The goal was to investigate associations between dietary intakes of riboflavin, vitamin B-6, and vitamin B-12, and menstrual function among premenopausal women.
This was a secondary analysis of a prospective cohort study conducted at the University at Buffalo during 2005 to 2007.
Participants were 259 healthy, regularly menstruating women (aged 18 to 44 years) with self-reported menstrual cycles between 21 and 35 days, who were not trying to conceive, and who had not used hormonal contraception during the past 3 months.
Intakes of B vitamins were assessed via 24-hour dietary recalls four times per menstrual cycle for two cycles. Serum reproductive hormones and plasma homocysteine were measured eight and three times, respectively, per cycle for two cycles. Anovulatory cycles were determined by progesterone concentrations ≤5 ng/mL (15.9 nmol/L) and no observed serum luteinizing hormone peak during the mid or late luteal phase visit.
Weighted linear mixed regressions were used to evaluate associations between cycle-averaged B vitamin intakes and hormones and homocysteine, and generalized linear regressions for associations with anovulation. Models were adjusted for age, race, body mass index, physical activity, alternate Mediterranean diet score, intakes of total energy, protein, fiber, and folate, and percentage of energy intake from fat.
Higher intakes of riboflavin (per 0.1 mg increase in intake) were inversely correlated with estradiol (-0.87%, 95% CI -1.67 to -0.06) and homocysteine levels (-0.61%, 95% CI -1.10 to -0.12). Higher vitamin B-6 intakes were suggestive of higher follicle-stimulating hormone, although the results were not statistically significant (0.63% difference, 95% CI -0.03 to 1.29, per 0.1 mg increase in intake; P=0.06). Small increases in testosterone and decreases in homocysteine were found with vitamin B-12 intake. No associations were observed between intake of B vitamins and a risk of sporadic anovulation.
Higher intakes of riboflavin were associated with a small decrease in serum estradiol among healthy, regularly menstruating women. Higher intakes of riboflavin and vitamin B-12 were associated with lower plasma homocysteine concentrations. Overall, riboflavin, vitamin B-6, and vitamin B-12 that are one-carbon nutrients do not appear to influence the ovarian cycle among premenopausal women.

[Introduction] Background: Vitamins B2, B6, and B12 are key players in one-carbon metabolism as enzymatic cofactors, and deficiency of these nutrients may influence reproductive outcomes possibly through affecting reproductive hormones.

[introduction] Background: Vitamins B2, B6, and B12 are key players in one-carbon metabolism as enzymatic cofactors, and deficiency of these nutrients may influence reproductive outcomes possibly through affecting reproductive hormones.

[results] Results: Higher intakes of vitamin B2 (per 0.1 mg increase in intake) were inversely correlated with estradiol (−0.87%, 95% confidence interval [CI] −1.67, −0.06) and homocysteine levels (−0.61%, 95% CI −1.10, −0.12). Higher vitamin B6 intakes were suggestive of higher follicle-stimulating hormone though the results were not statistically significant (0.63% difference, 95% CI −0.03, 1.29, per 0.1 mg increase in intake; P=0.06). Small increases in testosterone and decreases in homocysteine were found with vitamin B12 intake. No associations were observed between intake of B-vitamins and a risk of sporadic anovulation.

[conclusion] Conclusion: Higher intakes of vitamin B2 were associated with a small decrease in serum estradiol among healthy, regularly-menstruating women. Higher intakes of B2 and B12 were associated with lower plasma homocysteine concentrations. Overall, vitamins B2, B6, and B12 that are one-carbon nutrients do not appear to influence the ovarian cycle among premenopausal women.

retrieved_papers.title: Understanding pharmacokinetic variability in women across different life stages.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] The historical exclusion of adult women, including pre- and post-menopausal women, pregnant and breastfeeding women, from clinical trials has contributed to gaps in pharmacological knowledge and suboptimal care in these populations. To address this, it is mandatory to address absorption, distribution, metabolism, and elimination in adult women. Understanding these factors is critical for achieving appropriate dosing and effective pharmacotherapy.
This review summarizes pharmacokinetic variability in adult women across differentlife stages, highlighting the effects of age, the menstrual cycle, pregnancy, lactation, oral contraceptives, life style and hormone replacement therapy, providing some specific examples. Moreover, examples of sex differences in pharmacokinetic of protein-based therapeutics and physiologically based pharmacokinetic modeling are provided.
Emphasizing the urgent need of new pharmacological trials which including adult women considering the phase of their life, and life styles with the aim that current and future research will reduce disparities in patient care related to age, life stage, and sex conducted by investigators expert in pharmacokinetic and sex and gender research.

retrieved_papers.title: Longitudinal changes in mammographic density and reproductive hormones during the menopausal transition in middle-aged Korean women: effect modification by obesity.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] Breast cancer, strongly associated with female hormones, peaks around perimenopause in Asia, versus post-menopause in Western countries. This study aims to investigate longitudinal changes in reproductive hormones and mammographic density (MD) through the menopausal transition (MT), as MD serves as both a strong breast cancer risk factor and a proxy marker of cumulative hormone exposure.
This longitudinal study included 4,737 Korean women who were in the premenopausal stage at baseline. MD was measured using digital mammography and analyzed with LIBRA software. Mean of craniocaudal views from both breasts were used for density assessments. A subset of 255 women with samples collected at four visits was assayed for hormone measurements across menopausal stages. Linear mixed-effects models were used to analyze longitudinal changes in reproductive hormones, including follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), estradiol (E2), and anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), and MD to evaluate potential interaction by obesity status.
For MD analyses, the mean age of participants was 41.0 (± 3.3) years, with a median follow-up of 7.0 years (IQR 4.1-9.1). For hormone analyses, the mean age was 45.9 (± 2.4) years, with a median follow-up of 6.1 years (IQR 5.8-6.9). As menopausal stage advanced, FSH increased, whereas AMH and E2 decreased. The association between E2 and menopausal stages differed by obesity (P for interaction = 0.016); in early transition, E2 increased in the non-obese group (14.53 [95% CI: -15.68, 44.75]) but decreased in the obese group (- 86.02 [95% CI: -151.05, - 21.00]). Similarly, MD changes across the MT varied by BMI category (P for interaction < 0.001); overall, mean MD decreased with advancing stages, but an increasing MD pattern was observed during early transition in the underweight group (1.69 [95% CI: -0.08, 3.46]).
In Korean women undergoing MT, transient increases in MD paralleled E2 elevations, particularly in non-obese women. These findings align with the peak breast cancer incidence in Korean women in their 40s, preceding menopause, and the importance of considering hormonal and MD dynamics, as well as BMI, in breast cancer risk assessment and screening strategies for Asian women.

[Introduction] Background Breast cancer is the most prevalent malignancy among women worldwide with increasing incidence, underscoring the need to identify modifiable risk factors and develop targeted prevention strategies across populations [ 1 , 2 ]. In Asian countries, including Korea, breast cancer incidence peaks earlier—typically during the perimenopausal period—compared to that in Western populations, which peaks predominantly after menopause [ 3 ]. Endogenous estrogen levels and mammographic density (MD) are well-established risk factors for breast cancer [ 4 , 5 ]. Although both decline significantly after menopause, the perimenopausal period (also known as the menopausal transition [MT]), is characterized by dynamic hormonal fluctuations [ 6 ]. and a non-linear decline in breast density [ 7 , 8 ]. Notably, breast cancer risk does not necessarily decline during the MT, particularly among Asian women, highlighting a potentially vulnerable period that warrants further investigation into the interplay between hormonal changes, breast tissue composition, and cancer risk. The MT begins with irregular menstruation due to declining ovarian follicle numbers [ 6 ]. The Study of Women’s Health Across the Nation (SWAN) challenged the traditional view of a uniform estrogen decline during the MT, showing that non-obese women may experience transient elevations in estradiol (E2) levels before their final menstrual period (FMP) [ 9 ]. Elevated E2 levels are associated with increased risk of breast cancer development, particularly estrogen receptor (ER)-positive subtypes [ 10 ]; this transient hormonal increase may influence breast cancer risk. Moreover, body mass index (BMI) shows a strong inverse association with hormone receptor-positive subtypes [ 11 ], and menopausal status modifies this relationship as follows: in post-menopausal women, obesity increases breast cancer risk, but may reduce the risk in premenopausal women [ 12 ]. A large multicenter study of premenopausal women reported a 12–23% reduction in breast cancer risk per 5 kg/m² increase in BMI among premenopausal women including Asians [ 11 ], a finding confirmed in Korean populations [ 13 ]. Accordingly, the interaction between hormonal fluctuations and BMI could potentially influence breast cancer risk in perimenopausal women. We hypothesized that transient E2 level elevations occurring during the MT may be accompanied by temporary increases in MD, a well-established risk factor for breast cancer, and that these patterns differ by BMI. To test this hypothesis, we conducted a longitudinal analysis of changes in E2, follicle-stimulating hormone [FSH], and anti-Müllerian hormone [AMH] levels, and MD, across four MT stages (pre-menopause, early transition, late transition, and post-menopause), while accounting for the interaction of this association with BMI.

[introduction] Background Breast cancer is the most prevalent malignancy among women worldwide with increasing incidence, underscoring the need to identify modifiable risk factors and develop targeted prevention strategies across populations [ 1 , 2 ]. In Asian countries, including Korea, breast cancer incidence peaks earlier—typically during the perimenopausal period—compared to that in Western populations, which peaks predominantly after menopause [ 3 ]. Endogenous estrogen levels and mammographic density (MD) are well-established risk factors for breast cancer [ 4 , 5 ]. Although both decline significantly after menopause, the perimenopausal period (also known as the menopausal transition [MT]), is characterized by dynamic hormonal fluctuations [ 6 ]. and a non-linear decline in breast density [ 7 , 8 ]. Notably, breast cancer risk does not necessarily decline during the MT, particularly among Asian women, highlighting a potentially vulnerable period that warrants further investigation into the interplay between hormonal changes, breast tissue composition, and cancer risk. The MT begins with irregular menstruation due to declining ovarian follicle numbers [ 6 ]. The Study of Women’s Health Across the Nation (SWAN) challenged the traditional view of a uniform estrogen decline during the MT, showing that non-obese women may experience transient elevations in estradiol (E2) levels before their final menstrual period (FMP) [ 9 ]. Elevated E2 levels are associated with increased risk of breast cancer development, particularly estrogen receptor (ER)-positive subtypes [ 10 ]; this transient hormonal increase may influence breast cancer risk. Moreover, body mass index (BMI) shows a strong inverse association with hormone receptor-positive subtypes [ 11 ], and menopausal status modifies this relationship as follows: in post-menopausal women, obesity increases breast cancer risk, but may reduce the risk in premenopausal women [ 12 ]. A large multicenter study of premenopausal women reported a 12–23% reduction in breast cancer risk per 5 kg/m² increase in BMI among premenopausal women including Asians [ 11 ], a finding confirmed in Korean populations [ 13 ]. Accordingly, the interaction between hormonal fluctuations and BMI could potentially influence breast cancer risk in perimenopausal women. We hypothesized that transient E2 level elevations occurring during the MT may be accompanied by temporary increases in MD, a well-established risk factor for breast cancer, and that these patterns differ by BMI. To test this hypothesis, we conducted a longitudinal analysis of changes in E2, follicle-stimulating hormone [FSH], and anti-Müllerian hormone [AMH] levels, and MD, across four MT stages (pre-menopause, early transition, late transition, and post-menopause), while accounting for the interaction of this association with BMI.

[methods] Methods Study population This longitudinal observational study included women aged 42 to 52 years, who were either premenopausal or in the early MT, between 2014 and 2018, with follow-up through 2023. Participants underwent annual or biennial health examinations at the Total Healthcare Center of Kangbuk Samsung Hospital in Seoul and Suwon, South Korea [ 14 , 15 ]. Women were eligible for the MD analysis if digital mammography images were available and suitable for density quantification. Although recruitment for the prospective cohort began in 2014, participants had undergone digital mammography at our center before enrollment; these pre-enrollment images were retrospectively retrieved and included in the analysis after obtaining explicit participant consent for research use. The MD dataset comprised quantitative measurements from digital mammograms obtained between 2009—when digital mammography was first implemented at our center—and 2020, when mammographic data became available for de-identification and analysis. Of the 5,016 initially enrolled participants, we excluded 230 individuals who withdrew from the study, along with those with missing breast density data or a history of hormone therapy at baseline. This resulted in 4,737 participants included in the final mammographic density analysis (Fig. 1 ). For the hormonal analysis, we included women from the same prospective cohort who met the following criteria: (a) experienced natural menopause during follow-up (2014–2023), (b) attended at least one follow-up visit after menopause, and (c) had stored serum samples from at least four time points, including one premenopausal and one postmenopausal sample obtained within 1 year of the final menstrual period (FMP). From all eligible women meeting these criteria, we selected 255 participants using systematic sampling prioritized by proximity of sample collection timing to the FMP, ensuring optimal capture of hormonal changes in E2, FSH, and AMH levels across the MT. This approach yielded approximately 1,020 serum samples for hormonal analysis (4 samples per participant). This selection process yielded 255 women, of whom 238 (93.3%) also had mammography data and were included in analyses examining both hormone levels and MD. The remaining 17 women (6.7%) did not have suitable mammographic data. For participants who initiated hormone therapy during follow-up, only data collected prior to therapy initiation were included, and these individuals were censored at the start of hormone therapy owing to the small number of cases (Fig. 1 ). This study was approved by the Institutional Review Board of Kangbuk Samsung Hospital (IRB No. KBSMC 2025-03-008), and the need for informed consent was waived owing to the use of de-identified retrospective data collected during the health screening process. Data collection Demographic, behavioral, reproductive, and medical history data were collected through standardized, self-administered questionnaires. Participants were classified as ever (≥ 100 lifetime cigarettes) or never smokers [ 16 ]. Alcohol consumption was categorized as ≥ 10 or < 10 g/day [ 17 ]. Physical activity was evaluated using the validated Korean version of the short-form International Physical Activity Questionnaire [ 18 ]. Trained nurses measured body height and weight with participants barefoot and in a hospital gown. Body mass index (BMI) was then classified according to Asian-specific criteria [ 19 ]: underweight (< 18.5 kg/m 2 ), normal weight (18.5–23 kg/m 2 ), overweight (23–25 kg/m 2 ), and obese (≥ 25 kg/m 2 ). Marital status was classified as single, married/cohabiting, or divorced/separated/widowed. Education level was classified as college or higher and high school or lower. Parity was defined as having one or more births (live or stillbirths) or none. Age at menarche was categorized as < 14, 14–16, and >17 years. Medical history included hypertension, diabetes, medication use for dyslipidemia or hormone therapy. Quantification of mammographic breast density measurements All MD measurements were obtained at the time of participants’ routine health examination visits, not according to a predetermined schedule corresponding to specific MT stages. Mammographic data, including Breast Imaging Reporting and Data System’s (BI-RADS) final assessment categories and MD, were obtained from the original radiology reports of routine breast cancer screening. Participants underwent a standard four-view digital mammography examination, capturing bilateral craniocaudal (CC) and mediolateral oblique (MLO) views, using a full-field digital mammography (FFDM) system (Sonograph 2000D/DMR/DS, GE Healthcare, Chicago, IL, USA), at the Suwon and Seoul Total Healthcare Centers. Breast density was quantified using Laboratory for Individualized Breast Radiodensity Assessment (LIBRA), an open-source, fully automated software that analyzes both raw and processed FFDM images to obtain area-based measurements of MD [ 20 ]. Laboratory for Individualized Breast Radiodensity Assessment (LIBRA) estimates the total absolute dense area, while the percentage of breast density is calculated by normalizing the absolute dense area against the total breast area. The density assessments performed using LIBRA showed a strong correlation with the widely used Volpara ® methods ( r = 0.89, P < 0.001) [ 21 ]. In this study, LIBRA was used to determine the percentage of breast density (absolute dense area divided by total breast area × 100) from images stored in Digital Imaging and Communications in Medicine (DICOM) format, with density measurements averaged across left and right mammographic views for bilateral CC views [ 22 ]. The CC views were selected because pectoral muscle removal is more reliable and standardized in CC projections compared to MLO views, reducing potential measurement artifacts [ 23 , 24 ]. While our primary analysis used bilateral averaged MD measurements, we also conducted single-side analysis as a sensitivity analysis to assess the robustness of our findings. This approach addresses potential concerns where unilateral breast pathology, asymmetric breast composition, or differential imaging quality between sides might influence bilateral density measurements [ 25 ]. Percent dense area was selected as the primary MD metric because it is the most widely used and clinically relevant measure in breast cancer risk assessment and epidemiological research. Unlike absolute dense area, percent dense area accounts for overall breast size, making it more comparable across women with different breast volumes and body sizes [ 26 ]. This standardization is particularly important when studying hormonal influences on MD across the MT, as breast composition and total breast volume change significantly during this period [ 27 ]. Serum hormone assays Blood samples were obtained from the antecubital vein after at least a 10-h fast on the same day as the health examination, including mammography. Three hormones were analyzed in this study, including E2 and FSH serum levels quantified using electrochemiluminescence immunoassay (ECLIA) on a Roche Cobas e801 analyzer; and AMH, measured using an Elecsys AMH Plus reagent on the Cobas e801 immunoassay analyzer (Roche Diagnostics, Tokyo, Japan). Defining the menopausal stages and menstrual cycle phase Menopausal stage at each MD measurement was classified retrospectively as pre-menopause, early transition, late transition, or post-menopause according to the STRAW + 10 criteria [ 28 ], based on menstrual history information from the structured self-administered questionnaire at each visit: (1) Pre-menopause — does not meet any of the diagnostic criteria for the subsequent stages; (2) Early menopausal transition — a difference of ≥ 7 days in menstrual cycle length observed on at least two occasions within a 1-year period; (3) Late menopausal transition — amenorrhea persisting for ≥ 60 days; and (4) Post-menopause — amenorrhea persisting for ≥ 12 months. At each visit, the date of last menstrual period, regularity of menstrual cycles, and usual cycle length (if cycles were regular) were also assessed. For hormone measurements obtained during pre-menopause and early transition stages, menstrual cycle phase at the time of blood draw was determined based on days since last menstrual period and estimated cycle length. Cycle phases were classified as: early follicular (≤ 7 days since menses onset), late follicular/peri-ovulatory (8–14 days since onset), mid-luteal (7–14 days before next expected menses), late luteal (< 7 days before next expected menses), or irregular cycles (when specific phase could not be determined due to cycle irregularity). Statistical analyses Baseline characteristics are presented using descriptive statistics, including the mean (standard deviation [SD]) and percentage, as appropriate. We defined confounding variables using the following criteria: Each variable is (1) causally associated with the outcome (MD and Hormones), (2) non-causally or causally associated with the exposure (MT), but (3) is not an intermediate variable in the causal pathway between exposure (MT) and the outcome (MD and Hormones). We examined correlations among the three hormones (E2, FSH, and AMH) using Pearson correlation analysis. Mutual adjustment for the three hormones was performed using their original values to evaluate interaction terms between each hormone and MT status. Among the 238 participants with data on MD and all three hormones, we extended the main model by including each of the three log-transformed hormones (E2, FSH, AMH) as explanatory variables to estimate their associations with MD. To investigate the MD changes along with hormonal changes by MT stage over time, a linear mixed model with a random intercept was applied using maximum likelihood estimation, which was considered a within-participant correlation for the study participants. Despite the uneven distribution of MD measurements across MT, we used linear mixed-effects models to appropriately analyze the longitudinal data, which can handle unbalanced repeated measures and varying follow-up intervals among participants [ 29 , 30 ]. When participants had multiple mammograms within the same menopausal stage, all measurements were included in analyses using linear mixed-effects models with random intercepts, which appropriately handle unequal numbers of observations and varying follow-up intervals among participants. A linear mixed model with a random intercept for identifiers was applied, adjusted for age, smoking status, age at menarche, parity, marital status, and education level. An interaction term between MT status and BMI categories was included. All covariates were treated as time-varying except for age at menarche. The normality of residuals from MD model was assessed by visual inspection of their distribution within each menopausal stage, although fixed effects in linear mixed-effects models are robust to violations of residual normality [ 31 ]. For anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) values, which had a lower detection limit of 0.001 ng/mL, a sensitivity analysis was performed using log-transformed AMH values to address potential distributional concerns. We used the mean value of the CC view MD from both the breasts. We compared changes in breast density across time-variant menopausal stages, stratified by BMI categories (underweight, normal, overweight, obese). For hormonal changes, BMI was dichotomized (non-obese: BMI < 25 kg/m², obese: BMI ≥ 25 kg/m²) for detailed subgroup analysis, owing to limited sample size. In additional analyses, changes in breast density were also evaluated across time-varying menopausal stages, stratified by body fat percentage categories: <25%, 25–29.9%, 30.0–34.9%, and ≥ 35%. The analysis was conducted using Stata version 17.0 (College Station, Texas, USA), with statistical significance set at 0.05.

[results] Results Table 1 shows baseline characteristics: for MD, mean age 41.0 (± 3.3) years and BMI 22.0 (± 2.9) kg/m²; for hormones, mean age 45.9 (± 2.4) years and BMI 22.7 (± 3.1) kg/m². The mean baseline age of 41 years reflects the age when participants’ first digital MD measurements became available (from 2009 onwards), while the 42–52 year age range represents their age at formal enrollment into the prospective study. The median right and left breast densities were 40.3% (interquartile range [IQR]: 23.5–57.7) and 39.1% (IQR: 21.3–57.0), respectively. The median baseline E2, FSH, and AMH levels were 118.0 pg/mL (IQR: 68.4–183.0); 7.9 mIU/mL (IQR: 4.9–13.7); and 0.17 ng/mL (IQR: 0.07–0.36), respectively. The distributions of MD across MT stages are presented in the supplementary figures (Additional file 2, Figure S1 -1, 1–2, 1–3, and 1–4). For MD and hormone analyses, participants were followed for a median of 7.0 years (IQR 4.1–9.1) and 6.1 years (IQR 5.8–6.9), respectively. The median interval between observations was 1.0 year (IQR 0.9–1.4) for MD and 2.0 years (IQR 1.2–2.8) for hormones. Among the 255 women in the hormonal analysis, 238 (93.3%) also had mammography data, enabling combined hormone–density analyses; 17 (6.7%) did not have available mammograms. The smaller hormonal sample reflects stringent eligibility criteria and the requirement for multiple biospecimens spanning the MT. Age-adjusted baseline comparisons showed differences in smoking status, education level, and age at menarche, while BMI was comparable between groups (Additional file 1, Table S1 ). Longitudinal hormonal changes across MT Table 2 presents the longitudinal changes in E2, FSH, and AMH levels across the MT. Age was negatively associated with E2 and AMH levels, but positively associated with FSH levels. Hormonal patterns varied by menopausal stage as follows: FSH increased and AMH and E2 decreased with advancing menopausal stage, without interaction terms, after adjusting for age and other confounders. E2 levels significantly decreased during the late transition (− 55.82 [95% CI: −77.37, − 34.28]) and post-menopause (− 94.83 [95% CI: −121.35, − 68.32]) stages, whereas a non-significant change was observed in the early transition (− 2.33 [95% CI: −30.00, 25.34]). FSH levels showed a non-significant increase during the early transition (1.74 [95% CI: −4.78, 8.26]), followed by significant increases during the late transition (27.39 [95% CI: 22.23, 32.56]) and post-menopause (38.55 [95% CI: 32.08, 45.02]) stages. AMH levels significantly declined from the early transition (− 0.11 [95% CI: −0.16, − 0.06]) and continued to decrease during the late transition (− 0.13 [95% CI: −0.17, − 0.09]) and post-menopause (− 0.07 [95% CI: −0.13, − 0.02]) stages. Changes in E2 and AMH levels across the MT significantly differed by BMI category (obese vs. non-obese), whereas FSH levels did not (Table 2 ; Fig. 2 - 1 – 3 ). Notably, during the early transition, a transient increase in E2 levels was observed only among women with BMI < 25.0 kg/m² (14.53 [95% CI: -15.68, 44.75]), whereas a significant decline was observed among women with BMI ≥ 25.0 kg/m² (-86.02 [95% CI: -151.05, -21.00]), with a significant interaction by obesity status ( P for interaction = 0.016). In later stages, E2 levels declined further in both groups, with greater reductions observed among obese women. Similarly, AMH levels declined drastically across all MT stages in women with BMI ≥ 25.0 kg/m² than those with BMI < 25.0 kg/m², with a significant interaction by obesity status ( P for interaction = 0.038). In our dataset, the lowest AMH value was 0.001 ng/mL, corresponding to the assay’s lower detection limit, with postmenopausal women exhibiting the expected near-zero values. The sensitivity analysis using log-transformed AMH values yielded results consistent with the main analysis (Additional file 3, Figure S2 ). Among the 1,020 hormone measurements from 255 women, the distribution of menstrual cycle phases at the time of blood draw was as follows: early follicular phase in 64 measurements (6.3%), late follicular/peri-ovulatory phase in 91 measurements (8.9%), mid-luteal phase in 118 measurements (11.6%), late luteal phase in 81 measurements (7.9%), postmenopausal status in 276 measurements (27.1%), and irregular menstrual cycles in 390 measurements (38.2%). The high proportion of irregular cycles reflected the natural increase in menstrual irregularity characteristic of the MT period. Additionally, in sensitivity analyses adjusting for menstrual cycle phase, the patterns of estradiol, follicle-stimulating hormone, and AMH changes across MT stages remained consistent with the main analysis. Specifically, the significant interaction between BMI status and estradiol levels across menopausal stages persisted ( P for interaction = 0.030) (Additional file 4, Figure S3 ). Longitudinal MD changes across MT All 4,737 women with at least once measurement of mammogram were included in the analysis. Among these, 4,378 (92.5%) had at least two MD measurements and the number of mammograms per person was median 6 (interquartile range 3–8, maximum 12) over the study period. All participants were premenopausal at baseline. By the end of follow-up, MD data were available for 1,483 (31.3%) in early transition, 1,039 (21.9%) in late transition, and 633 (13.4%) in post-menopause. Table 3 presents changes in MD across the MT. After adjusting for age and other confounders without interaction, MD showed a non-significant decrease during the early transition (− 0.28 [95% CI: −0.71, 0.16]) compared to the pre-menopausal stage, followed by significant decreases during the late transition (− 2.22 [95% CI: −2.75, − 1.69]) and post-menopause (− 3.62 [95% CI: −4.30, − 2.93]). Age was negatively associated with MD (− 0.88 [95% CI: −0.93, − 0.83]). The distributions of MD residuals across MT stages are presented in the supplementary figures (Additional file 5, Figure S4 -1, S4-2, S4-3, and 4–4). The residuals showed approximately normal distributions of MD across menopausal stages, satisfying the assumptions for linear mixed-effects models without requiring transformation (Additional file 5, Figure S4 -1, S4-2, S4-3, and 4–4). Figure 3 illustrates that changes in MD across the MT significantly differed by BMI category (underweight, normal weight, overweight, and obese) after adjusting for the same confounders. Notably, during the early transition, MD showed an increasing pattern (1.69 [95% CI: -0.08, 3.46]) in the underweight group, whereas only modest changes were observed in the normal weight (-0.79 [95% CI: -1.32, -0.25]), overweight (-0.03 [95% CI: -0.91, 0.86]), and obese (0.66 [95% CI: -0.27, 1.60]) groups (Table 3 ). As an additional analysis to assess potential residual confounding, we evaluated the interaction between continuous BMI and MT stages, with MD as the outcome. The interaction terms were statistically significant (overall P for interaction < 0.001), consistent with the results obtained when BMI was modeled as a categorical variable. Separate analyses of the right- and left-sided MD across the MT, including interaction terms for BMI categories, demonstrated patterns consistent with those observed when using the mean of both the breasts (Additional file 1, Table S2 ). During the early transition, single-sided MD showed an increasing pattern in the underweight group for both breasts (Additional file 6, Figure S5 -1, S5-2). In additional analyses, bilateral mean MD also showed a slight increase in the lowest body fat percentage group (< 25%) during the early transition (Additional file 7, Figure S6 ), aligning with patterns observed in BMI-based categories. Among participants, oral contraceptive use was low: 1.1% (50/4,737) in the MD analysis and 1.6% (4/255) in the hormonal analysis. In the sensitivity analysis, adjustment for oral contraceptive use did not affect the results (Additional file 8, Figure S7-1 and 7 -2). Correlations among hormones and their associations with MD E2 was negatively correlated with FSH ( r = − 0.50, p < 0.001) and positively correlated with AMH ( r = 0.22, p < 0.001), while FSH was negatively correlated with AMH ( r = − 0.33, p < 0.001) (Additional file 1, Table S3). After mutual adjustment, the interaction term between E2 and MT remained significant without appreciable change. The interaction term between AMH and MT attenuated from significant to marginally significant, whereas the interaction term between FSH and MT was not statistically significant irrespective of mutual adjustment (Additional file 9, Figure S8). We further performed sensitivity analysis among 238 participants with complete hormone and MD data. When added individually, log-transformed E2 showed a positive association with MD (1.08 [95% CI: 0.35, 1.82]), while log-transformed FSH showed an inverse association (–1.54 [95% CI: − 2.59, − 0.49]). However, when E2, FSH, and AMH were included simultaneously, variance inflation factor (VIF) values remained below 2, and none of the hormones were significantly associated with MD (Additional file 1, Table S4).

[discussion] Discussion We observed the following distinct hormonal patterns across MT stages: FSH levels increased and AMH levels decreased progressively, whereas E2 showed a transient increase during the early transition, followed by a marked decline. Notably, this transient increase in E2 was observed only among non-obese women, consistent to the SWAN findings. A corresponding increase in MD during the early transition was particularly evident in underweight women, potentially reflecting the temporal E2 elevation during this period. These patterns may represent a previously underrecognized feature of early MT and could help explain the incidence of breast cancer among Korean women, a relatively lean population. Our findings align with those of the SWAN, which showed a transient E2 increase in non-obese women during MT, suggesting BMI-hormonal interactions [ 9 ]. A previous review [ 32 ] and small population-based studies [ 33 , 34 ] have shown that perimenopause is marked by complex, extreme, hormonal fluctuations, particularly of estrogen. The transient increase in E2 levels during early MT possibly results from declining ovarian follicles, leading to reduced negative feedback and elevated FSH levels [ 33 ]. Elevated FSH levels may stimulate estrogen production in the subsequent cycle and likely initiates folliculogenesis prematurely [ 35 , 36 ]. Additionally, reduced hypothalamic-pituitary sensitivity to estrogen may cause E2 elevation before the FMP [ 37 ]. In underweight women, with lower ovarian reserve, this FSH-driven feedback response may be exaggerated. Limited follicle availability may prompt increased pituitary FSH output to recruit the remaining follicles, intensifying transient estrogen surges during early MT. This may ultimately shorten the length of the follicular phase and the overall cycle, thereby accelerating the approach to menopause [ 38 ]. In contrast, obese women show attenuated changes in E2 and FSH during MT. Obesity appears to suppress gonadotropin secretion, the FSH increase after FMP, and altering E2 patterns—showing lower pre-FMP but higher post-FMP E2 levels—resulting in lower overall FSH and E2 levels in overweight and obese women [ 39 , 40 ]. Unlike previous studies, we assessed AMH changes throughout the MT alongside E2 and FSH. AMH is a well-established marker of ovarian reserve and a predictor of time to menopause, with potentially greater accuracy than FSH [ 41 , 42 ]. Our findings show that AMH levels steadily decreased throughout MT without significant fluctuations, consistent with prior studies highlighting its stability [ 43 , 44 ]. We observed that obese women maintained higher mean AMH levels during MT compared to non-obese women, a finding that contrasts with some earlier studies [ 45 , 46 ]. However, a recent meta-analysis reported inconclusive results, attributed to high heterogeneity in AMH levels, small sample sizes, cross-sectional designs, and inadequate representation across the entire adiposity spectrum in previous studies [ 47 ]. Given our relatively lean premenopausal population, further studies are needed to clarify the relationship between obesity and AMH levels, considering obesity subtypes and fat mass measures. Research on longitudinal changes in MD to estimate breast cancer risk across detailed MT stages remains limited, particularly in large populations with regular screening data. A study based on the San Francisco Mammography Registry (2007–2013) using automated software (Volpara) to measure breast density, found that women with higher premenopausal density experienced more significant declines in dense breast volume during the transition [ 48 ]. Another study from the Melbourne Women’s Midlife Health Project reported decreased mean percentage of mammographically dense tissue throughout the MT [ 49 ]. A longitudinal analysis revealed that women with stable MD (≤ 10% annual change) had higher breast cancer risk than those with >10% annual decline [ 50 ]. Although these studies examined density changes over time, unlike our study, they did not specifically categorize distinct MT stages, or assessed interaction with BMI, or evaluate concurrent hormonal changes, limiting understanding of the underlying hormonal mechanisms. Interestingly, we observed similar pattern of changes in MD corresponding to the changes in E2 levels across MT, particularly in underweight women. The association between MD and cyclic variations in E2 levels has been studied in both premenopausal [ 51 ] and post-menopausal women [ 52 , 53 ]. In premenopausal women, higher levels of estrogen during the menstrual cycle have been associated with increased MD, highlighting a hormonal influence on breast tissue composition [ 51 ]. Similarly, a meta-analysis examining the association between premenopausal endogenous estrogen levels and breast cancer risk found a positive correlation, with elevated circulating E2 levels associated with a higher risk of breast cancer [ 4 ]. Given that estrogen, a key determinant of breast density in premenopausal women, shows a positive association with MD, transient MD elevation along with E2 elevation appears compatible. To our knowledge, this is the first study examining longitudinal MD changes in relation to reproductive hormone fluctuations across distinct MT stages. We analyzed changes in MD as a proxy for breast cancer risk across MT. MD is a well-established risk factor in both Western [ 24 , 26 , 54 ] and Asian populations, including Koreans [ 13 , 55 – 57 ], regardless of menopausal status. MD serves as an important predictor of future breast cancer risk [ 13 , 55 , 56 ]. Studies indicate that women with increasing or persistently high MD trajectories are at higher breast cancer risk [ 58 ]. Thus, MD may be a tissue-specific marker of the biological processes underlying the rate of breast tissue aging and, thereby, shape the breast cancer incidence–age curve [ 7 , 59 , 60 ]. However, further cohort studies are needed to investigate long-term MD trajectories in relation to obesity phenotypes—such as specific adiposity measures—and their interaction with hormonal changes. These investigations should also incorporate incident breast cancer data to validate and extend current findings. Single-side analysis serves as a sensitivity analysis to validate our main findings, given the high bilateral correlation ( r = 0.85–0.95) consistently reported in MD studies [ 61 ]. Single-side analysis ensures that our observed associations between hormonal changes and MD across the MT are not dependent on bilateral averaging methodology and remain valid even when only unilateral measurements are available or reliable. Analysis was conducted using a linear mixed-effects model adjusted for age and other covariates, including an interaction term between menopausal stage and BMI category At each health examination visit, oral contraceptive use was assessed using the structured self-administered questionnaire. During the observation period, among the 4,737 participants in the MD analysis, 50 (1.1%) reported oral contraceptive use, and among the 255 participants in the hormonal analysis, 4 (1.6%) reported use. The low oral contraceptive use rate in our cohort was expected and consistent with our study design. First, any hormone use including oral contraceptives was an exclusion criterion at enrollment. Second, our cohort focused on women aged 42–52 years during the MT, when oral contraceptive use typically declines. For context, a recent Korean population study reported oral contraceptive usage rates of 2–8% among women aged 20–59 years [ 62 , 63 ], with lower rates in older age groups, making our observed rates plausible for this demographic. BMI, body mass index This longitudinal study has several limitations. First, self-reported reproductive factors, including hormone replacement therapy use, age at menarche, parity, and marital status, are subject to potential measurement error. However, this error may be non-differential, occurring independently of outcome status and affecting all participants equally in direction and magnitude. Second, hormone levels were not measured for all cohort participants who underwent repeated MD assessments; instead, hormone data were collected from a sub-cohort of 255 participants, which may limit the comprehensiveness of our hormonal analysis. Owing to the small sample size of the sub-cohort, non-obese women could not be further categorized into an underweight group. Third, genetic data were unavailable, despite evidence suggesting that breast density and endogenous hormones, including E2, show high genetic predisposition [ 64 ]. Fourth, the relatively short follow-up period posed limitations for capturing the complete MT across all participants, as the entire MT process can span 8–10 years. Consequently, approximately 50.1% of participants remained in the premenopausal stage, while about 50% progressed through the MT, but only 13.4% (633 women) reached post-menopause by the end of follow-up. Among those who reached post-menopause, 105 had MD measurements at all four menopausal stages. This is partly due to variable and sometimes brief duration of certain transition stages (particularly late transition, which averages 1–3 years), combined with routine health examination intervals of 1–2 years, resulting in some participants progressing through stages between scheduled visits, and some participants not attending health examinations during specific transition periods. Despite uneven distribution of measurements across menopausal stages, we used linear mixed-effects models to handle unbalanced repeated measures and varying follow-up intervals among participants [ 29 , 30 ]. Lastly, different subsets were used for MD and hormonal analyses due to eligibility and data availability, which may limit direct comparability. Although most women in the hormonal subset al.so had mammography data, baseline differences (smoking status, education, age at menarche) may reflect selection bias. The small hormonal analysis sample size, dictated by stringent eligibility for serial biospecimens across the MT, may limit statistical power and generalizability. However, the present study’s strengths include a large sample size, automated quantitative breast density measurements, detailed menopausal staging, concurrent assessments of MD and endogenous hormones, and a longitudinal analytical approach to assess changes in density and hormone levels across MT. The transient increase in breast density and E2 levels—key breast cancer risk factors—may provide physiological insight into the elevated premenopausal breast cancer risk observed in Korean women. Further studies are needed to confirm these findings across obesity status, assess their relevance to breast cancer development, and validate the results in diverse populations.

[conclusion] Conclusions In Korean women undergoing MT, transient increases in MD paralleled E2 elevations, particularly in non-obese women. These findings align with the peak breast cancer incidence in Korean women in their 40s, preceding menopause, and the importance of considering hormonal and MD dynamics, as well as BMI, in breast cancer risk assessment and screening strategies for Asian women.

retrieved_papers.title: Hormone phase influences sympathetic responses to high levels of lower body negative pressure in young healthy women.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2016]
[Abstract] We tested the hypothesis that sympathetic responses to baroreceptor unloading may be affected by circulating sex hormones. During lower body negative pressure at -30, -60, and -80 mmHg, muscle sympathetic nerve activity (MSNA), heart rate, and blood pressure were recorded in women who were taking (n = 8) or not taking (n = 9) hormonal contraceptives. All women were tested twice, once during the low-hormone phase (i.e., the early follicular phase of the menstrual cycle and the placebo phase of hormonal contraceptive use), and again during the high-hormone phase (i.e., the midluteal phase of the menstrual cycle and active phase of contraceptive use). During baroreceptor unloading, the reductions in stroke volume and resultant increases in MSNA and total peripheral resistance were greater in high-hormone than low-hormone phases in both groups. When normalized to the fall in stroke volume, increases in MSNA were no longer different between hormone phases. While stroke volume and sympathetic responses were similar between women taking and not taking hormonal contraceptives, mean arterial pressure was maintained during baroreceptor unloading in women not taking hormonal contraceptives but not in women using hormonal contraceptives. These data suggest that differences in sympathetic activation between hormone phases, as elicited by lower body negative pressure, are the result of hormonally mediated changes in the hemodynamic consequences of negative pressure, rather than centrally driven alterations to sympathetic regulation.

retrieved_papers.title: Physical Activity and Sex Steroid Hormones in Women: A Prospective Study.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2025]
[Abstract] Female reproductive function is sensitive to energetic stress and reacts to negative energy balance with reproductive suppression. This sensitivity is important for understanding human reproduction from an evolutionary perspective and also for the prevention of chronic diseases, especially hormone-dependent cancers such as breast cancer. High estradiol and progesterone levels increase cancer risk, and regular exercise may lower these hormones and cumulative hormonal exposure. This study examined the effects of increased physical activity during consecutive menstrual cycles on estradiol, progesterone, and the frequency of ovulation in healthy women.
Data were collected from 135 healthy, urban women who participated in the project for three consecutive menstrual cycles. In cycle 1, participants maintained their habitual activity; in cycles 2 and 3, they were requested to engage in at least 180 min per week of moderate physical activity. Physical activity was measured with wristband accelerometers. Estradiol and progesterone were assessed from daily saliva samples in cycles 1 and 3. Ovulation was monitored using urinary luteinizing hormone tests.
Average daily step counts increased by ~14% from cycle 1 to cycle 2, with no further rise in cycle 3. Greater increases in daily steps from cycle 1 to cycle 3 were significantly associated with lower progesterone levels in cycle 3, after adjusting for baseline activity, baseline hormone levels, and body fat. Physical activity did not have a significant effect on estradiol. The occurrence of ovulation declined from 70% in cycle 1 to 39% in cycle 3, but it was not related to step counts or their changes.
This study confirmed that energy expenditure alone, even when not leading to negative energy balance, is sufficient to cause modest changes in ovarian function. Even moderate increases in physical activity can lower progesterone levels, reducing the chance of conception in a cycle and also the cumulative lifetime hormonal exposure. These findings highlight the importance of encouraging regular physical activity, particularly in premenopausal women, as a strategy to modulate hormone levels, maintain reproductive health, and potentially reduce the long-term risk of hormone-related cancers.

retrieved_papers.title: Managing Hot Flushes in Menopausal Women: A Review.
retrieved_papers.body:
[Year: 2018]
[Abstract] Hot flushes during menopause are distressing for women and result in poor quality of life. Purpose of the current review was to evaluate the available treatment modalities that should be utilised for the management of hot flushes. Menopause refers to last menses of women life and can be declared after amenorrhea of 12 months. Vasomotor symptoms including hot flushes and night sweats are common after menopause, affecting almost 50 - 85% women older than 45 years. The mean increment in core body and skin temperature is 0.5°C and 0.25 - 3°C during a hot flush attack. Low level of estrogen during menopause and its association in triggering episodes of hot flushes, is still under debate. The most accepted hypothesis is a narrowing of the thermoneutral zone (TNZ) triggered by estrogen fluctuations. Although, hormone replacement therapy (HRT) remains the standard treatment for the alleviation of such symptoms, incidence of life threatening side effects restrained medical professionals from its use. Complications associated with the use of HRT can be avoided by appropriate evaluation of patients before initiating therapy. Several guidelines have also recommended HRT (estrogen and progesterone) to be safe for up to a period of seven years. Both hormonal and non-hormonal treatments are used for the management of hot flushes. Since hot flushes are the least appreciated and neglected complication of menopause, current review provides detailed information on its background, pathophysiology and management, and emphasises the need of its treatment.

[Introduction] Introduction Menopause is the biological process in which a woman's monthly menstrual cycle permanently stops for at least 12 consecutive months [ 1 ]. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), premenopausal women are individuals who have experienced episodes of irregular bleeding within the last year [ 1 ]. Perimenopausal women have experienced either irregular menstruation in the past year or cessation of menstruation for more than three months but less than one year. The onset of menopause exhibits variability among different populations and is subject to the effect of both hereditary and environmental variables. The commonly accepted age range is between 45-55 years, and the occurrence of menopause prior to the age of 40 is referred to as premature menopause [ 1 ]. Menopause is a significant stage in a woman's life that is linked to a range of health issues, including as vasomotor, sexual, and psychological symptoms. These symptoms can have a detrimental effect on both the quality of life and productivity [ 2 ]. The most common menopausal symptoms among women are hot flashes (87.4%), night sweats (66.6%), and sleeplessness (60.1%) [ 3 ]. These symptoms can cause significant distress to many persons. Hormonal replacement therapy (HRT) entails the administration of hormones that are normally depleted during the menopausal phase. Hormone replacement therapy involves the administration of estrogen to alleviate symptoms associated with menopause. If the uterus is still present, progesterone is also added to provide protection for the uterine lining. Routine addition or recommendation of progesterone is not necessary if the uterus is not intact. Administering these medications can effectively diminish or eradicate a wide range of menopausal symptoms, thereby enhancing the overall quality of life [ 3 ]. A previous study in Malaysia and other Asian nations, reported misinterpretations or false beliefs of menopause among women [ 4 ]. In Asian culture, menopause is commonly regarded as a natural consequence of aging, associated with social status and wisdom within the community. Research conducted on women from Asia and Europe has revealed that younger women generally exhibit more favorable perception in comparison to older women who have undergone menopause [ 4 ]. Thai women view menopause as a routine, natural biological process that does not necessitate intervention, but certain Pakistani women regard it as a medical ailment that necessitates medical attention [ 4 ]. Healthcare professionals from all fields (except pediatrics) come across patients experiencing menopause. Nevertheless, their understanding and implementation of menopausal hormone therapy (MHT) still varies [ 5 ]. A study in Australia assessed the knowledge and attitudes of Australian health professionals, including general practitioners, gynecologists, and endocrinologists, on HRT. The study found that only 52% of the participants had a sufficient level of knowledge about non-hormonal therapy, while 72% had a good understanding of menopausal physiology or HRT. Moreover, there were no significant discrepancies in knowledge detected among various disciplines. However, 91% of health providers expressed their willingness to offer HRT to women who are going through menopausal symptoms. Members of menopause societies demonstrated a higher inclination to endorse HRT for prolonged durations. The main obstacle identified in prescription HRT was the concern expressed by clients regarding the possibility of acquiring breast cancer [ 5 ]. According to a survey conducted in 2019 among medical residents specializing in family medicine, internal medicine, and obstetrics and gynecology, only 6.8% of the participants expressed confidence in their ability to effectively handle the medical needs of women going through menopause [ 6 ]. A further study conducted in China involving healthcare professionals uncovered a deficiency in their comprehension of the core principles behind HRT, since nearly half of them were incapable of correctly identifying its contraindications. Enhancing clinicians' expertise and education in the management of menopause is essential for providing complete, evidence-based care. Moreover, the study revealed that receiving training on menopause care has a substantial impact on physicians' attitudes, behaviors, and confidence in identifying menopausal symptoms and offering suitable suggestions for symptom management [ 7 ]. While many physicians acknowledge the necessity of education on menopausal therapy, there exist notable gaps in knowledge that require attention. Ensuring the provision of comprehensive, evidence-based menopausal care necessitates investing in continuous education and training for physicians. Hence, it is crucial to evaluate attitudes, practices, and barriers of physicians in Saudi Arabia, regarding menopausal HRT.

[introduction] Introduction Menopause is the biological process in which a woman's monthly menstrual cycle permanently stops for at least 12 consecutive months [ 1 ]. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), premenopausal women are individuals who have experienced episodes of irregular bleeding within the last year [ 1 ]. Perimenopausal women have experienced either irregular menstruation in the past year or cessation of menstruation for more than three months but less than one year. The onset of menopause exhibits variability among different populations and is subject to the effect of both hereditary and environmental variables. The study found that only 52% of the participants had a sufficient level of knowledge about non-hormonal therapy, while 72% had a good understanding of menopausal physiology or HRT. Moreover, there were no significant discrepancies in knowledge detected among various disciplines. However, 91% of health providers expressed their willingness to offer HRT to women who are going through menopausal symptoms. Members of menopause societies demonstrated a higher inclination to endorse HRT for prolonged durations. The main obstacle identified in prescription HRT was the concern expressed by clients regarding the possibility of acquiring breast cancer [ 5 ]. According to a survey conducted in 2019 among medical residents specializing in family medicine, internal medicine, and obstetrics and gynecology, only 6.8% of the participants expressed confidence in their ability to effectively handle the medical needs of women going through menopause [ 6 ]. A further study conducted in China involving healthcare professionals uncovered a deficiency in their comprehension of the core principles behind HRT, since nearly half of them were incapable of correctly identifying its contraindications. Enhancing clinicians' expertise and education in the management of menopause is essential for providing complete, evidence-based care. Moreover, the study revealed that receiving training on menopause care has a substantial impact on physicians' attitudes, behaviors, and confidence in identifying menopausal symptoms and offering suitable suggestions for symptom management [ 7 ]. While many physicians acknowledge the necessity of education on menopausal therapy, there exist notable gaps in knowledge that require attention. Ensuring the provision of comprehensive, evidence-based menopausal care necessitates investing in continuous education and training for physicians. Hence, it is crucial to evaluate attitudes, practices, and barriers of physicians in Saudi Arabia, regarding menopausal HRT.

[methods] Materials and methods Study design An online survey was conducted from June to September 2023 to investigate the attitudes, practices, and barriers of physicians in Saudi Arabia, regarding menopausal HRT. Study population The study population consisted of practicing physicians in Saudi Arabia specializing in gynecology, endocrinology, family medicine, internal medicine, and general practice at various levels, including consultants, senior registrars, and residents. There were no restrictions regarding the gender or demographic features of the study participants. Physicians of various specialties or those who are not currently practicing medicine in Saudi Arabia were removed. Sampling technique This study employed a convenient sampling approach. The survey link was distributed to the intended research participants in Saudi Arabia using several social media platforms (Facebook, Twitter, Snapchat, WhatsApp, and Instagram) utilizing a Google Form hyperlink. In addition, a cover letter was enclosed alongside a consent form. Participation was voluntary, granting individuals the freedom to withdraw as they saw fit. The comments were given anonymously, with no tracking of email addresses or any identifiable information. Questionnaire tool This study utilized a pre-existing questionnaire that was created by Yeganeh et al. [ 5 ]. The study questionnaire consisted of five components. The initial section was designed to collect data on the physician's demographic features, including: age category, gender, nationality, specialty, level of expertise, years of experience, practice region, practice location, and the weekly number of perimenopausal or postmenopausal female patients they attend to. The second section examined the physicians' attitude toward hormone replacement therapy. The third section analyzed the practices of physicians regarding HRT. This included their ability to stay updated with current HRT recommendations, their preferred method of obtaining up-to-date information about HRT, their preferred duration of systemic HRT for women with premature menopause, their preferred duration of combined HRT for symptomatic women over 50 years old, their preferred duration of estrogen-only HRT for symptomatic women without a uterus over 50 years old, their preferred treatment for hot flashes, their preferred type of systemic HRT for women with premature menopause without contraindications, and their preferred type of systemic HRT for women over 50 years old without contraindications. The fourth section examined the likelihood of administering systemic hormone replacement HRT to a woman experiencing menopause, while the fifth section explored the challenges associated with HRT. Questionnaire piloting In the pilot phase, the questionnaire was sent randomly to different locations in Saudi Arabia among a sample of 30 participants to evaluate the face validity of the study tool. The degree to which the items measure or assess what it is intended to measure.
These participants did not make any contributions to the findings of the primary investigation. Ethical approval The ethical approval for this study was obtained from the ethical research committee of the Institutional Review Board (IRB) of Imam Muhammed Ibn Saud Islamic University (IMSIU), Riyadh, Saudi Arabia, wherein they reviewed and approved this project (HAPO-01-R-0011; Project No.499/2023). Informed consent was obtained from all participants. Data analysis The data were analyzed utilizing the Statistical Package for Social Science Software (SPSS), especially version 28 (IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, USA). Numerical values and percentages (%) were used to represent categorical variables.

[results] Results Participants’ demographic characteristics A total of 95 physicians were involved in this study. Around 41.1% (n=39) of them were aged 25-34 years. More than half of them (53.7; n=51) were males. The vast majority of the study participants (82.1%; n=78) were Saudis. Around one-third of the study participants (35.8%; n=34) were gynaecologists. Almost 47.4% (n=45) of the study participants were residents. Around 34.7% (n=33) of the study participants reported that they have an experience of less than five years. More than half of the study participants (65.3%; n=62) reported that they practice in the Central region. Around 30.5% (n=29) of the study participants reported that they work at military, security, or national guard hospitals. More than half of the study participants (55.8%; n=53) reported that the number of perimenopausal or postmenopausal women that they see as patients each week is less than five patients. For further details on the demographic and practice characteristics of the study participants refer to Table 1 . Table 1 Participants’ demographic and practice characteristics Variable Frequency Percentage Age group 25-34 years 39 41.1% 35-44 years 19 20.0% 45-54 years 16 16.8% 55-64 years 16 16.8% 65 years and older 5 5.3% Gender Males 51 53.7% Females 44 46.3% Nationality Non-Saudi 17 17.9% Saudi 78 82.1% Specialty Gynaecologist 34 35.8% Endocrinologist 14 14.7% Family medicine 17 17.9% Internal medicine 16 16.8% General practitioner 14 14.7% Level of experience Consultant 30 31.6% Fellow 4 4.2% Senior registrar 6 6.3% Registrar 10 10.5% Resident 45 47.4% Years of experience Less than 5 years 33 34.7% 5-10 years 30 31.6% 11-15 years 18 18.9% 16-20 years 6 6.3% More than 20 years 8 8.4% Region of Practice Central region 62 65.3% Eastern region 11 11.6% Western region 14 14.7% Northern region 3 3.2% Southern region 5 5.3% Place of practice Military, security, or national guard hospital 29 30.5% University hospital 23 24.2% MOH secondary hospital 14 14.7% Primary healthcare centre 13 13.7% Private hospital 10 10.5% Medical city/specialised hospital 6 6.3% The number of perimenopausal or postmenopausal women that I see as patients each week Less than five patients 53 55.8% 6-10 patients 30 31.6% 11-15 patients 8 8.4% 16-20 patients 2 2.1% More than 20 patients 2 2.1% Attitude towards hormone replacement therapy Table 2 below presents physicians’ attitudes towards hormone replacement therapy. A total of 60.0% of the study participants agreed that in general (agreed or strongly agreed), HRT should be offered to menopausal women who have menopausal symptoms. Besides, around 24.2% of them agreed that in general, HRT should be offered to menopausal women who do not have menopausal symptoms. Table 2 Attitude towards hormone replacement therapy HRT: Hormone replacement therapy Strongly agree Agree Neutral Disagree Strongly disagree In general, HRT should be offered to menopausal women who have menopausal symptoms. 21.1% 38.9% 37.9% 2.1% 0.0% In general, HRT should be offered to menopausal women who do not have menopausal symptoms. 4.2% 20.0% 28.4% 40.0% 7.4% Hormone replacement therapy practices profile Table 3 below presents hormone replacement therapy practices profile of the study participants. A total of 48.4% (n=46) of the study participants agreed that they find it easy to keep up with current HRT recommendations/guidelines/evidence. The most commonly preferred methods of obtaining up-to-date information about HRT were Ministry of Health publications and journal articles, contributing for 36.8% (n=35) and 24.2% (n=23), respectively. The most commonly preferred duration of systemic (i.e. non-vaginal) HRT for women with premature menopause was one to five years, accounting for 60.0% (n=57). The most commonly preferred duration of systemic (non-vaginal) combined HRT (oestrogen + progesterone) for symptomatic women aged over 50 years was one to five years, accounting for 55.8% (n=53). The most commonly preferred duration of systemic (non-vaginal) oestrogen-only HRT for symptomatic women without a uterus aged over 50 years was one to five years, accounting for 49.5% (n=47). The most commonly preferred treatment for hot flashes was lifestyle modifications (32.6%; n=31). The most commonly preferred type of systemic (i.e. non-vaginal) HRT for women older than 50 years without contraindications was oral hormone replacement therapy, accounting for 49.5% (n=47). Likelihood of prescribing systemic HRT to menopausal women The most commonly agreed upon condition for which physicians would prescribe systemic HRT to a menopausal woman were those who have family history of breast cancer, personal history of breast cancer, and patient has concern about breast cancer, accounting for 48.5% (n=46), 43.2% (n=41), and 40.0% (n=38), respectively, Table 4 .
